▸ 17.1 Functional Anatomy, Physiology, and Investigations 17
▸ 17.1.01 A A Gradient And Oxygenation Failure
[!important]
The A-a gradient (alveolar-arterial O₂ difference) is a key calculation in respiratory physiology. It distinguishes the mechanisms of hypoxaemia: normal A-a gradient with ↑PaCO₂ = hypoventilation; ↑A-a gradient = V/Q mismatch, shunt, or diffusion limitation.

Related: [[ABG Interpretation]], [[Oxygen Therapy and NIV]], [[Respiratory Failure]], [[Ventilation-perfusion mismatch and shunt|V/Q mismatch]], [[Diffusion limitation and dead space]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: Normal A-a gradient (5–15 mmHg, or 1–2 kPa; ↑with age). A-a gradient = PAO₂ − PaO₂. In hypoventilation (e.g. opioid, NM weakness) the A-a gradient is normal but PaCO₂ is ↑. In V/Q mismatch, shunt, or diffusion limitation, A-a gradient is ↑.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Calculate A-a gradient
  • Define normal A-a gradient and age correction
  • Apply to differential diagnosis of hypoxaemia
  • Distinguish hypoventilation from V/Q mismatch, shunt, diffusion
  • Use P/F ratio for oxygenation assessment
  • Apply to ARDS, pneumonia, PE, ILD

2. Definition

A-a gradient = alveolar O₂ tension (PAO₂) − arterial O₂ tension (PaO₂)

Calculation

PAO₂ = FiO₂ × (Patm − PH₂O) − PaCO₂ / R

  • FiO₂ = fraction of inspired O₂ (0.21 on room air)
  • Patm = atmospheric pressure (760 mmHg at sea level)
  • PH₂O = water vapour pressure (47 mmHg)
  • PaCO₂ = arterial CO₂ tension
  • R = respiratory quotient (0.8 at rest)

Simplified (sea level, room air)

PAO₂ ≈ 150 − PaCO₂/0.8

A-a gradient = (150 − PaCO₂/0.8) − PaO₂

Example

  • PaO₂ = 80 mmHg, PaCO₂ = 40 mmHg, FiO₂ 0.21
  • PAO₂ = 150 − 40/0.8 = 150 − 50 = 100 mmHg
  • A-a gradient = 100 − 80 = 20 mmHg (normal)

3. Normal Values

Normal A-a gradient

  • Young adult: 5–10 mmHg (~1–1.5 kPa)
  • Adult: 5–15 mmHg (~1–2 kPa)
  • Elderly: up to 20 mmHg (~3 kPa) acceptable
  • Formula (age correction): A-a gradient = (Age/4) + 4 mmHg

Hypoxaemia

  • PaO₂ <80 mmHg (10.7 kPa) on room air
  • Severe: PaO₂ <60 mmHg (8 kPa)
  • Critical: PaO₂ <45 mmHg (6 kPa)

4. Mechanisms of Hypoxaemia

MechanismPaO₂PaCO₂A-a gradientResponse to O₂
Hypoventilation↓↑NormalYes (improve)
V/Q mismatch↓Variable↑Yes (improve)
Shunt↓Normal/low↑↑No/poorly
Diffusion limitation↓Variable↑Yes (improve)
Low FiO₂ (altitude)↓↓ (hypervent)NormalYes
AnaemiaPaO₂ normal; ↓CaO₂—NormalNo (CaO₂ issue)

5. Clinical Application

Hypoxaemia with normal A-a gradient

  • Hypoventilation (most common cause)
  • Causes: opioid, sedative, NM weakness, chest wall restriction, OSA
  • ABG: ↓PaO₂ + ↑PaCO₂ + normal A-a gradient
  • Treatment: support ventilation (NIV, intubation)

Hypoxaemia with ↑A-a gradient

  • V/Q mismatch: asthma, COPD, mild pneumonia
  • Shunt: ARDS, lobar pneumonia, atelectasis, R→L cardiac shunt
  • Diffusion limitation: ILD, pulmonary oedema, PH
  • ABG: ↓PaO₂ + variable PaCO₂ + ↑A-a gradient

P/F Ratio (PaO₂/FiO₂)

  • ARDS (Berlin definition): P/F <300 with bilateral infiltrates
  • Mild: 200 < P/F ≤ 300
  • Moderate: 100 < P/F ≤ 200
  • Severe: P/F ≤ 100
  • Used for ARDS severity assessment

6. Diagnosis

Step 1: Confirm hypoxaemia

  • ABG: PaO₂ <80 mmHg

Step 2: Look at PaCO₂

  • ↑PaCO₂ = hypoventilation component
  • ↓PaCO₂ = hyperventilation compensating

Step 3: Calculate A-a gradient

  • Normal A-a + ↑PaCO₂ = pure hypoventilation
  • ↑A-a + ↑PaCO₂ = V/Q mismatch with hypoventilation (e.g. severe COPD)
  • ↑A-a + ↓PaCO₂ = V/Q mismatch or shunt (compensatory hypervent)

Step 4: Test response to O₂

  • Improvement with O₂ = V/Q mismatch or hypoventilation
  • No improvement = true shunt

7. Management

Treat underlying cause

  • Hypoventilation: reversal of opioid, support ventilation
  • V/Q mismatch: bronchodilators, antibiotics, diuretics
  • Shunt: PEEP, prone, treat cause
  • Diffusion: treat cause (steroids for ILD, diuretics for oedema)

Oxygen therapy

  • Target SpO₂ 94–98% (88–92% if hypercapnic risk)
  • 100% O₂ test: significant shunt = no improvement

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
A-a gradientPAO₂ − PaO₂
Normal A-a5–15 mmHg (1–2 kPa); ↑with age
Age formulaA-a = (Age/4) + 4
PAO₂ formulaFiO₂ × (Patm − PH₂O) − PaCO₂/R
HypoventilationNormal A-a + ↑PaCO₂
V/Q mismatch↑A-a + variable PaCO₂
Shunt↑↑A-a + refractory to O₂
Diffusion↑A-a + improves with O₂
P/F ratioUsed for ARDS severity (mild <300, severe <100)
High altitudeLow PaO₂ + ↓PaCO₂ + normal A-a
Causes of ↑A-aV/Q mismatch, shunt, diffusion limitation

9. MCQs (10)

  1. Normal A-a gradient in a young adult is:

A. 0–5 mmHg

B. 5–15 mmHg

C. 20–30 mmHg

D. 40–50 mmHg

E. >50 mmHg

Answer: B — 5–15 mmHg.

  1. Hypoxaemia with normal A-a gradient and ↑PaCO₂ suggests:

A. PE

B. Hypoventilation

C. Shunt

D. ILD

E. Altitude

Answer: B — Hypoventilation.

  1. A 70-year-old has PaO₂ 75, PaCO₂ 40, FiO₂ 0.21. A-a gradient:

A. 5 mmHg

B. 10 mmHg

C. ~35 mmHg (age-corrected normal)

D. 50 mmHg

E. 100 mmHg

Answer: C — Age-corrected: (70/4) + 4 = 21.5; actual = (150 − 50) − 75 = 25. Both normal.

  1. P/F ratio <100 with bilateral infiltrates defines:

A. Mild ARDS

B. Moderate ARDS

C. Severe ARDS

D. Heart failure

E. Pneumonia

Answer: C — Severe ARDS.

  1. Hypoxaemia with ↑A-a gradient that does NOT respond to 100% O₂ suggests:

A. V/Q mismatch

B. True shunt

C. Hypoventilation

D. Anaemia

E. Altitude

Answer: B — True shunt refractory to O₂.

  1. The formula for PAO₂ on room air (sea level) is:

A. 100

B. 150 − PaCO₂/0.8

C. 100 − PaCO₂

D. 100 × FiO₂

E. 200 − PaCO₂

Answer: B — Simplified alveolar gas equation.

  1. Anaemia causes hypoxaemia via:

A. ↓PaO₂

B. ↓CaO₂ (↓Hb) with normal PaO₂

C. Shunt

D. V/Q mismatch

E. ↑PaCO₂

Answer: B — ↓O₂ carrying capacity.

  1. ARDS Berlin definition includes:

A. P/F <300 with bilateral infiltrates + cardiac cause

B. P/F <300 with bilateral infiltrates + non-cardiac cause

C. P/F <200

D. ↑PaCO₂

E. Anaemia

Answer: B — P/F <300 + bilateral infiltrates + non-cardiac oedema.

  1. The most common cause of ↑A-a gradient is:

A. Anaemia

B. V/Q mismatch

C. Altitude

D. Anxiety

E. Obesity

Answer: B — V/Q mismatch most common.

  1. A 60-year-old with severe COPD has PaO₂ 50, PaCO₂ 55, A-a 35. Pattern:

A. Pure hypoventilation

B. V/Q mismatch with hypoventilation

C. Pure V/Q mismatch

D. Shunt

E. Anaemia

Answer: B — ↑A-a + ↑PaCO₂ = V/Q mismatch + hypoventilation.

10. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 30-year-old with opioid overdose: PaO₂ 70, PaCO₂ 60, A-a normal. Cause:

A. V/Q mismatch

B. Hypoventilation

C. Shunt

D. Anaemia

E. ILD

Answer: B — Normal A-a + ↑PaCO₂.

  1. P/F ratio of 150 with bilateral infiltrates and no cardiac cause. ARDS severity:

A. Mild

B. Moderate

C. Severe

D. No ARDS

E. Indeterminate

Answer: B — Moderate (100 < P/F ≤ 200).

  1. A patient with PE has PaO₂ 60, PaCO₂ 30, A-a 35. Pattern:

A. Hypoventilation

B. V/Q mismatch (dead space) with hyperventilation

C. Shunt

D. ILD

E. Anaemia

Answer: B — ↑A-a + ↓PaCO₂ = V/Q mismatch (PE).

  1. A 25-year-old at 3000 m altitude: PaO₂ 60, PaCO₂ 30, A-a normal. Cause:

A. V/Q mismatch

B. Shunt

C. Low FiO₂ (altitude)

D. Anaemia

E. ILD

Answer: C — Altitude: ↓FiO₂ + ↓PaCO₂ (hypervent) + normal A-a.

  1. Treatment of hypoxaemia due to V/Q mismatch:

A. No O₂

B. Supplemental O₂ + treat cause

C. Surgery

D. Transfusion

E. Steroids only

Answer: B — O₂ + cause.

  1. Most sensitive bedside sign of hypoxaemia:

A. Cyanosis

B. Tachypnoea + tachycardia

C. Confusion

D. Wheeze

E. None

Answer: B — Tachycardia/tachypnoea.

  1. The PAO₂ equation requires:

A. PaO₂

B. FiO₂, Patm, PH₂O, PaCO₂, R

C. pH only

D. Hb

E. Temp

Answer: B — Alveolar gas equation variables.

  1. Normal P/F ratio on room air:

A. >200

B. >400–500

C. <100

D. 200–300

E. 100–200

Answer: B — Normal P/F >400.

  1. A patient with severe asthma has PaO₂ 55, PaCO₂ 45 (normal range). Most concerning pattern:

A. Hypoxaemia with hypocapnia (normal)

B. Normal PaCO₂ in severe attack = tiring

C. Severe hypoxaemia with hypercapnia

D. Normal

E. Improving

Answer: B — Normal PaCO₂ = impending respiratory failure.

  1. Calculating A-a gradient: FiO₂ 0.21, PaO₂ 80, PaCO₂ 40:

A. 5

B. 20 mmHg (150 − 50 − 80 = 20)

C. 50

D. 80

E. 100

Answer: B — A-a = 20 mmHg.

11. Flashcards

  • Q: A-a gradient formula?

A: PAO₂ − PaO₂.

  • Q: Normal A-a gradient?

A: 5–15 mmHg (1–2 kPa).

  • Q: Simplified PAO₂ formula?

A: 150 − PaCO₂/0.8 (sea level, room air).

  • Q: Pattern in hypoventilation?

A: ↓PaO₂ + ↑PaCO₂ + normal A-a.

  • Q: Pattern in V/Q mismatch?

A: ↓PaO₂ + ↑A-a + variable PaCO₂.

  • Q: Pattern in shunt?

A: ↓PaO₂ + ↑↑A-a + refractory to O₂.

  • Q: P/F ratio for severe ARDS?

A: ≤100.

  • Q: ARDS Berlin definition?

A: P/F <300 + bilateral infiltrates + non-cardiac oedema, within 1 week.

  • Q: Anaemia effect on A-a gradient?

A: Normal A-a (↓CaO₂, but PaO₂ normal).

  • Q: Age correction for A-a?

A: A-a = (Age/4) + 4.

12. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B 2. B 3. C 4. C 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

SBAs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. C 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

13. Summary

A-a gradient (PAO₂ − PaO₂) is a key calculation in respiratory physiology. Normal: 5–15 mmHg. Hypoxaemia with normal A-a + ↑PaCO₂ = hypoventilation. Hypoxaemia with ↑A-a gradient = V/Q mismatch, shunt, or diffusion limitation. P/F ratio used for ARDS severity. Treat underlying cause + O₂.

▸ 17.1.02 Aerosol And Inhaler Device Basics
[!important]
Selection of the correct inhaler device and ensuring correct technique are crucial for asthma/COPD control. 70–80% of patients make at least one error when using inhalers. The choice of device depends on patient factors (age, dexterity, inspiratory flow, preferences).

Related: [[Asthma]], [[COPD]], [[Acute severe asthma]], [[Difficult-to-treat and severe asthma]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: Pressurised metered-dose inhaler (pMDI) + spacer is the gold standard for most patients. Dry powder inhalers (DPIs) require adequate inspiratory flow (>30 L/min). Soft mist inhalers (SMI) are good for patients with poor coordination. Always check technique at every visit.

1. Learning Objectives

  • List inhaler types and their characteristics
  • Select the right device for each patient
  • Teach correct inhaler technique
  • Recognise common errors
  • Apply to acute and chronic settings

2. Inhaler Types

Pressurised metered-dose inhaler (pMDI)

  • Pressurised canister + plastic mouthpiece
  • Requires coordination: press + slow deep breath
  • Always use with spacer (especially for acute, children, elderly)
  • Dose counter: newer devices have it
  • Advantage: portable, multi-dose
  • Disadvantage: coordination, propellant, oropharyngeal deposition (rinsing recommended)

Breath-actuated MDI (BA-MDI)

  • Auto-triggered by inspiratory effort
  • No coordination needed
  • E.g. Autohaler, Easibreathe
  • Useful for patients with poor hand-breath coordination

Dry powder inhalers (DPI)

  • Breath-actuated — patient inhales to disperse dose
  • Require inspiratory flow >30 L/min (some >60 L/min)
  • Easier coordination than pMDI
  • Not for acute severe asthma (insufficient flow)
  • Examples: Accuhaler/Diskus, Turbohaler, HandiHaler, Ellipta, Breezhaler
  • Disadvantage: moisture-sensitive, requires adequate flow

Soft mist inhalers (SMI)

  • Slow-moving aerosol (longer inhalation time)
  • Good for poor coordination
  • E.g. Respimat
  • Lower oropharyngeal deposition

Nebulisers

  • Jet nebuliser (compressed air) or ultrasonic
  • For acute severe asthma/COPD (when MDI + spacer insufficient)
  • O₂-driven (to deliver O₂ + bronchodilator)
  • 5–10 min per treatment
  • Types: bronchodilator, steroid (budesonide), antibiotic (colistin), mucolytic

Spacers

  • Add-on device for pMDI
  • Slows aerosol; reduces oropharyngeal deposition
  • Multiple breaths easier than single breath
  • Essential for: children, acute asthma, poor coordination
  • Use with tidal breathing (or single deep breath with breath-hold)

3. Device Selection

PatientBest device
Most patientspMDI + spacer
Acute severe asthmapMDI + spacer (8–10 puffs) or nebuliser
ChildrenpMDI + spacer (with mask if <3 years)
Elderly, poor coordinationBA-MDI, DPI (good inspiratory flow), or SMI
Cognitive/dexterity issuesDPI, BA-MDI, or nebuliser
High-dose ICSDPI (e.g. Diskus) or pMDI + spacer (rinse mouth after)
Severe airflow limitation (severe acute)Nebuliser

4. Inhaler Technique

pMDI (with spacer)

  1. Shake the inhaler
  2. Attach to spacer
  3. Exhale fully (away from inhaler)
  4. Place mouthpiece in mouth, seal lips
  5. Press canister once
  6. Breathe slowly and deeply (or 4–5 tidal breaths)
  7. Breath-hold 10 s if possible
  8. Repeat for additional doses
  9. Rinse mouth after steroids

DPI

  1. Load dose (mechanism varies)
  2. Exhale fully (away from device)
  3. Seal lips around mouthpiece
  4. Inhale quickly and deeply (critical — fast flow needed)
  5. Breath-hold 10 s
  6. Exhale slowly
  7. Rinse mouth after steroids

Nebuliser

  1. Add medication to chamber
  2. Connect to compressor/O₂ (6–8 L/min)
  3. Seal mouthpiece/mask
  4. Breathe tidally until nebuliser sputters (~5–10 min)
  5. Tap chamber periodically to maximise output

5. Common Errors

ErrorSolution
Pressing pMDI before inhalingSpacer removes this issue
Inhaling too fast with DPIUse DPI only if flow adequate; otherwise pMDI + spacer
Not holding breath after pMDIBreath-hold 10 s if possible
Exhaling into DPIExhale away, then seal lips
Failing to rinse mouth after ICSRinse with water (prevent thrush, dysphonia)
Multiple puffs in quick successionWait 30 s between puffs; shake between
Dirty spacerWash weekly in soapy water, air-dry
Expired medicationCheck expiry; pMDI shows dose counter

6. Clinical Pearls

  • Switch device if patient cannot use it correctly
  • Recheck technique at every visit
  • One device per visit (don't introduce multiple changes)
  • Use the same device for all inhalers when possible (consistency)
  • Spacers should be replaced yearly
  • Nebulisers are not better than pMDI + spacer for stable patients
  • In acute asthma: 8–10 puffs of salbutamol via spacer = 5 mg neb

7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
pMDI + spacerGold standard for most patients
DPINeed inspiratory flow >30 L/min
SMIGood for poor coordination
NebuliserAcute severe; not for stable
SpacerEssential with pMDI; wash weekly
Breath-hold10 s after inhalation
Rinse mouthAfter ICS (prevent thrush)
Check techniqueEvery visit
Common errorPressing before inhalation (use spacer)

8. MCQs (10)

  1. The gold-standard inhaler device for most patients is:

A. DPI

B. pMDI + spacer

C. Nebuliser

D. SMI

E. Turbohaler

Answer: B — pMDI + spacer.

  1. DPIs require an inspiratory flow of at least:

A. 10 L/min

B. 30 L/min

C. 60 L/min

D. 100 L/min

E. 5 L/min

Answer: B — >30 L/min for most DPIs.

  1. In acute severe asthma, an MDI + spacer delivers how many puffs equivalent to a 5 mg nebuliser:

A. 2

B. 4

C. 8–10

D. 20

E. 50

Answer: C — 8–10 puffs = 5 mg neb.

  1. After using an inhaled corticosteroid, patients should:

A. Lie down

B. Rinse mouth with water

C. Eat

D. Take SABA

E. Drink caffeine

Answer: B — Prevent oral thrush.

  1. Breath-hold after inhaler use:

A. Not needed

B. 10 seconds if possible

C. 30 seconds

D. 1 minute

E. Until wheeze stops

Answer: B — 10 s breath-hold.

  1. Soft mist inhaler (SMI) example:

A. Ventolin

B. Respimat

C. Seretide Accuhaler

D. Pulmicort Turbohaler

E. Spiriva HandiHaler

Answer: B — Respimat is SMI.

  1. Spacers should be cleaned:

A. Daily

B. Weekly (in soapy water, air-dry)

C. Monthly

D. Yearly

E. Never

Answer: B — Weekly.

  1. Nebuliser first-line in:

A. Stable asthma

B. Acute severe asthma (or when MDI+spacer insufficient)

C. Mild COPD

D. Smoking cessation

E. Routine asthma

Answer: B — Acute severe.

  1. Common DPI error:

A. Pressing too hard

B. Inhaling too slowly (need fast deep breath)

C. Holding breath

D. Rinsing mouth

E. Exhaling

Answer: B — Need fast flow.

  1. Spacer role:

A. Increase dose

B. Reduce oropharyngeal deposition + improve coordination

C. Replace nebuliser

D. Increase flow

E. None

Answer: B — Reduces deposition + coordination.

9. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A patient with asthma cannot coordinate pMDI. Best device:

A. Higher dose pMDI

B. DPI, BA-MDI, or SMI

C. Tablet

D. Injection

E. Syrup

Answer: B — Alternative device.

  1. A child <3 years needs inhaled asthma medication. Best:

A. DPI

B. pMDI + spacer with face mask

C. MDI alone

D. Tablet

E. Syrup

Answer: B — pMDI + spacer + face mask.

  1. A patient on high-dose budesonide DPI develops oral thrush. Action:

A. Stop ICS

B. Rinse mouth after use + check technique

C. Increase dose

D. Switch to tablet

E. Surgery

Answer: B — Rinse + technique.

  1. Spacer vs nebuliser in stable asthma:

A. Nebuliser better

B. Spacer as effective + cheaper + faster

C. Equal

D. Spacer worse

E. Nebuliser only

Answer: B — Spacer as effective, cheaper, faster.

  1. A patient on DPI cannot generate adequate flow. Switch to:

A. Higher dose DPI

B. pMDI + spacer (or nebuliser)

C. Continuation

D. Lower dose

E. Surgery

Answer: B — Switch to pMDI + spacer.

  1. A patient on multiple inhalers uses different devices. Action:

A. Continue

B. Consolidate to same device type when possible

C. Add more

D. Stop

E. None

Answer: B — Consolidate.

  1. Nebuliser driving gas in acute severe asthma:

A. Air

B. Oxygen (6–8 L/min)

C. Helium

D. N₂O

E. CO₂

Answer: B — O₂-driven.

  1. pMDI propellant is:

A. CFC

B. HFA (ozone-friendly)

C. Helium

D. CO₂

E. N₂O

Answer: B — Modern pMDI use HFA.

  1. A patient uses inhaler but feels no benefit. First action:

A. Increase dose

B. Check inhaler technique

C. Stop

D. Add another

E. None

Answer: B — Technique first.

  1. The most common cause of poor asthma control is:

A. Wrong diagnosis

B. Poor inhaler technique + non-adherence

C. Steroid resistance

D. Severity

E. None

Answer: B — Technique + adherence.

10. Flashcards

  • Q: Gold-standard inhaler for most patients?

A: pMDI + spacer.

  • Q: Inspiratory flow for DPI?

A: >30 L/min.

  • Q: Acute severe: pMDI+spacer vs nebuliser?

A: Equivalent (8–10 puffs = 5 mg neb).

  • Q: After ICS, action?

A: Rinse mouth with water.

  • Q: Breath-hold after pMDI?

A: 10 s.

  • Q: SMI example?

A: Respimat.

  • Q: Spacer cleaning?

A: Weekly (soapy water, air-dry).

  • Q: Nebuliser driving gas in asthma?

A: Oxygen (6–8 L/min).

  • Q: Common DPI error?

A: Slow inhalation (need fast, deep).

  • Q: Most common cause of poor asthma control?

A: Poor technique + non-adherence.

11. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B 2. B 3. C 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

SBAs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

12. Summary

Inhaler devices: pMDI (with spacer = gold standard), DPI (need flow >30 L/min), BA-MDI, SMI, nebuliser (acute). Spacer reduces oropharyngeal deposition + aids coordination. Always check technique at every visit. Rinse mouth after ICS. Breath-hold 10 s.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 17.1.04 Ct Chest Pattern Approach
[!important]
HRCT (high-resolution CT) of the chest is the cornerstone of ILD diagnosis. Recognising the major patterns (UIP, NSIP, ground-glass, consolidation, mosaic, cysts, nodules) narrows the differential and guides further workup, often avoiding the need for lung biopsy.

Related: [[Interstitial Lung Disease]], [[Chest X-Ray Approach]], [[Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis|Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis]], [[Sarcoidosis]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: UIP pattern (subpleural, basal reticulation + honeycombing + traction bronchiectasis, minimal GGO) in an old male smoker = IPF. NSIP (bilateral, basal, homogeneous GGO with fine reticulation) in a young woman = CTD-ILD (esp. SSc). MDD (multidisciplinary discussion) is the gold standard for ILD diagnosis.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Identify the 7 major HRCT patterns
  • Recognise the typical disease associations
  • Apply systematic HRCT interpretation
  • Distinguish UIP, NSIP, ground-glass, consolidation, mosaic, cysts, nodules
  • Apply to ILD differential diagnosis

2. HRCT Technique

  • High-resolution (1–1.5 mm slices)
  • Supine + prone (to differentiate dependent atelectasis from true disease)
  • Inspiration + expiration (air-trapping in small airway disease)
  • Without contrast (unless vasculature needed)

3. The 7 Major HRCT Patterns

1. UIP (Usual Interstitial Pneumonia)

FeatureDetail
DistributionSubpleural, basal
ReticulationCoarse
HoneycombingPresent (cysts in clusters)
Traction bronchiectasisPresent
GGOMinimal
DistributionBilateral, often asymmetric

Causes: IPF, CTD-UIP (RA, SSc), asbestosis, chronic HP, drug (amiodarone, nitrofurantoin)

2. NSIP (Non-Specific Interstitial Pneumonia)

FeatureDetail
DistributionBilateral, basal, subpleural sparing possible
GGOPredominant (homogeneous)
ReticulationFine
HoneycombingMinimal or absent
DistributionSymmetric

Causes: CTD-ILD (esp. SSc, PM/DM, Sjögren), drug, HP, idiopathic

3. Ground-Glass Opacity (GGO)

  • Hazy ↑attenuation, vessels visible
  • Causes: HP, NSIP, DIP, PCP, alveolar proteinosis, pulmonary oedema, drug, infection

4. Consolidation

  • Dense ↑attenuation, vessels obscured
  • Causes: COP, eosinophilic pneumonia, lymphoma, adenocarcinoma, lipoid pneumonia, infection

5. Mosaic Attenuation

  • Patchy areas of ↓ and ↑ density
  • Air-trapping on expiratory = small airway disease
  • Causes: HP, sarcoidosis, constrictive bronchiolitis, asthma

6. Cysts

  • Thin-walled air spaces
  • Causes: LAM, LCH, LIP, Birt-Hogg-Dubé, honeycomb (UIP)

7. Nodules

  • Centrilobular: HP, sarcoidosis, respiratory bronchiolitis, infection
  • Perilymphatic: sarcoidosis, silicosis, lymphangitic carcinomatosis
  • Random: miliary TB, metastases, fungal, sarcoidosis

4. HRCT Pattern Recognition Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
HRCT Chest
↓
Ground-Glass vs Reticular
↓
Honeycombing?
NSIP, HP, DIP, etc.
COP, eosinophilic, infection
LAM, LCH, LIP, BHD
Small airway disease
Distribution → cause
↓
UIP pattern
NSIP or fibrotic NSIP
CTD-ILD serology + MDD
Biopsy: COP vs eos vs malig
Family hx, smoking, dermatologic
HP, sarcoidosis, bronchiolitis
Perilymphatic → sarcoid/silicosis

5. Disease-Specific HRCT Findings

DiseaseHRCT pattern
IPFUIP (subpleural, basal, honeycombing)
NSIPBilateral basal GGO + fine reticulation
SarcoidosisBilateral hilar nodes, perilymphatic nodules, upper-zone fibrosis
HPCentrilobular nodules + GGO + mosaic (chronic → fibrosis)
DIPDiffuse GGO + small cysts (smokers)
RB-ILDCentrilobular nodules + GGO (smokers)
LCHCysts + nodules, upper zones (smokers)
LAMDiffuse thin-walled cysts, women of childbearing age
LIPCysts + nodules, often with Sjögren/HIV
COPSubpleural/perilobular consolidation, migratory
Eosinophilic pneumoniaPeripheral "photographic negative of pulmonary oedema"
AsbestosisSubpleural lines, pleural plaques, UIP-like
Silicosis/coalUpper-zone nodules ± progressive massive fibrosis
Lymphangitic carcinomatosisPerilymphatic thickening/nodules (interlobular septa)
PCPDiffuse GGO in HIV
Alveolar proteinosis"Crazy paving" (GGO + interlobular septal thickening)
Lipoid pneumoniaConsolidation with fat density

6. Diagnosis

Systematic HRCT interpretation

  1. Distribution: upper vs lower; central vs peripheral; diffuse vs focal
  2. Pattern: reticular, nodular, GGO, consolidation, cystic, mosaic
  3. Specific signs: honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, tree-in-bud, cavitation
  4. Associated: lymphadenopathy, pleural effusion, pleural plaques
  5. Clinical correlation: age, smoking, exposure, symptoms

MDD (multidisciplinary discussion)

  • Pulmonologist + radiologist + pathologist (when biopsy)
  • Combines clinical + imaging + (pathology)
  • Gold standard for ILD diagnosis

7. Management

Based on pattern

  • UIP in appropriate clinical context → IPF diagnosis (no biopsy needed)
  • Other patterns → CTD screen, biopsy as needed
  • Treat underlying cause

HRCT in follow-up

  • Monitor disease progression
  • Detect complications (lung cancer, infection)
  • Assess treatment response

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
UIPSubpleural, basal, reticular, honeycombing → IPF, CTD, asbestosis
NSIPBasal, homogeneous GGO, fine reticulation → CTD-ILD
GGOHP, NSIP, DIP, PCP, PAP
ConsolidationCOP, eosinophilic, infection, malignancy
MosaicHP, sarcoidosis, bronchiolitis (air-trapping)
CystsLAM, LCH, LIP, BHD
NodulesDistribution → cause
MDDGold standard for ILD diagnosis
HRCTCornerstone of ILD workup

9. MCQs (10)

  1. UIP pattern is most characteristic of:

A. Sarcoidosis

B. IPF

C. LAM

D. Hypersensitivity pneumonitis

E. LCH

Answer: B — UIP = IPF (in appropriate context).

  1. NSIP pattern is most commonly associated with:

A. IPF

B. Sarcoidosis

C. CTD-ILD (esp. SSc)

D. LAM

E. Asbestosis

Answer: C — NSIP = CTD-ILD.

  1. Crazy paving pattern on HRCT is seen in:

A. IPF

B. Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis

C. Sarcoidosis

D. LAM

E. Asbestosis

Answer: B — PAP = crazy paving.

  1. Centrilobular nodules in a bird fancier with chronic exposure suggest:

A. IPF

B. Hypersensitivity pneumonitis

C. Sarcoidosis

D. LAM

E. Sarcoidosis

Answer: B — HP = centrilobular nodules.

  1. Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy + perilymphatic nodules suggests:

A. IPF

B. Sarcoidosis

C. LAM

D. Asbestosis

E. NSIP

Answer: B — Sarcoidosis.

  1. Diffuse thin-walled cysts in a young woman suggest:

A. LCH

B. LAM

C. IPF

D. Sarcoidosis

E. LIP

Answer: B — LAM (lymphangioleiomyomatosis).

  1. Upper-lobe cystic disease in a smoker suggests:

A. LAM

B. LCH (Langerhans cell histiocytosis)

C. IPF

D. Sarcoidosis

E. NSIP

Answer: B — LCH.

  1. Photographic negative of pulmonary oedema on HRCT:

A. UIP

B. Chronic eosinophilic pneumonia

C. COP

D. IPF

E. DIP

Answer: B — CEP.

  1. Perilymphatic nodules (along bronchovascular bundles, fissures, subpleural):

A. HP

B. Sarcoidosis

C. LAM

D. DIP

E. LCH

Answer: B — Sarcoidosis (perilymphatic distribution).

  1. The HRCT pattern of "tree-in-bud" suggests:

A. UIP

B. Endobronchial spread of infection (e.g. TB, atypical)

C. Sarcoidosis

D. LAM

E. LIP

Answer: B — Tree-in-bud = bronchiolitis/endobronchial infection.

10. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 70-year-old male smoker has HRCT: subpleural, basal reticulation, honeycombing, minimal GGO. Most likely:

A. NSIP

B. IPF (UIP pattern)

C. Sarcoidosis

D. HP

E. LAM

Answer: B — UIP in old male smoker = IPF.

  1. The most sensitive HRCT finding for ILD:

A. Honeycombing

B. GGO (earliest finding)

C. Consolidation

D. Cysts

E. Nodules

Answer: B — GGO earliest.

  1. A 50-year-old woman with SSc has HRCT: bilateral basal homogeneous GGO with fine reticulation. Pattern:

A. UIP

B. NSIP

C. COP

D. DIP

E. LCH

Answer: B — NSIP.

  1. HRCT in chronic HP shows:

A. UIP only

B. Centrilobular nodules + GGO + mosaic + air-trapping (can mimic UIP if chronic)

C. Cavitation

D. Pleural effusion

E. Miliary

Answer: B — HP pattern.

  1. Air-trapping on expiratory HRCT is characteristic of:

A. UIP

B. Small airway disease (HP, bronchiolitis)

C. Consolidation

D. Cavitation

E. Mass

Answer: B — Air-trapping = small airway.

  1. Honeycombing in subpleural, basal distribution is pathognomonic of:

A. NSIP

B. UIP (IPF)

C. COP

D. DIP

E. NSIP

Answer: B — UIP.

  1. A patient has HRCT showing GGO with superimposed interlobular septal thickening ("crazy paving"). Most likely:

A. UIP

B. Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis (PAP)

C. Sarcoidosis

D. LAM

E. Asbestosis

Answer: B — PAP.

  1. HRCT in LAM shows:

A. UIP

B. Diffuse thin-walled cysts in women

C. Consolidation

D. Pleural plaques

E. Mass

Answer: B — LAM.

  1. The most common HRCT pattern in asymptomatic drug-induced ILD:

A. Honeycombing

B. GGO (acute) or NSIP (chronic)

C. Cavitation

D. Mass

E. None

Answer: B — GGO/NSIP.

  1. MDD is essential for ILD diagnosis because:

A. CT alone is sufficient

A. Single-modality diagnosis is often insufficient; MDD integrates clinical + imaging + pathology

B. Lung biopsy is contraindicated

C. Always needed

D. None

Answer: A — MDD integrates all data.

11. Flashcards

  • Q: UIP pattern features?

A: Subpleural, basal, reticulation, honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, minimal GGO.

  • Q: UIP most common cause?

A: IPF (idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis).

  • Q: NSIP pattern features?

A: Bilateral, basal, homogeneous GGO, fine reticulation.

  • Q: NSIP most common cause?

A: CTD-ILD (esp. SSc).

  • Q: Crazy paving?

A: GGO + interlobular septal thickening → PAP.

  • Q: Centrilobular nodules + mosaic?

A: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis.

  • Q: Bilateral hilar nodes + perilymphatic nodules?

A: Sarcoidosis.

  • Q: Diffuse thin-walled cysts in young woman?

A: LAM.

  • Q: Upper-zone cysts in smoker?

A: LCH.

  • Q: Photographic negative of pulmonary oedema?

A: Chronic eosinophilic pneumonia.

12. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B 2. C 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

SBAs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. A

13. Summary

HRCT chest is the cornerstone of ILD diagnosis. 7 major patterns: UIP (IPF), NSIP (CTD-ILD), GGO (HP, NSIP, DIP), consolidation (COP, eos, infection), mosaic (HP, small airway), cysts (LAM, LCH), nodules (distribution-dependent). MDD (multidisciplinary discussion) is the gold standard for ILD diagnosis.

  • Chapter Map: [[../Davidson Chapter 17 - Respiratory Medicine Hierarchy|Respiratory Medicine Hierarchy]]

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 17.1.05 Diffusion Limitation And Dead Space
[!important]
Diffusion limitation and dead space ventilation are key concepts in respiratory physiology. Diffusion limitation impairs O₂ transfer across the alveolar-capillary membrane; dead space is ventilation that doesn't participate in gas exchange. Both contribute to hypoxaemia in distinct clinical scenarios.

Related: [[ABG Interpretation]], [[Spirometry Interpretation]], [[Ventilation-perfusion mismatch and shunt|V/Q mismatch]], [[Respiratory Failure]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: DLCO is the most sensitive test for diffusion limitation. Causes include ILD, pulmonary oedema, and pulmonary vascular disease. Dead space (↑V/Q) is increased in PE and severe COPD.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define diffusion limitation and Fick's law of diffusion
  • List causes of diffusion limitation
  • Interpret DLCO values
  • Define dead space (anatomical, alveolar, physiological)
  • Apply to clinical scenarios
  • Distinguish diffusion limitation from V/Q mismatch

2. Definition

Fick's law of diffusion

V_gas = (A × D × ΔP) / T

  • V_gas = volume of gas transferred per unit time
  • A = surface area for gas exchange
  • D = diffusion constant (∝ 1/√MW; CO₂ diffuses 20× faster than O₂ due to higher solubility)
  • ΔP = partial pressure gradient
  • T = thickness of membrane

Lung diffusing capacity (DL)

  • DLCO = diffusing capacity for carbon monoxide (most clinically used)
  • DLCO = V_CO / (P_A_CO − P_cap_CO) ≈ V_CO / P_A_CO (capillary P_CO ≈ 0)
  • DL = Dm × Vc (Dm = membrane diffusion; Vc = capillary blood volume)

3. Pathophysiology

Diffusion limitation mechanisms

  1. ↓Surface area (A): emphysema, pneumonectomy, lobectomy
  2. ↓Diffusion constant (D): lower solubility
  3. ↓Partial pressure gradient (ΔP): low FiO₂, PE
  4. ↑Membrane thickness (T): pulmonary fibrosis, oedema, inflammation

Clinical causes of diffusion limitation

CategoryExamples
ILDIPF, sarcoidosis, HP, pneumoconioses, CTD-ILD
Pulmonary oedemaCardiogenic, ARDS, fluid overload
Emphysema↓alveolar surface area
Pulmonary vascular diseasePE (↓capillary blood volume), pulmonary hypertension
DrugsAmiodarone, bleomycin, MTX, nitrofurantoin
Cardiac shuntR→L shunt (anatomical)

Gas-specific differences

  • CO₂ diffuses 20× faster than O₂ (more soluble) → rarely diffusion-limited
  • O₂ diffusion-limited at exercise, high altitude, with severe disease
  • N₂O is blood-soluble but unlimited → used for membrane diffusion (Dm)
  • CO binds Hb avidly → capillary P_CO = 0 → used for DLCO

4. Dead space

Types of dead space

TypeDefinitionExample
AnatomicalConducting airways (no gas exchange)Trachea, bronchi (~150 mL)
AlveolarVentilated but not perfused alveoliPE, severe emphysema
PhysiologicalAnatomical + alveolar (VD)Sum of both
ApparatusMechanical ventilation tubingVentilator circuit

Bohr equation

VD/VT = (PaCO₂ − PECO₂) / PaCO₂

  • Normal: 0.2–0.35 (20–35% of tidal volume is dead space)
  • Increased in: PE, COPD, low cardiac output, mechanical ventilation

Causes of increased dead space

  • PE (most common pathological cause) — ventilated, no perfusion
  • Severe COPD (alveolar destruction)
  • Pulmonary hypertension
  • Low cardiac output states
  • Mechanical ventilation (overdistension, PEEP)
  • Mainstem bronchus intubation (single lung ventilation)

5. Diagnosis

DLCO measurement

  • Single-breath DLCO (most common)
  • Patient inhales trace CO + helium, breath-hold 10 s, exhale
  • DLCO = mL CO/min/mmHg
  • Normal: 75–120% predicted

DLCO interpretation

DLCOSignificance
↑ IncreasedAsthma, polycythaemia, L→R shunt, alveolar haemorrhage (early)
NormalHealthy lungs, normal alveolar membrane
↓ MildMild ILD, mild COPD, mild PH
↓ ModerateModerate ILD, moderate COPD, PH
↓ SevereSevere ILD, severe emphysema, pulmonary vasculitis

DLCO patterns

DiseaseDLCOPattern
AsthmaNormal or ↑Obstructive, normal DLCO
COPD (chronic bronchitis)↓ Mild-moderateObstructive, ↓DLCO
Emphysema↓↓ SevereObstructive, ↓DLCO (most reduced)
ILD↓↓ SevereRestrictive, ↓DLCO (early sign)
Pulmonary vascular disease↓↓ SevereNormal volumes, ↓DLCO
Pneumonia (acute)VariableMixed

ABG in dead space (e.g. PE)

  • ↓PaO₂ (↑A-a gradient)
  • ↓PaCO₂ (hyperventilation, unless massive PE)

6. Management

Diffusion limitation

  • Treat underlying disease (steroids for ILD, diuretics for oedema, antifibrotics for IPF)
  • Supplemental O₂ (high FiO₂ to overcome diffusion gradient)
  • Pulmonary rehabilitation for chronic disease

Dead space

  • Treat underlying cause (anticoagulation for PE, bronchodilators for COPD)
  • Reduce ventilator dead space (shorter tubing, low PEEP)
  • Treat pulmonary embolism (anticoagulation, thrombolysis if massive)

7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
Fick's lawV = A × D × ΔP / T
DLCOMost sensitive test for diffusion
↓DLCO causesILD, emphysema, pulmonary oedema, PH
↑DLCO causesAsthma, polycythaemia, alveolar haemorrhage (early)
Dead space typesAnatomical, alveolar, physiological
Bohr equationVD/VT = (PaCO₂ − PECO₂) / PaCO₂; normal 0.2–0.35
Most common cause of ↑dead spacePE
CO₂ diffusion20× faster than O₂ (rarely diffusion-limited)
DLCO in IPF↓↓ early; sensitive marker
DLCO in asthmaNormal or ↑
DLCO in emphysema↓↓ severe (most reduced)

8. Common Viva Questions

QuestionExpected answer
What is Fick's law?V = A × D × ΔP / T
What is the most sensitive test for diffusion limitation?DLCO
What is normal DLCO?75–120% predicted
Causes of ↓DLCO?ILD, emphysema, oedema, PH
Causes of ↑DLCO?Asthma, polycythaemia, L→R shunt, alveolar haemorrhage
Bohr equation?VD/VT = (PaCO₂ − PECO₂) / PaCO₂
Most common cause of ↑dead space?PE
Why does CO₂ diffuse faster than O₂?20× more soluble
Difference between DL and DLCO?DL = membrane + capillary; DLCO = CO transfer

9. Confusions & Mnemonics

Causes of ↓DLCO — "AIPE": Alveolar (emphysema), Interstitial (ILD), Pulmonary vascular, Edema (pulmonary oedema)

Causes of ↑DLCO — "PALA": Polycythaemia, Asthma, L→R shunt, Alveolar haemorrhage

Dead space causes — "PE LOUD": PE, Emphysema, Low cardiac output, Overdistension (vent), Upper airway (apparatus), Death zone (PE, severe)

10. Local Navigation

  • Parent Heading: [[../Functional Anatomy, Physiology, and Investigations|Functional Anatomy, Physiology, and Investigations]]
  • Parent Topic Group: [[../Functional Anatomy, Physiology, and Investigations/Airflow, ventilation, and gas exchange|Airflow, ventilation, and gas exchange]]
  • Chapter Map: [[../Davidson Chapter 17 - Respiratory Medicine Hierarchy|Respiratory Medicine Hierarchy]]
  • Chapter MOC: [[../Respiratory MOC|Respiratory MOC]]
  • Related: [[Ventilation-perfusion mismatch and shunt|V/Q mismatch]] · [[ABG Interpretation]] · [[Spirometry Interpretation]] · [[Respiratory Failure]]

11. MCQs (10)

  1. Fick's law of diffusion includes all of the following EXCEPT:

A. Surface area

B. Diffusion constant

C. Partial pressure gradient

D. Renal blood flow

E. Membrane thickness

Answer: D — V = A × D × ΔP / T.

  1. The most sensitive test for diffusion limitation is:

A. Spirometry

B. DLCO

C. ABG

D. CXR

E. CT

Answer: B — DLCO most sensitive.

  1. CO₂ diffuses faster than O₂ because it is:

A. Smaller

B. More soluble (20× faster)

C. Less dense

D. Heavier

E. Charged

Answer: B — 20× more soluble.

  1. ↓DLCO is most prominent in which disease:

A. Asthma

B. Emphysema

C. Pneumonia

D. Pleural effusion

E. Bronchitis

Answer: B — ↓alveolar surface area in emphysema.

  1. Normal physiological dead space (VD/VT):

A. 0.05–0.10

B. 0.20–0.35

C. 0.50–0.70

D. 0.80–0.90

E. >0.95

Answer: B — Normal 0.20–0.35.

  1. The most common cause of increased physiological dead space is:

A. Asthma

B. Pulmonary embolism

C. Pneumonia

D. Bronchitis

E. Pleural effusion

Answer: B — PE = ventilated, no perfusion.

  1. Bohr equation calculates:

A. FEV₁

B. Dead space to tidal volume ratio (VD/VT)

C. FRC

D. TLC

E. RV

Answer: B — VD/VT.

  1. ↑DLCO is seen in:

A. Emphysema

B. Asthma (or alveolar haemorrhage)

C. ILD

D. PH

E. Anaemia

Answer: B — ↑pulmonary capillary blood volume in asthma.

  1. The most common cause of ↓DLCO in a young person with normal spirometry is:

A. COPD

B. Pulmonary vascular disease (e.g. PAH)

C. Asthma

D. Bronchitis

E. Pleural effusion

Answer: B — ↓capillary blood volume.

  1. Physiological dead space includes:

A. Anatomical dead space only

B. Alveolar dead space only

C. Anatomical + alveolar dead space

D. Apparatus dead space

E. Trachea only

Answer: C — Anatomical + alveolar.

12. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 60-year-old with IPF has progressive dyspnoea, ↓FVC, ↓DLCO. Mechanism:

A. V/Q mismatch

B. Diffusion limitation (thickened alveolar membrane)

C. Shunt

D. Hypoventilation

E. Anaemia

Answer: B — IPF = diffusion limitation.

  1. A 70-year-old with COPD has ↓FEV₁, ↓FEV₁/FVC, ↓DLCO. Cause of ↓DLCO:

A. Mucus plugging

B. ↓alveolar surface area (emphysema)

C. Bronchospasm

D. Anaemia

E. Altitude

Answer: B — Loss of alveolar surface.

  1. A patient has ↑VD/VT (0.6) on ventilator. Cause:

A. Pneumonia

B. PE or overdistension

C. Bronchospasm

D. Anaemia

E. Obesity

Answer: B — High dead space.

  1. A patient with PE has ABG: pH 7.50, PaO₂ 65, PaCO₂ 30. Best interpretation:

A. Hypoventilation

B. Hyperventilation compensating for hypoxaemia (dead space)

C. Anxiety only

D. Asthma

E. Pneumonia

Answer: B — Compensatory hypervent.

  1. A 45-year-old with ↓DLCO but normal spirometry and no ILD findings. Cause:

A. Asthma

B. Pulmonary vascular disease (e.g. PAH, PE)

C. ILD

D. COPD

E. None

Answer: B — ↓capillary blood volume.

  1. DLCO is increased in:

A. Emphysema

B. Asthma, polycythaemia, L→R shunt, alveolar haemorrhage

C. ILD

D. Anaemia

E. PH

Answer: B — Multiple causes.

  1. Alveolar dead space is increased in:

A. Asthma

B. PE, severe emphysema, low cardiac output

C. Pneumonia

D. Anaemia

E. Obesity

Answer: B — Wasted ventilation.

  1. Treatment of dead space due to PE:

A. Bronchodilators

B. Anticoagulation ± thrombolysis

C. Steroids

D. Diuretics

E. Surgery

Answer: B — Treat PE.

  1. Pulmonary haemorrhage effect on DLCO:

A. ↓

B. ↑ (Hb in alveoli binds CO, ↑DLCO)

C. No change

D. Initially ↓, then ↑

E. Variable

Answer: B — ↑CO uptake.

  1. Increased dead space ventilation leads to:

A. ↑PaCO₂ (if no compensation)

B. Hyperventilation to maintain PaCO₂, until exhaustion

C. ↓PaCO₂ always

D. No effect

E. Always respiratory failure

Answer: B — Compensatory hyperventilation.

13. Flashcards

  • Q: Fick's law of diffusion?

A: V = A × D × ΔP / T

  • Q: Most sensitive test for diffusion limitation?

A: DLCO

  • Q: Why does CO₂ diffuse 20× faster than O₂?

A: More soluble in blood.

  • Q: ↓DLCO causes?

A: ILD, emphysema, pulmonary oedema, pulmonary vascular disease.

  • Q: ↑DLCO causes?

A: Asthma, polycythaemia, L→R shunt, alveolar haemorrhage.

  • Q: Normal physiological dead space (VD/VT)?

A: 0.20–0.35.

  • Q: Most common cause of ↑dead space?

A: Pulmonary embolism.

  • Q: Bohr equation?

A: VD/VT = (PaCO₂ − PECO₂) / PaCO₂.

  • Q: How does PE affect ABG?

A: ↓PaO₂ (↑A-a gradient), ↓PaCO₂ (hyperventilation).

  • Q: DLCO in IPF?

A: ↓↓ early; sensitive marker of disease severity.

14. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. D 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. C

SBAs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

15. Summary

Diffusion limitation (Fick's law) and dead space ventilation are key respiratory physiology concepts. DLCO is the most sensitive test for diffusion limitation; causes of ↓DLCO include ILD, emphysema, oedema, and pulmonary vascular disease. Dead space is increased in PE, severe COPD, and low cardiac output states; the Bohr equation (VD/VT) quantifies this.

Gas-specific differences
4. Dead space
Types of dead space
Bohr equation
Causes of increased dead space
5. Diagnosis
DLCO measurement
DLCO interpretation
DLCO patterns
ABG in dead space (e.g. PE)
6. Management
Diffusion limitation
Dead space
7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

15. Summary
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 17.1.06 High Flow Nasal Oxygen Basics
[!important]
High-flow nasal cannula (HFNC) delivers heated, humidified O₂ at flows of 20–60 L/min with adjustable FiO₂. It provides physiological benefits beyond conventional O₂ (dead space washout, mild PEEP, humidification) and is first-line in acute hypoxaemic respiratory failure (P/F 100–300).

Related: [[Oxygen Therapy and NIV]], [[Oxygen delivery devices]], [[ABG Interpretation]], [[Respiratory Failure]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: HFNC is first-line in acute hypoxaemic respiratory failure (P/F 100–300) and is also useful post-extubation, in immunocompromised patients, and for palliative dyspnoea. It is not appropriate for hypercapnic failure (use NIV instead).

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define HFNC and its mechanism
  • List indications and contraindications
  • Set up and titrate HFNC
  • Recognise when HFNC is failing
  • Distinguish from NIV/CPAP

2. Definition

HFNC = device delivering heated (37°C) and humidified (100% RH) O₂ via nasal cannula at flows >20 L/min (up to 60 L/min) with adjustable FiO₂ (0.21–1.0).

3. Mechanism / Physiological Effects

EffectMechanismBenefit
Dead space washoutHigh flow flushes nasopharyngeal CO₂↓PaCO₂, ↓work of breathing
Mild PEEP (3–5 cmH₂O)Continuous flow maintains airway patencyRecruits alveoli, ↓atelectasis
Stable FiO₂High flow matches inspiratory demandPredictable oxygenation
HumidificationHeated, 100% RH↓mucosal drying, ↑mucociliary clearance, comfort
Reduced WOB↓work of generating inspiratory flowLess fatigue

4. Indications

IndicationNotes
Acute hypoxaemic respiratory failureP/F 100–300; first-line
Pneumonia (CAP, COVID-19)Especially in immunocompromised
Post-extubationReduces reintubation
Pre-oxygenationBefore intubation
Palliative careDyspnoea in advanced disease
Bronchiolitis (paediatric)First-line in infants
Acute heart failureSometimes (if not hypercapnic)
ImmunocompromisedReduces intubation vs NIV

5. Contraindications

AbsoluteRelative
Need for immediate intubationHypercapnic respiratory failure (use NIV)
Recent upper airway surgeryAgitated/uncooperative patient
Severe facial trauma/burnsNasal obstruction (e.g. polyps)
Hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH <7.35) — use NIVGCS <8 (relative)

6. Setup

Equipment

  • HFNC device (e.g. AIRVO 2, Optiflow, Vapotherm)
  • Nasal cannula (sized to nares)
  • Tubing with humidification chamber
  • Sterile water for humidifier

Initial settings

ParameterInitialRange
Flow30–40 L/min20–60 L/min
FiO₂Titrate to SpO₂0.21–1.0
Temperature37°C (or 31°C for comfort)31–37°C

Titration

  • Start: 30 L/min + FiO₂ to target SpO₂ 92–98%
  • Increase flow to 40–50–60 L/min if WOB or hypoxaemia persists
  • Increase FiO₂ if SpO₂ < target
  • Monitor: ABG within 1–2 h, then 4–6 h

7. Monitoring

Clinical

  • Respiratory rate (key early indicator)
  • WOB (accessory muscle use, recession)
  • SpO₂ continuously
  • Heart rate, BP
  • Comfort (tolerates mouth breathing)

Lab

  • ABG within 1–2 h of initiation
  • Recheck after changes

HFNC failure criteria

  • ROX index = (SpO₂/FiO₂)/RR
  • ROX ≥4.88 at 2, 6, 12 h → likely success
  • ROX <3.85 → high risk of failure
  • ↑RR, ↓SpO₂ despite ↑FiO₂
  • Worsening ABG (↓PaO₂, ↑PaCO₂, ↓pH)
  • Exhaustion, GCS ↓

8. Management

Weaning

  • Step down when stable (SpO₂ >92% on FiO₂ <0.4, RR <25)
  • Reduce FiO₂ first (to 0.4)
  • Then reduce flow (to 20 L/min)
  • Switch to nasal cannula when FiO₂ <0.4 and flow <25 L/min

Escalation

  • HFNC failure → NIV (or intubation if critical)
  • Maintain airway, suction if needed
  • Consider position (sitting up, prone if ARDS)
  • Treat underlying cause

9. Comparison with Other Devices

DeviceFlowFiO₂HumidificationPEEPIndication
Nasal cannula1–6 L0.24–0.44NoNoStable mild hypoxaemia
HFNC20–60 L0.21–1.0Yes3–5 cmH₂OAcute hypoxaemic failure
CPAPVariable0.21–1.0Yes (with humidifier)5–15 cmH₂OOSA, oedema
BiPAPVariable0.21–1.0YesIPAP 10–20 / EPAP 4–8Hypercapnic failure

10. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
HFNC flow20–60 L/min
HFNC FiO₂0.21–1.0
HumidificationHeated, 100% RH (37°C)
PEEP effect3–5 cmH₂O
First-lineAcute hypoxaemic failure (P/F 100–300)
Not forHypercapnic failure (use NIV)
ROX index(SpO₂/FiO₂)/RR; ≥4.88 success
BenefitsDead space washout, humidification, comfort, stable FiO₂
Failure criteria↑RR, ↓SpO₂, exhaustion, worsening ABG

11. MCQs (10)

  1. HFNC delivers flow up to:

A. 10 L/min

B. 20 L/min

C. 60 L/min

D. 100 L/min

E. 200 L/min

Answer: C — 20–60 L/min.

  1. HFNC is first-line in:

A. Hypercapnic respiratory failure

B. Acute hypoxaemic respiratory failure (P/F 100–300)

C. OSA

D. Cardiac arrest

E. None

Answer: B — Hypoxaemic failure.

  1. HFNC temperature is typically:

A. 25°C

B. 37°C (with 100% humidification)

C. 40°C

D. Ambient

E. 20°C

Answer: B — 37°C, 100% RH.

  1. HFNC generates a PEEP of approximately:

A. 0

B. 3–5 cmH₂O

C. 10 cmH₂O

D. 15 cmH₂O

E. 20 cmH₂O

Answer: B — Mild PEEP.

  1. ROX index is calculated as:

A. (PaO₂/FiO₂)/RR

B. (SpO₂/FiO₂)/RR

C. RR/SpO₂

D. FiO₂/RR

E. SpO₂/PaO₂

Answer: B — (SpO₂/FiO₂)/RR.

  1. ROX ≥4.88 predicts:

A. HFNC failure

B. HFNC success

C. Need for intubation

D. Cardiac failure

E. None

Answer: B — Success.

  1. HFNC is not appropriate in:

A. Acute hypoxaemic failure

B. Post-extubation

C. Hypercapnic respiratory failure (use NIV)

D. Bronchiolitis

E. COVID-19 pneumonia

Answer: C — Use NIV.

  1. First-line in immunocompromised hypoxaemic failure:

A. Intubate

B. HFNC (reduces intubation)

C. NIV

D. Mask O₂

E. Surgery

Answer: B — HFNC.

  1. HFNC initial flow for adult:

A. 5 L

B. 15 L

C. 30–40 L/min

D. 80 L

E. 5 L

Answer: C — 30–40 L/min.

  1. When to step down HFNC:

A. Always

B. When SpO₂ >92% on FiO₂ <0.4 and RR <25

C. Never

D. After 24 h

E. Only at night

Answer: B — Stable on lower support.

12. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A patient with pneumonia is on HFNC at 40 L/min, FiO₂ 0.5, SpO₂ 90%, RR 28. Action:

A. Continue

B. Increase FiO₂ to 0.6 + check ABG; if not improving, escalate to NIV

C. Discharge

D. Stop

E. Antibiotics only

Answer: B — Step up FiO₂ + ABG; escalate if failing.

  1. HFNC vs NIV in hypercapnic failure:

A. HFNC better

B. NIV better

C. Equal

D. Both fail

E. Unknown

Answer: B — NIV for hypercapnia.

  1. A patient post-extubation on HFNC. Best step-down:

A. NRB

B. Nasal cannula 2 L (when FiO₂ <0.4)

C. Stop O₂

D. Mask

E. Intubate

Answer: B — Nasal cannula.

  1. HFNC is used as pre-oxygenation before intubation because:

A. Faster

B. Provides high FiO₂ + apnoeic oxygenation

C. Cheaper

D. No risk

E. None

Answer: B — Apnoeic oxygenation.

  1. ROX <3.85 at 12 h suggests:

A. HFNC success

B. HFNC failure — consider escalation

C. No change

D. Discharge

E. Stable

Answer: B — Failure.

  1. First-line in COVID-19 hypoxaemic failure:

A. CPAP

B. HFNC

C. Mask

D. Surgery

E. Steroids

Answer: B — HFNC.

  1. HFNC for hypercapnic patient:

A. First-line

B. Not first-line; use NIV

C. Equal

D. Always

E. Combination

Answer: B — NIV for hypercapnia.

  1. A patient on HFNC is desaturating, RR 35, exhaustion. Next step:

A. Continue HFNC

B. Intubate

C. Discharge

D. Antibiotics

E. Wait

Answer: B — Failure → intubate.

  1. HFNC humidification benefit:

A. No benefit

B. ↓Mucosal drying + ↑mucociliary clearance + comfort

C. Steroid effect

D. Antibiotic

E. None

Answer: B — Multiple benefits.

  1. HFNC flow in stable ward (post-recovery):

A. 60 L

B. 20 L/min (weaned)

C. 100 L

D. 5 L

E. 50 L

Answer: B — Weaned to 20 L/min.

13. Flashcards

  • Q: HFNC flow range?

A: 20–60 L/min.

  • Q: HFNC PEEP?

A: 3–5 cmH₂O.

  • Q: HFNC temperature?

A: 37°C, 100% RH.

  • Q: First-line indication?

A: Acute hypoxaemic failure (P/F 100–300).

  • Q: Not appropriate for?

A: Hypercapnic failure (use NIV).

  • Q: ROX index?

A: (SpO₂/FiO₂)/RR.

  • Q: ROX ≥4.88 predicts?

A: HFNC success.

  • Q: Initial flow?

A: 30–40 L/min.

  • Q: When to step down?

A: SpO₂ >92% on FiO₂ <0.4 and RR <25.

  • Q: HFNC vs mask O₂?

A: HFNC provides humidification, dead space washout, stable FiO₂, mild PEEP.

14. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. C 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. C 8. B 9. C 10. B

SBAs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

15. Summary

HFNC delivers 20–60 L/min of heated, humidified O₂ (37°C) with adjustable FiO₂ (0.21–1.0). Benefits include dead space washout, mild PEEP (3–5 cmH₂O), stable FiO₂, comfort. First-line in acute hypoxaemic failure (P/F 100–300). Not for hypercapnic failure (use NIV). ROX index (SpO₂/FiO₂/RR) ≥4.88 predicts success.

Weaning

▸ 17.1.07 Indications For Invasive Ventilation In Respiratory Disease
[!important]
Invasive mechanical ventilation is required when non-invasive support (O₂, HFNC, NIV) is insufficient or contraindicated. Key indications: cardiopulmonary arrest, severe hypoxaemia (PaO₂ <60 mmHg), severe acidosis (pH <7.20), respiratory muscle exhaustion, airway protection (GCS <8), NIV failure. Decision based on clinical + ABG + trajectory.

Related: [[Oxygen Therapy and NIV]], [[NIV failure and escalation triggers]], [[Respiratory Failure]], [[ARDS]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: "GCS ≤8, intubate". Classic indications: (1) respiratory arrest/apnoea, (2) severe hypoxaemia (PaO₂ <60 on high FiO₂), (3) severe acidosis (pH <7.20, ↑PaCO₂), (4) respiratory muscle exhaustion, (5) airway protection, (6) NIV failure. Don't delay intubation in deteriorating patients.

1. Definition

Invasive mechanical ventilation = positive pressure ventilation delivered via endotracheal tube (or tracheostomy).

2. Absolute Indications

1. Cardiopulmonary arrest

  • Intubate immediately
  • Danger, Airway, Response, Send for help (DARS)
  • Compressions, Airway, Breathing (CAB)

2. Respiratory arrest / apnoea

  • Bag-mask ventilation initially
  • Intubate when feasible

3. Severe hypoxaemia

  • PaO₂ <60 mmHg (8 kPa) on high FiO₂ (P/F <200)
  • P/F ratio <200 with bilateral infiltrates (ARDS criteria)
  • Severe pneumonia with rapid deterioration
  • Massive PE with shock
  • Pulmonary oedema unresponsive to NIV

4. Severe hypercapnic acidosis

  • pH <7.20 with ↑PaCO₂
  • NIV failure (pH <7.20 after 1–2 h of NIV)
  • Severe asthma with ↑PaCO₂ + exhaustion
  • Severe AECOPD with exhaustion

5. Respiratory muscle exhaustion

  • Paradoxical breathing (abdominal paradox)
  • Tachypnoea >30 (sustained)
  • Use of accessory muscles with worsening
  • Drowsiness (hypercapnic encephalopathy)
  • Silent chest (severe asthma)

6. Airway protection

  • GCS ≤8 (cannot protect airway)
  • Severe bulbar dysfunction (GBS, MG, stroke)
  • Massive haemoptysis with airway compromise
  • Upper airway obstruction (FB, angioedema, severe stridor)

7. NIV failure

  • pH <7.20 after 1–2 h of NIV
  • ↑PaCO₂ + ↑RR + GCS ↓ despite NIV
  • Mask intolerance
  • Exhaustion

3. Relative Indications

  • Impending respiratory failure (tachypnoea >35, exhaustion)
  • ↓Mental status (GCS <10) with risk of aspiration
  • Severe pneumonia with rapid deterioration
  • ARDS with P/F <200
  • Massive PE with shock
  • Severe asthma not responding to therapy

4. Specific Disease Indications

DiseaseSpecific indication
Severe asthma↑PaCO₂ + exhaustion, silent chest, ↑RR
COPD (AECOPD)NIV failure (pH <7.25), GCS ↓, shock
ARDSP/F <200 + exhaustion
PneumoniaSepsis + respiratory failure, GCS ↓
PE (massive)Shock + respiratory failure
NM weakness (GBS, MG)VC <20 mL/kg, GCS ↓, aspiration
APOCardiogenic shock, severe acidosis, exhaustion

5. Clinical Assessment

Signs of impending respiratory failure

  • ↑Respiratory rate (>30/min, or paradoxically slowing)
  • Accessory muscle use (SCM, scalenes)
  • Paradoxical breathing (abdominal paradox — diaphragmatic fatigue)
  • Tracheal tug
  • Cyanosis
  • Confusion, agitation, drowsiness (hypoxaemia/hypercapnia)
  • Diaphoresis

ABG criteria

ParameterThreshold
PaO₂<60 mmHg (8 kPa) on FiO₂ ≥0.5 (or P/F <200)
PaCO₂>60 mmHg (8 kPa) with pH <7.20
pH<7.20 (especially rising PaCO₂)
SpO₂<90% despite high FiO₂

6. Pre-Intubation Checklist

Equipment

  • Suction (working)
  • Bag-valve-mask (BVM) + O₂
  • Laryngoscope (Mac 3–4, Miller 2–3)
  • Endotracheal tubes (7.0, 7.5, 8.0 mm)
  • Bougie, stylet
  • Capnography
  • Monitoring (SpO₂, ECG, BP)
  • Emergency drugs (succinylcholine, rocuronium, propofol, ketamine, fentanyl)

Drugs (Rapid Sequence Induction)

  • Preoxygenation: 100% O₂ for 3–5 min
  • Induction: propofol 1.5–2.5 mg/kg, ketamine 1–2 mg/kg, or etomidate 0.3 mg/kg
  • Paralysis: succinylcholine 1.5 mg/kg (rapid onset, short) or rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg (longer, reversible with sugammadex)
  • Cricoid pressure (controversial)

Difficult airway predictors

  • Previous difficult intubation
  • Mallampati III–IV
  • Limited mouth opening, neck mobility
  • Obstructive sleep apnoea
  • Obesity, large neck

7. Post-Intubation Management

Initial ventilator settings

ParameterSetting
ModeVolume-control (AC/VC) or pressure-control (PC)
Tidal volume6–8 mL/kg (lung-protective; 4–6 in ARDS)
RR12–20/min
FiO₂Titrate to SpO₂ 92–96% (88–92% in COPD)
PEEP5–10 cmH₂O (higher in ARDS)
I:E ratio1:2 (longer expiration in obstructive)

Monitoring

  • Capnography (ETCO₂)
  • Pulse oximetry
  • ABG within 30–60 min
  • Ventilator waveforms (pressure, flow, volume loops)
  • CXR (post-intubation, daily)
  • Sedation score (RASS)

Sedation

  • Light sedation preferred (RASS 0 to -2)
  • Propofol (short, ↑TG with prolonged)
  • Dexmedetomidine (less respiratory depression)
  • Midazolam (accumulation in renal failure)
  • Fentanyl/morphine (analgesia)

8. Complications of Invasive Ventilation

ComplicationMechanismManagement
VAPAspiration, colonisationHead elevation, oral care, sedation holiday
BarotraumaHigh pressures↓Pplat, lung-protective
VolutraumaHigh volumes↓VT (6 mL/kg)
AtelectraumaRepetitive collapsePEEP
O₂ toxicityHigh FiO₂↓FiO₂ (target <0.6)
VILIMulti-factorialLung-protective
Hemodynamic compromise↑Intrathoracic pressure, ↓preloadFluids, vasopressors
AspirationETT bypasses upper airwayCuff pressure, OG tube
DeliriumICU environmentSleep-wake cycle, antipsychotics
Tracheal stenosisCuff pressureMonitor cuff <30 cmH₂O

9. Weaning

Criteria

  • Underlying cause resolving
  • SpO₂ >90% on FiO₂ ≤0.4, PEEP ≤5
  • RSBI (Rapid Shallow Breathing Index) <105 (RR/TV)
  • Hemodynamic stability
  • Adequate mental status
  • Able to protect airway

Spontaneous breathing trial (SBT)

  • T-piece or PSV 5–8 cmH₂O for 30–120 min
  • Monitor RR, SpO₂, ABG
  • Success → extubate
  • Failure (RR >35, SpO₂ <90, distress, ↑PaCO₂) → re-ventilate

10. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
"GCS 8, intubate"Airway protection
Absolute indicationsArrest, severe hypoxaemia, pH <7.20, exhaustion, airway protection
NIV failure criteriapH <7.20, ↑PaCO₂, exhaustion, GCS ↓
Initial ventilator settingsVT 6–8 mL/kg, RR 12–20, PEEP 5–10, FiO₂ titrated
Lung-protective ventilationVT 6 mL/kg, plateau <30 cmH₂O (ARDS)
CapnographyConfirms ETT placement
ComplicationsVAP, barotrauma, O₂ toxicity, delirium
WeaningRSBI <105, SBT 30–120 min, extubate

11. MCQs (10)

  1. The classic threshold for intubation in altered mental status is:

A. GCS <12

B. GCS ≤8

C. GCS <10

D. GCS <14

E. GCS <6

Answer: B — GCS ≤8, intubate.

  1. Absolute indication for intubation:

A. Mild hypoxaemia

B. Cardiopulmonary arrest

C. Mild dyspnoea

D. Tachypnoea alone

E. Cough

Answer: B — Arrest.

  1. NIV failure is indicated by:

A. Stable pH

B. pH <7.20, ↑PaCO₂, exhaustion

C. ↓RR

D. Improved SpO₂

E. Comfortable patient

Answer: B — Acidosis + exhaustion.

  1. Initial tidal volume in ARDS (lung-protective):

A. 12 mL/kg

B. 10 mL/kg

C. 6 mL/kg

D. 4 mL/kg

E. 15 mL/kg

Answer: C — 6 mL/kg.

  1. RSBI (Rapid Shallow Breathing Index) is calculated as:

A. RR × TV

B. RR / TV (litres)

C. TV / RR

D. RR + TV

E. SpO₂ / RR

Answer: B — RR/TV.

  1. RSBI <105 predicts:

A. Weaning failure

B. Weaning success

C. ARDS

D. PE

E. None

Answer: B — Success.

  1. Plateau pressure target in ARDS:

A. <50 cmH₂O

B. <30 cmH₂O

C. >40 cmH₂O

D. 60 cmH₂O

E. No target

Answer: B — <30 cmH₂O.

  1. Capnography in intubation confirms:

A. Heart rate

B. ETT placement in trachea (ETCO₂)

C. BP

D. SpO₂

E. RR

Answer: B — ETCO₂ detection.

  1. First-line drug for rapid sequence intubation in haemodynamic instability:

A. Propofol

B. Ketamine (preserves BP)

C. Thiopentone

D. Etomidate only

E. Midazolam

Answer: B — Ketamine.

  1. Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) prevention includes:

A. Supine position

B. Head elevation 30–45° + oral care + sedation holiday

C. Routine antibiotics

D. Steroids

E. None

Answer: B — Bundle.

12. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 60-year-old with severe pneumonia has PaO₂ 50 on 15 L NRB, RR 38, GCS 13. Best action:

A. NRB

B. Intubate (severe hypoxaemia not responding)

C. HFNC

D. Wait

E. Discharge

Answer: B — Severe hypoxaemia.

  1. A patient on NIV for AECOPD has pH 7.18, ↑PaCO₂, GCS dropping. Action:

A. Continue NIV

B. Intubate immediately

C. Sedate

D. Discharge

E. Antibiotics

Answer: B — NIV failure.

  1. Initial tidal volume in non-ARDS invasive ventilation:

A. 4 mL/kg

B. 6–8 mL/kg

C. 12 mL/kg

D. 15 mL/kg

E. 20 mL/kg

Answer: B — 6–8 mL/kg.

  1. A patient on ventilator has P/F 100, bilateral infiltrates. ARDS severity:

A. Mild

B. Moderate

C. Severe

D. No ARDS

E. Indeterminate

Answer: C — Severe (P/F ≤100).

  1. Spontaneous breathing trial duration:

A. 5 min

B. 30–120 min

C. 4 h

D. 24 h

E. None

Answer: B — 30–120 min.

  1. The first sign of ventilator-induced diaphragmatic dysfunction:

A. Hypercapnia

B. Difficulty weaning

C. Hypoxaemia

D. Tachycardia

E. None

Answer: B — Weaning difficulty.

  1. A patient on ventilator has sudden hypotension + tachycardia. First step:

A. CPR

B. Disconnect from ventilator, hand-bag, assess for tension PTX, ETT displacement, hypovolaemia

C. Vasopressors

D. Stop

E. Intubate again

Answer: B — DOPE mnemonic.

  1. ETT cuff pressure target:

A. 10–20 cmH₂O

B. 20–30 cmH₂O

C. 40–50 cmH₂O

D. >50

E. No target

Answer: B — 20–30 cmH₂O.

  1. The most common cause of failure to intubate:

A. Laryngeal oedema

B. Poor visualisation

C. Tumour

D. Secretions

E. Equipment

Answer: B — Poor view.

  1. Permissive hypercapnia in ARDS:

A. Allowed (pH >7.2) to avoid barotrauma

B. Always correct

C. Never

D. Same as normal

E. None

Answer: A — Allowed pH >7.2.

13. Flashcards

  • Q: GCS threshold for intubation?

A: ≤8.

  • Q: Absolute indications for intubation?

A: Arrest, severe hypoxaemia, pH <7.20, exhaustion, airway protection.

  • Q: NIV failure criteria?

A: pH <7.20, ↑PaCO₂, exhaustion, GCS ↓, mask intolerance.

  • Q: Lung-protective VT?

A: 6 mL/kg ideal body weight.

  • Q: Plateau pressure target in ARDS?

A: <30 cmH₂O.

  • Q: RSBI formula?

A: RR / TV (litres).

  • Q: RSBI threshold?

A: <105 predicts successful weaning.

  • Q: ETT cuff pressure?

A: 20–30 cmH₂O.

  • Q: VAP prevention bundle?

A: Head elevation, oral care, sedation holiday, subglottic secretion drainage.

  • Q: Initial induction drug in shock?

A: Ketamine (preserves BP).

14. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — GCS ≤8, intubate.
  2. B — Cardiopulmonary arrest (immediate intubation).
  3. B — pH <7.20, ↑PaCO₂, exhaustion.
  4. C — 6 mL/kg (lung-protective).
  5. B — RR / TV (litres).
  6. B — Success (RSBI <105).
  7. B — <30 cmH₂O (lung-protective).
  8. B — ETCO₂ detection.
  9. B — Ketamine (preserves BP).
  10. B — Head elevation, oral care, sedation holiday (VAP prevention bundle).

SBAs

  1. B — Severe hypoxaemia not responding to NRB → intubate.
  2. B — NIV failure (pH <7.20, ↑PaCO₂, GCS dropping) → intubate.
  3. B — 6–8 mL/kg (initial tidal volume in non-ARDS).
  4. C — P/F 100 = severe ARDS (Berlin definition).
  5. B — SBT 30–120 min.
  6. B — Difficulty weaning = first sign of VIDD.
  7. B — DOPE mnemonic: Displace, Obstruct, Pneumothorax, Equipment.
  8. B — 20–30 cmH₂O (cuff pressure target).
  9. B — Poor visualisation (most common cause of failure).
  10. A — Permissive hypercapnia allowed (pH >7.2) to avoid barotrauma.

15. Summary

Invasive ventilation is required for cardiopulmonary arrest, severe hypoxaemia (P/F <200), severe acidosis (pH <7.20), exhaustion, airway protection (GCS ≤8), NIV failure. Initial settings: VT 6–8 mL/kg, RR 12–20, PEEP 5–10. Lung-protective (VT 6 mL/kg, plateau <30 cmH₂O) in ARDS. Complications: VAP, barotrauma, O₂ toxicity, delirium. Weaning: RSBI <105, SBT 30–120 min.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 17.1.08 Oxygen Delivery Devices
[!important]
Selection of the correct O₂ delivery device is critical for achieving target SpO₂ while avoiding complications (CO₂ retention, mucosal drying, O₂ toxicity). Devices range from nasal cannula (low flow) to non-rebreather mask (high flow) to HFNC and NIV/CPAP for more advanced support.

Related: [[Oxygen Therapy and NIV]], [[ABG Interpretation]], [[Respiratory Failure]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: For patients at risk of hypercapnia (COPD, OHS, NM weakness), use Venturi mask (24–28%) for controlled O₂. Reserve high-flow O₂ (15 L NRB) for emergencies. Target SpO₂ 94–98% (most patients) or 88–92% (hypercapnic risk).

1. Learning Objectives

  • List O₂ delivery devices and their FiO₂ ranges
  • Select appropriate device for clinical scenario
  • Recognise when to use controlled O₂ (Venturi)
  • Understand HFNC, CPAP, BiPAP basics
  • Apply to acute and chronic settings

2. Low-Flow Devices

DeviceFlow (L/min)FiO₂Indications
Nasal cannula1–60.24–0.44 (↑1% per L)Stable patients, mild hypoxaemia, home O₂
Simple face mask5–100.35–0.60Short-term O₂, ED, ward
Non-rebreather mask (NRB)10–150.60–0.90Emergency, severe hypoxaemia

Nasal cannula

  • Most common low-flow device
  • FiO₂ increases ~4% per L/min (1 L = 24%, 6 L = 44%)
  • Limitations: variable FiO₂ (depends on respiratory pattern), drying, no precise delivery
  • Suitable for: stable ward patients, home O₂, mild-moderate hypoxaemia

Simple face mask

  • 5–10 L/min
  • FiO₂ 0.35–0.60
  • Reservoir effect (small)
  • Not for CO₂ retainers

Non-rebreather mask

  • Reservoir bag + one-way valves
  • 10–15 L/min (must keep bag inflated)
  • FiO₂ up to 0.90
  • For emergencies/severe hypoxaemia
  • Avoid in COPD (risk of CO₂ retention)

3. High-Flow / Fixed-Performance Devices

Venturi mask (preferred for COPD)

  • Fixed performance (precise FiO₂)
  • Colours indicate FiO₂:
  • Blue = 24% (2 L)
  • White = 28% (4 L)
  • Yellow = 35% (8 L)
  • Red = 40% (10 L)
  • Green = 60% (15 L)
  • First-line for COPD with hypercapnic risk
  • Target SpO₂ 88–92%

High-Flow Nasal Cannula (HFNC)

  • High flow (20–60 L/min) + heated + humidified
  • FiO₂ 0.21–1.0 (variable)
  • Washout of nasopharyngeal dead space (↓CO₂)
  • CPAP effect (3–5 cmH₂O)
  • Indications: acute hypoxaemic respiratory failure (P/F 100–300), post-extubation, immunocompromised
  • Advantages: comfortable, tolerates mouth breathing, mucociliary clearance

4. Non-Invasive Ventilation (NIV)

CPAP (continuous positive airway pressure)

  • Single pressure (e.g. 5–15 cmH₂O)
  • Indications: OSA, cardiogenic pulmonary oedema
  • Mechanism: recruits alveoli, maintains airway patency

BiPAP (bilevel positive airway pressure)

  • Two pressures: IPAP (inspiratory) + EPAP (expiratory)
  • IPAP: 10–20 cmH₂O (drives ventilation)
  • EPAP: 4–8 cmH₂O (recruits, prevents collapse)
  • Indications: hypercapnic respiratory failure (COPD, OHS, NM weakness), acute cardiogenic oedema
  • See [[Oxygen Therapy and NIV|Oxygen Therapy and NIV]] for details

5. Special Devices

Tracheostomy mask

  • For tracheostomy patients
  • Delivers humidified O₂
  • Connects to tracheostomy tube

T-piece (T-piece resuscitator)

  • For extubated patients / spontaneous breathing trial
  • Delivers humidified O₂ with PEEP valve

Incubator / hood (neonates)

  • Controlled environment for infants

6. Selection Algorithm

SpO₂ targetClinical scenarioDevice
94–98% (most)Pneumonia, asthma, most wardNasal cannula 2–4 L, simple mask 5 L
88–92% (hypercapnic risk)AECOPD, OHS, NM weakness, severe asthmaVenturi 24–28%
EmergencySevere hypoxaemia, shock, arrestNRB 15 L
HFNCAcute hypoxaemic failure, post-extubation, immunocompromisedHFNC 30–60 L, FiO₂ titrated
CPAPOSA, cardiogenic oedemaCPAP 5–15 cmH₂O
BiPAPHypercapnic failure (pH 7.25–7.35)BiPAP IPAP 12–15, EPAP 4–5

7. Management

Initiation

  • Select device based on target SpO₂ + clinical scenario
  • Start with appropriate flow/FiO₂
  • ABG within 30–60 min for acutely unwell patients
  • Monitor SpO₂ continuously

Titration

  • Adjust FiO₂/flow to target SpO₂
  • Recheck ABG if unstable or after major change
  • Document device, flow, FiO₂, SpO₂ hourly

Weaning

  • Step-down when SpO₂ stable on lower support:
  • NRB → simple mask → nasal cannula → room air
  • HFNC → nasal cannula → room air
  • NIV/CPAP → HFNC or nasal cannula → room air
  • Wean slowly; check ABG/SpO₂ after each step

Complications

  • CO₂ retention (high-flow O₂ in COPD)
  • Mucosal drying (nasal cannula, HFNC)
  • O₂ toxicity (prolonged FiO₂ >0.60)
  • Pressure injury (CPAP/BiPAP mask)
  • Aspiration (impaired swallowing with HFNC)
  • Fire risk (especially with NRB, smoking)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
Nasal cannula1–6 L = 24–44% FiO₂ (↑4% per L)
Simple mask5–10 L = 35–60% FiO₂
Non-rebreather10–15 L = 60–90% FiO₂; emergencies only
Venturi maskFixed FiO₂ (24%, 28%, 35%, 40%, 60%); first-line for COPD
HFNC20–60 L, heated, humidified; acute hypoxaemic failure
CPAPOSA, cardiogenic oedema
BiPAPHypercapnic failure (pH 7.25–7.35)
Target SpO₂94–98% (most); 88–92% (hypercapnic risk)
ABG within 1 hAfter starting O₂ in acute patients
Avoid high-flow O₂In COPD (use Venturi)

9. MCQs (10)

  1. A COPD patient needs O₂. First-line device:

A. Nasal cannula 4 L

B. Venturi mask 24–28%

C. NRB 15 L

D. Simple mask

E. HFNC

Answer: B — Venturi for COPD (controlled FiO₂).

  1. Nasal cannula 2 L delivers approximately:

A. 21%

B. 28%

C. 35%

D. 50%

E. 100%

Answer: B — 2 L = 24% + 4% = 28%.

  1. A 60-year-old with severe pneumonia has SpO₂ 80% on 15 L NRB. Best next step:

A. Continue NRB

B. HFNC or NIV/CPAP

C. Simple mask

D. Nasal cannula

E. Observation

Answer: B — Severe hypoxaemia not responding to NRB → HFNC/NIV.

  1. A blue Venturi mask delivers:

A. 21%

B. 24%

C. 28%

D. 35%

E. 60%

Answer: B — Blue = 24% (2 L).

  1. O₂ toxicity occurs with prolonged FiO₂ above:

A. 0.30

B. 0.50

C. 0.60

D. 0.80

E. 1.00

Answer: C — >0.60 for >24–48 h.

  1. HFNC delivers:

A. 2–6 L

B. 10–15 L

C. 20–60 L

D. 100 L

E. 0.5–1 L

Answer: C — 20–60 L.

  1. CPAP first-line in:

A. Severe COPD

B. OSA, cardiogenic pulmonary oedema

C. PE

D. Pneumonia

E. ARDS

Answer: B — CPAP for OSA/oedema.

  1. BiPAP first-line in:

A. OSA

B. Hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH 7.25–7.35) in COPD

C. PE

D. ARDS

E. Pneumonia

Answer: B — BiPAP for hypercapnic.

  1. Target SpO₂ in a COPD patient:

A. 94–98%

B. 88–92%

C. 100%

D. 85–88%

E. ≥95%

Answer: B — 88–92% in COPD.

  1. Which device provides the most precise FiO₂?

A. Nasal cannula

B. Simple mask

C. NRB

D. Venturi mask

E. HFNC

Answer: D — Venturi = fixed performance.

10. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A patient with severe asthma has SpO₂ 88% on arrival. Initial O₂:

A. Venturi 24%

B. High-flow O₂ (15 L NRB) to target 94–98%

C. Nasal cannula 2 L

D. No O₂

E. HFNC

Answer: B — Acute severe asthma, no hypercapnic risk.

  1. First-line O₂ for a stable ward patient with mild hypoxaemia:

A. NRB

B. Nasal cannula 2 L

C. HFNC

D. Venturi

E. CPAP

Answer: B — Mild hypoxaemia.

  1. A patient with OHS is on home BiPAP. Admitted with pneumonia. SpO₂ 88%:

A. Add supplemental O₂ + continue BiPAP

B. Stop BiPAP

C. Intubate

D. Discharge

E. CPAP only

Answer: A — BiPAP + O₂.

  1. A patient on 28% Venturi has SpO₂ 88%. Action:

A. Stop O₂

B. Increase to 35% Venturi + recheck ABG

C. Intubate

D. Discharge

E. No action

Answer: B — Step up.

  1. HFNC is first-line in:

A. Severe COPD

B. Acute hypoxaemic respiratory failure (P/F 100–300)

C. PE

D. OSA

E. None

Answer: B — HFNC for mild-moderate hypoxaemic.

  1. A nasal cannula flow of 6 L delivers:

A. 24%

B. 28%

C. 44%

D. 50%

E. 60%

Answer: C — 6 L = 24% + 4×6 = 48%? Actually 24% + 4% per L = 24% + 20% = 44%.

  1. A patient with severe hypoxaemia on NRB has dry mucosa. Solution:

A. Reduce flow

B. Add humidification or switch to HFNC

C. Stop O₂

D. Intubate

E. Steroids

Answer: B — Humidify.

  1. A 70-year-old with COPD and SpO₂ 92% on 2 L nasal cannula. Target achieved?

A. Yes (≥88% in COPD)

B. No (target 94–98%)

C. No (target 100%)

D. No (target 95%)

E. Cannot determine

Answer: A — 92% is within 88–92% target for COPD.

  1. A COPD patient on 28% Venturi has ABG: PaO₂ 60, PaCO₂ 50. Worsening. Action:

A. Increase O₂ to 35%

B. Reduce O₂ to 24% (24–28% may worsen hypercapnia) + consider NIV

C. Intubate

D. Discharge

E. Steroids

Answer: B — Lower O₂, consider NIV.

  1. A patient on HFNC has SpO₂ 95% on 40 L/min, FiO₂ 0.5. P/F ratio:

A. 95

B. 150

C. 190

D. 190 (PaO₂ 95 mmHg on FiO₂ 0.5)

E. 200

Answer: D — SpO₂ ~95% ≈ PaO₂ 80; P/F = 80/0.5 = 160. (Approximate).

11. Flashcards

  • Q: First-line O₂ device for COPD?

A: Venturi mask 24–28% (controlled FiO₂).

  • Q: Nasal cannula 2 L FiO₂?

A: ~28%.

  • Q: Non-rebreather flow?

A: 10–15 L/min (keep reservoir bag inflated).

  • Q: Venturi blue colour?

A: 24% (2 L).

  • Q: HFNC flow range?

A: 20–60 L/min.

  • Q: CPAP first-line in?

A: OSA, cardiogenic pulmonary oedema.

  • Q: BiPAP first-line in?

A: Hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH 7.25–7.35) in COPD, OHS, NM weakness.

  • Q: Target SpO₂ in COPD?

A: 88–92%.

  • Q: O₂ toxicity threshold?

A: FiO₂ >0.60 for >24–48 h.

  • Q: ABG check after starting O₂ in acute patient?

A: Within 30–60 min.

12. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. C 6. C 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. D

SBAs

  1. B 2. B 3. A 4. B 5. B 6. C 7. B 8. A 9. B 10. D

13. Summary

O₂ delivery devices: nasal cannula (low flow, 24–44%), simple mask (5–10 L, 35–60%), NRB (10–15 L, 60–90%, emergencies), Venturi (fixed FiO₂, first-line for COPD), HFNC (20–60 L, acute hypoxaemic failure), CPAP (OSA, oedema), BiPAP (hypercapnic failure). Target SpO₂ 94–98% (most) or 88–92% (hypercapnic risk).
