▸ 17.2.03 Acute Severe Breathlessness Approach
[!important]
Acute severe breathlessness is a time-critical emergency. Immediate management: A–B–C assessment, ABG, O₂, treat life-threatening causes (PTX, PE, severe asthma, pulmonary oedema, anaphylaxis, arrhythmia). Common differentials: pneumonia, PE, asthma, COPD exacerbation, pneumothorax, pulmonary oedema, hyperventilation.

Related: [[Dyspnea]], [[Acute hypoxemia approach]], [[Pneumothorax]], [[Pulmonary Embolism]], [[Respiratory Failure]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: Sudden dyspnoea + unilateral hyperresonance + ↓breath sounds = pneumothorax → needle decompression + chest drain. Sudden + pleuritic + tachycardia = PE. Acute severe asthma = silent chest = intubation consideration. Pulmonary oedema = bilateral crackles + S3 + cardiomegaly = diuretics.

1. Definition

Acute breathlessness = sudden onset dyspnoea (seconds to hours), often severe enough to require immediate assessment.

2. Aetiology

Life-threatening

CauseClue
Tension pneumothoraxUnilateral hyperresonance, ↓BS, tracheal deviation, shock
Massive PESudden, pleuritic, tachycardia, hypotension, A-a gradient
Severe asthmaSilent chest, exhaustion, normal/↑PaCO₂
Acute pulmonary oedemaBilateral crackles, S3, cardiomegaly, pink frothy sputum
AnaphylaxisUrticaria, angioedema, hypotension, stridor
Cardiac arrest / arrhythmiaShock, no output, VT/VF
Upper airway obstructionStridor, drooling, tripod
ARDSRefractory hypoxaemia, bilateral infiltrates
Pneumothorax (simple)Sudden pleuritic pain, dyspnoea, ↓BS
Massive haemoptysisBlood, airway compromise

Acute (less immediately life-threatening)

  • Pneumonia (CAP, HAP)
  • Asthma exacerbation (mild–moderate)
  • AECOPD
  • Pleural effusion (large)
  • Pulmonary embolism (submassive)
  • Anxiety / hyperventilation (after exclusion)
  • Panic attack
  • Metabolic acidosis (Kussmaul)
  • Anaemia (acute drop, e.g. GI bleed)

3. Pathophysiology

Mechanisms of dyspnoea

  • ↑Drive (hypoxaemia, hypercapnia, acidosis)
  • ↑Work (obstructive/restrictive)
  • ↑Ventilation required (metabolic acidosis)
  • Weak muscles (NM, fatigue)

4. Clinical Assessment

Immediate (ABCs)

  • Airway (patent, no obstruction)
  • Breathing (RR, SpO₂, breath sounds, stridor, wheeze)
  • Circulation (HR, BP, JVP, oedema)
  • Disability (GCS, confusion)
  • Exposure (chest wall, legs)

History (rapid)

  • Onset (sudden vs gradual)
  • Triggers (allergens, exertion, position, trauma)
  • Associated symptoms (chest pain, palpitations, wheeze, fever, sputum, haemoptysis, leg swelling)
  • Past history (asthma, COPD, HF, cancer, DVT/PE)
  • Drugs (allergies, ACE-i, anticoagulants)
  • Smoking, occupation

Examination

  • General: distress, cyanosis, clubbing, cachexia
  • Neck: JVP, tracheal deviation, stridor
  • Chest: inspection, percussion, auscultation
  • Cardiac: murmurs, S3, S4, pericardial rub
  • Limbs: oedema, calf swelling (DVT), cyanosis, clubbing

5. Diagnosis

Immediate investigations

TestPurpose
SpO₂Hypoxaemia
ABGHypoxaemia, hypercapnia, pH
ECGMI, arrhythmia, RV strain, pericarditis
CXR (bedside)Pneumothorax, oedema, consolidation
Bedside echoPericardial effusion, RV strain, LV function
FBC, U&E, CRP, troponin, BNP, D-dimerAs indicated

Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Acute severe breathlessness
↓
ABC + SpO2 + ABG + ECG + CXR
↓
Unilateral hyperresonance + ↓BS?
Sudden + pleuritic + tachycardia?
Bilateral crackles + cardiomegaly?
Silent chest + exhaustion?
Stridor + anaphylaxis?
Otherwise: pneumonia, AECOPD, asthma, effusion, etc.
↓
Tension PTX → needle decompression + drain
PE suspected → CTPA + Wells/D-dimer
Acute pulmonary oedema → diuretics + O2 + NIV
Severe asthma → intubation consideration
IM adrenaline + airway

6. Management

General

  • Position: sitting up (improves mechanics)
  • O₂: target SpO₂ 94–98% (88–92% if hypercapnic risk)
  • IV access
  • Monitoring: SpO₂, RR, HR, BP, conscious level
  • Reassure (anxiety worsens dyspnoea)
  • Specific Rx by cause

By cause

CauseSpecific Rx
Tension PTXNeedle decompression (2nd ICS, midclavicular) + chest drain
Massive PEThrombolysis (alteplase) + anticoagulation
Acute pulmonary oedemaO₂ + IV furosemide + GTN + NIV (CPAP) +/− morphine
Severe asthmaO₂ + SABA + ipratropium nebs + IV hydrocortisone + IV MgSO₄ + intubation if failing
AECOPDO₂ (controlled 24–28%) + SABA + ipratropium + steroid + NIV (pH 7.25–7.35) + antibiotics
PneumoniaAntibiotics + O₂ + supportive
AnaphylaxisIM adrenaline 0.5 mg + O₂ + IV fluids + antihistamine + steroids +/− intubation
Pleural effusion (large)Therapeutic thoracentesis
Anxiety/panicReassure, breathing exercises, treat cause
Metabolic acidosisTreat cause (DKA, renal failure)

7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
Life-threateningTension PTX, massive PE, severe asthma, APO, anaphylaxis
Sudden + pleuriticPE, PTX
StridorUpper airway obstruction, anaphylaxis
Bilateral cracklesAPO, ARDS, pneumonia
WheezeAsthma, COPD
PositionSitting up
O₂ target94–98% (88–92% if hypercapnic)
Tension PTXNeedle decompression (2nd ICS, midclavicular)

8. MCQs (10)

  1. Sudden breathlessness + unilateral hyperresonance + ↓BS. First:

A. CXR

B. Needle decompression (tension pneumothorax)

C. Wait

D. Antibiotics

E. None

Answer: B — Decompress.

  1. Sudden breathlessness + pleuritic chest pain + tachycardia + normal CXR. Next:

A. Antibiotics

B. Wells score + D-dimer → CTPA (suspected PE)

C. Discharge

D. Steroids

E. None

Answer: B — Workup for PE.

  1. Acute severe breathlessness + bilateral crackles + S3 + cardiomegaly. First:

A. Antibiotics

B. IV furosemide + O₂ + GTN (acute pulmonary oedema)

C. Steroids

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — Pulmonary oedema.

  1. Silent chest in acute severe asthma indicates:

A. Improvement

B. Critical airflow obstruction (life-threatening)

C. Wheeze

D. Recovery

E. None

Answer: B — Critical.

  1. Anaphylaxis with stridor and hypotension. First:

A. Steroids

B. IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (1:1000)

C. Antihistamine

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — IM adrenaline.

  1. The most common cause of acute breathlessness in ED:

A. PE

B. Pneumonia / AECOPD / asthma

C. Tension PTX

D. None

E. APO

Answer: B — Pneumonia/COPD/asthma.

  1. Sudden dyspnoea with normal CXR, ECG S1Q3T3. Diagnosis:

A. Pneumonia

B. Pulmonary embolism

C. Asthma

D. None

E. PTX

Answer: B — PE.

  1. The most useful bedside test for tension pneumothorax:

A. CXR

B. Clinical diagnosis (hyperresonance + ↓BS + shock)

C. ABG

D. None

E. CT

Answer: B — Clinical.

  1. Position for acute severe breathlessness:

A. Supine

B. Sitting up (improves diaphragm mechanics)

C. Prone

D. Trendelenburg

E. None

Answer: B — Sitting.

  1. Tension pneumothorax decompression site:

A. 5th ICS, midaxillary

B. 2nd ICS, midclavicular

C. Back

D. None

E. 4th ICS

Answer: B — 2nd ICS, midclavicular.

9. SBA Questions (10)

  1. The most important first step in acute breathlessness:

A. CXR

B. ABCs (airway, breathing, circulation)

C. ABG

D. Spirogram

E. None

Answer: B — ABCs.

  1. Tension pneumothorax first management:

A. CXR

B. Needle decompression (don't wait for CXR)

C. CT

D. Discharge

E. None

Answer: B — Decompress.

  1. Acute breathlessness + ↓SpO₂ + bilateral crackles + normal CXR. Cause:

A. PTX

B. Pulmonary embolism (or early APO)

C. None

D. Asthma

E. COPD

Answer: B — PE.

  1. Anxiety/panic-related hyperventilation. ABG:

A. ↓PaO₂, ↓PaCO₂

B. ↓PaO₂, ↓PaCO₂ (resp alkalosis) + normal A-a

C. ↑PaO₂

D. ↑PaCO₂

E. None

Answer: B — Hypervent + resp alkalosis + normal A-a.

  1. Pleural effusion (massive). Best management:

A. Antibiotics

B. Therapeutic thoracentesis

C. Steroids

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — Drain.

  1. Acute breathlessness in COPD with pH 7.30, PaCO₂ 55. Best:

A. Intubate

B. NIV (BiPAP) + bronchodilators + steroids + antibiotics

C. Discharge

D. Steroids only

E. None

Answer: B — NIV bundle.

  1. Acute breathlessness + history of PE + high Wells + positive D-dimer. Next:

A. Discharge

B. CTPA + anticoagulate

C. Wait

D. Steroids

E. None

Answer: B — CTPA + anticoag.

  1. Tension pneumothorax signs include all EXCEPT:

A. Unilateral hyperresonance

B. ↓Breath sounds

C. Tracheal deviation

D. Equal chest expansion

E. Shock

Answer: D — Asymmetric expansion.

  1. The most common cause of sudden death in asthma:

A. Bronchospasm

B. Silent chest / inadequate ventilation

C. Pneumothorax

D. None

E. MI

Answer: B — Silent chest.

  1. A patient with asthma becomes drowsy with normal PaCO₂. Best:

A. Wait

B. Intubate (impending respiratory failure)

C. Antibiotics

D. Steroids

E. None

Answer: B — Intubate.

10. Flashcards

  • Q: Tension PTX first Rx?

A: Needle decompression (2nd ICS, midclavicular).

  • Q: PE sudden + pleuritic?

A: Wells + D-dimer + CTPA.

  • Q: APO first Rx?

A: O₂ + IV furosemide + GTN + NIV.

  • Q: Silent chest?

A: Critical airflow obstruction in asthma.

  • Q: Anaphylaxis first Rx?

A: IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (1:1000).

  • Q: Position?

A: Sitting up.

  • Q: O₂ target?

A: 94–98% (88–92% if hypercapnic risk).

  • Q: COPD exacerbation pH 7.30?

A: NIV + bronchodilators + steroids + antibiotics.

  • Q: Tension PTX CXR wait?

A: No — clinical diagnosis, decompress.

  • Q: Silent chest meaning?

A: Critical airflow obstruction.

11. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

SBAs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. D 9. B 10. B

12. Summary

Acute breathlessness: ABCs + O₂ + ABG + ECG + CXR. Life-threatening: tension PTX (needle decompression), massive PE (thrombolysis), severe asthma (silent chest → intubate), APO (diuretics + NIV), anaphylaxis (IM adrenaline). Position: sitting up. O₂ target 94–98%.

  • Parent Topic Group: [[../Symptom Approach to Respiratory Disease/Acute respiratory presentations|Acute respiratory presentations]]
  • Chapter Map: [[../Davidson Chapter 17 - Respiratory Medicine Hierarchy|Respiratory Medicine Hierarchy]]
  • Chapter MOC: [[../Respiratory MOC|Respiratory MOC]]

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 17.2.04 Chronic Cough And Sputum Approach
[!important]
Chronic cough (≥8 weeks) requires systematic evaluation. The most common causes are asthma, upper airway cough syndrome (UACS/post-nasal drip), GERD, ACE inhibitors, and smoking. Initial workup: history, exam, CXR, spirometry, empirical trials. Most cases resolve with targeted treatment.

Related: [[Asthma]], [[Upper airway obstruction and stridor]], [[Hemoptysis]], [[Pneumonia]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: Chronic cough = think upper airway, asthma, GERD, ACE-i, smoking. Most common cause: UACS (post-nasal drip). Stop ACE-inhibitor in any chronic cough. Trial of PPI for GERD-related cough. CXR + spirometry in all.

1. Definition

Chronic cough = cough lasting >8 weeks.

Subacute

  • 3–8 weeks
  • Often post-infectious

Acute

  • <3 weeks
  • Usually viral, self-limiting

2. Aetiology

Most common causes (90% of cases)

CauseNotes
Upper airway cough syndrome (UACS) / Post-nasal dripAllergic, non-allergic, chronic sinusitis
Asthma (cough-variant)Variable; may be only symptom
GERDLaryngopharyngeal reflux, often silent
ACE inhibitorsWithin hours-weeks of starting; resolve after stopping (weeks)
SmokingChronic irritation

Other causes

  • COPD (chronic bronchitis)
  • Bronchiectasis
  • Lung cancer (must exclude)
  • Interstitial lung disease (ILD)
  • TB
  • Pertussis (post-infectious cough)
  • Eosinophilic bronchitis
  • Bronchiolitis
  • Laryngeal dysfunction (VCD)
  • Post-viral cough
  • Mediastinal mass
  • Pleural disease
  • Cardiac (heart failure, pericardial)
  • Somatic cough syndrome (psychogenic)
  • Foreign body (especially in children)

3. Pathophysiology

Cough reflex

  • Afferents: vagus (larynx, trachea, bronchi, oesophagus, ear)
  • Centre: brainstem
  • Effector: diaphragm, intercostals, larynx
  • Sensory receptors: rapidly adapting (RARs), C-fibres (TRPV1, TRPA1)
  • Modulated by cortical input

Hypersensitivity

  • After viral infection, allergens, irritants
  • ↑Cough reflex sensitivity (upregulation of receptors)

4. Clinical Features

History

  • Duration, pattern (day/night, with meals, position)
  • Triggers (allergens, smoke, food, cold, talking, laughing)
  • Sputum (purulent, blood, post-nasal)
  • Associated symptoms (wheeze, dyspnoea, heartburn, nasal symptoms, throat clearing)
  • Drugs (ACE-i, beta-blockers, NSAIDs)
  • Smoking
  • Occupation, hobbies
  • Cardiac/respiratory history
  • GERD symptoms (heartburn, sour taste, worse lying)

Examination

  • Upper airway: rhinitis, polyps, post-nasal drip
  • Lungs: wheeze, crackles, monophonic wheeze (focal)
  • Cardiac: heart failure signs
  • Ear: foreign body, wax (Arnold's reflex)

5. Diagnosis

Investigations

TestPurpose
CXRExclude mass, infection, ILD, heart failure
Spirometry + BD reversibilityAsthma (often normal between episodes)
FeNOEosinophilic airway disease
CT chestIf CXR abnormal or high suspicion
SputumCytology, AFB, culture
BronchoscopyIf mass suspected, atypical, no response to trials
CT sinusesSinusitis, UACS
24-hr pH/impedanceGERD (atypical)
Methacholine challengeAsthma (if normal spirometry)

Empirical treatment trials

  • Stop ACE-inhibitor (if applicable) — 4 weeks
  • Antihistamine + decongestant (UACS trial) — 2–4 weeks
  • Inhaled corticosteroid (asthma/cough-variant) — 2–4 weeks
  • PPI (GERD trial) — 8–12 weeks (double dose AM + PM)
  • Stop smoking

6. Management

Treat underlying cause

  • UACS: intranasal corticosteroids + antihistamines +/− saline irrigation
  • Asthma: ICS ± LABA; LTRA
  • GERD: PPI, lifestyle (weight loss, head of bed elevated, avoid late meals)
  • ACE-i: stop, switch to ARB
  • Smoking: cessation
  • Pertussis: macrolide (if acute)
  • Eosinophilic bronchitis: ICS
  • Lung cancer: oncology referral
  • Vocal cord dysfunction: speech therapy

Symptomatic treatment

  • Antitussives (if cause untreatable):
  • Dextromethorphan, codeine, pholcodine
  • Morphine (severe chronic cough, palliative)
  • Mucolytics (guaifenesin)
  • Speech therapy (chronic refractory)
  • Gabapentin (neuropathic cough)
  • Local anaesthetics (nebulised lidocaine for intractable)

7. Red flags

  • Haemoptysis
  • Weight loss
  • Smoking history
  • Dyspnoea
  • Abnormal CXR
  • Hoarseness (laryngeal)
  • Recurrent pneumonia

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
Definition>8 weeks
Most commonUACS (post-nasal drip)
Other commonAsthma, GERD, ACE-i, smoking
First stepsStop ACE-i, CXR, spirometry
UACS RxAntihistamine + INCS
Asthma RxICS
GERD RxPPI (8–12 weeks) + lifestyle
Red flagCXR abnormality, haemoptysis
Trial sequence2–4 weeks for each

9. MCQs (10)

  1. Most common cause of chronic cough:

A. Asthma

B. Upper airway cough syndrome (UACS / post-nasal drip)

C. GERD

D. ACE-i

E. None

Answer: B — UACS.

  1. Definition of chronic cough:

A. >3 weeks

B. >8 weeks

C. >12 weeks

D. >6 months

E. None

Answer: B — >8 weeks.

  1. First-line investigation in chronic cough:

A. CT chest

B. CXR + spirometry

C. Bronchoscopy

D. MRI

E. None

Answer: B — CXR + spirometry.

  1. The most common drug causing chronic cough is:

A. Aspirin

B. ACE inhibitor

C. Beta-blocker

D. Statin

E. None

Answer: B — ACE-i.

  1. Treatment of UACS-related cough:

A. Antihistamine + intranasal corticosteroid

B. Antibiotics

C. Steroids systemic

D. Surgery

E. None

Answer: A — Antihist + INCS.

  1. The trial duration for empirical PPI in chronic cough:

A. 1 week

B. 4 weeks

C. 8–12 weeks (double dose)

D. 6 months

E. None

Answer: C — 8–12 weeks.

  1. GERD is associated with cough via:

A. Aspiration only

B. Laryngopharyngeal reflux, vagal reflex

C. Allergy

D. None

E. Smoking

Answer: B — LPR + vagal reflex.

  1. A patient with chronic cough and normal CXR/spirometry. Next:

A. Discharge

B. Trial empirical Rx (UACS, asthma, GERD)

C. Wait

D. Antibiotics

E. None

Answer: B — Empirical trials.

  1. The most useful diagnostic for asthma in chronic cough:

A. CXR

B. Spirometry + BD reversibility (or methacholine challenge)

C. CT

D. ABG

E. None

Answer: B — Spirometry + reversibility.

  1. The least likely cause of chronic cough is:

A. UACS

B. Asthma

C. GERD

D. ACE-i

E. Bacterial pneumonia (acute, not chronic)

Answer: E — Acute, not chronic.

10. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 60-year-old on lisinopril has cough 3 months. First:

A. Switch to ARB

B. Stop ACE-i → 4-week trial

C. Antibiotics

D. Steroids

E. None

Answer: B — Stop ACE-i.

  1. A 50-year-old with cough, post-nasal drip, normal CXR. Best:

A. INCS + antihistamine trial

B. Antibiotics

C. Steroids

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: A — UACS Rx.

  1. A 40-year-old with chronic cough, normal CXR, no improvement with UACS trial. Next:

A. Steroids

B. Spirometry + trial of ICS (asthma) or PPI (GERD)

C. Antibiotics

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — Asthma or GERD trial.

  1. The role of methacholine challenge in chronic cough:

A. Diagnosis of COPD

B. Exclusion of asthma (negative rules out)

C. None

D. Treatment

E. None

Answer: B — Exclude asthma.

  1. Speech therapy is useful in:

A. Pneumonia

B. Chronic refractory cough / vocal cord dysfunction

C. Asthma

D. PE

E. None

Answer: B — Chronic cough/VCD.

  1. Cough-variant asthma presents with:

A. Wheeze

B. Cough (often only symptom)

C. Sputum

D. None

E. Dyspnoea

Answer: B — Cough only.

  1. The first-line for cough-variant asthma:

A. SABA

B. ICS

C. Steroids oral

D. Theophylline

E. None

Answer: B — ICS.

  1. A patient has chronic cough and CT shows a 2 cm lung nodule. Most likely:

A. Asthma

B. Lung cancer (needs workup)

C. COPD

D. None

E. GERD

Answer: B — Lung cancer.

  1. The most effective non-pharmacological treatment for chronic cough:

A. Rest

B. Speech therapy / cough suppression techniques

C. Surgery

D. None

E. Exercise

Answer: B — Speech therapy.

  1. Gabapentin is used in chronic cough for:

A. Anxiety

B. Neuropathic cough (capsaicin-sensitive)

C. None

D. Asthma

E. Pain only

Answer: B — Neuropathic cough.

11. Flashcards

  • Q: Chronic cough definition?

A: >8 weeks.

  • Q: Most common cause?

A: UACS (post-nasal drip).

  • Q: First-line investigation?

A: CXR + spirometry.

  • Q: First drug to stop?

A: ACE inhibitor.

  • Q: UACS Rx?

A: INCS + antihistamine.

  • Q: GERD cough Rx?

A: PPI 8–12 weeks (double dose).

  • Q: Asthma cough Rx?

A: ICS.

  • Q: Red flag?

A: CXR abnormal, haemoptysis, weight loss.

  • Q: Cough-variant asthma?

A: Cough as only symptom.

  • Q: Gabapentin in cough?

A: Neuropathic cough.

12. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. A 6. C 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. E

SBAs

  1. B 2. A 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

13. Summary

Chronic cough >8 weeks. Most common: UACS (post-nasal drip). Other: asthma, GERD, ACE-i, smoking. First step: stop ACE-i, CXR + spirometry. Empirical trials: UACS (INCS + antihist), asthma (ICS), GERD (PPI 8–12 weeks). Red flag: CXR abnormal, haemoptysis, weight loss → workup for cancer.

▸ 17.2.05 Clubbing Cyanosis And Respiratory Distress Clues
[!important]
Clubbing is a key bedside sign with a specific differential (suppurative lung disease, malignancy, cyanotic heart disease, idiopathic). Cyanosis distinguishes central (respiratory/cardiac) from peripheral causes. Recognition of respiratory distress (use of accessory muscles, paradoxical breathing, tracheal tug) is vital for severity assessment.

Related: [[Lung Cancer]], [[Interstitial Lung Disease]], [[Bronchiectasis]], [[Acute severe breathlessness approach]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: Clubbing + chronic lung disease = think suppurative (bronchiectasis, CF, abscess) or malignancy (lung cancer, mesothelioma). Central cyanosis = hypoxaemia (respiratory/cardiac). Peripheral cyanosis = sluggish circulation (cold, shock, RVF). Respiratory distress signs (use of accessory muscles, paradoxical breathing, stridor) = severity + need for intervention.

1. Clubbing (Digital Clubbing)

Definition

Bulbous enlargement of distal phalanges (fingers/toes) with loss of nail-fold angle (Lovibond's angle).

Pathophysiology

  • Megakaryocytes and platelet clumps bypass pulmonary filter (in right-to-left shunt or chronic lung disease) → lodge in distal capillaries
  • Release PDGF and VEGF → connective tissue proliferation
  • Chronic hypoxaemia, inflammation contribute

Causes

CategoryExamples
RespiratoryBronchiectasis, CF, lung abscess, empyema, lung cancer, mesothelioma, ILD (esp. asbestosis)
CardiacCyanotic CHD, IE, myxoma
GICirrhosis, IBD, coeliac
EndocrineThyrotoxicosis
OtherIdiopathic (5–10%), hereditary, pregnancy
NOT clubbingCOPD alone, sarcoidosis, asthma (usually)

Examination

  • Schamroth window test: loss of diamond-shaped window when opposing nails are apposed
  • Phalangeal depth ratio (PDR) >1 (DTP/DIP)
  • Distal phalangeal fibrous thickening

Grades

GradeDescription
1↑Fluctuation, softening of nail bed
2Loss of nail-fold angle (Lovibond)
3↑AP and lateral curvature of nail
4Drumstick terminal phalanx

Clinical significance

  • Bilateral (most): respiratory, cardiac, GI, idiopathic
  • Unilateral: local vascular (AV fistula, aneurysm)
  • Painful (hypertrophic osteoarthropathy): lung cancer, mesothelioma
  • Symmetric + rapid: lung cancer, abscess

2. Cyanosis

Definition

Bluish discolouration of skin/mucosae from desaturated Hb >5 g/dL (capillary level).

Central cyanosis

  • Tongue, lips, oral mucosa, conjunctiva
  • Causes:
  • Respiratory: hypoxaemia (pneumonia, PE, COPD, PTX, OSA, foreign body)
  • Cardiac: cyanotic CHD, pulmonary hypertension
  • Haemoglobin: methaemoglobinaemia, sulphaemoglobinaemia

Peripheral cyanosis

  • Fingers, toes, earlobes, nose (cold exposure)
  • Causes: cold, shock, heart failure, Raynaud's, peripheral vascular disease
  • Normal SpO₂

Differential cyanosis

  • Upper body cyanotic, lower body pink = PDA with R→L shunt (Eisenmenger)
  • Reverse differential (lower body cyanotic) = PDA with R→L above (rare)

Methaemoglobinaemia

  • Chocolate-brown blood (does not change with O₂)
  • Causes: dapsone, benzocaine, nitrites
  • Treatment: methylene blue (1–2 mg/kg IV)
  • SpO₂ reads ~85% regardless of actual saturation

3. Respiratory Distress

Signs of severity

SignSignificance
Tachypnoea (>25/min)Most sensitive
TachycardiaCompensation
Use of accessory muscles (SCM, scalenes)↑WOB
Intercostal/subcostal recession↑WOB
Tracheal tug↑WOB
Paradoxical breathing (abdominal paradox)Diaphragmatic fatigue
Nasal flaringSevere (esp. children)
Pursed-lip breathingCOPD (auto-PEEP)
Tripoding (leaning forward)Severe obstructive
CyanosisHypoxaemia
Altered mental statusHypoxaemia, hypercapnia
DiaphoresisSevere, ↑WOB

Pattern recognition

  • Obstructive (asthma, COPD): prolonged expiration, wheeze, accessory muscle use, pursed-lip, tripoding
  • Restrictive (ILD): rapid shallow breathing, dry cough
  • Central airway obstruction (foreign body, tumour): stridor, monophonic wheeze

4. Cyanotic Congenital Heart Disease (5 Ts)

  • Tetralogy of Fallot
  • Transposition of great arteries
  • Tricuspid atresia
  • Truncus arteriosus
  • Total anomalous pulmonary venous return

5. Examination Approach

Quick respiratory assessment

  1. General: distress, colour, position, conscious level
  2. Hands: clubbing, cyanosis, CO₂ flap, nicotine, tar
  3. Face/neck: JVP, trachea, stridor, lymph nodes
  4. Chest: shape, movement, percussion, auscultation
  5. Cardiac: apex, murmurs, S3, S4, pericardial rub
  6. Abdomen: hepatomegaly, ascites (right HF)
  7. Limbs: oedema, calf swelling, clubbing

6. Management of Distress

General

  • Position: sitting up
  • O₂: target 94–98% (88–92% if hypercapnic)
  • Reassure (anxiety worsens)
  • Specific treatment by cause
  • Escalate (HDU/ITU) if severe

Cause-specific

Distress causeRx
Severe asthmaSABA + ipratropium + IV hydrocortisone + IV MgSO₄
AECOPDControlled O₂ + SABA + steroid + NIV
APOO₂ + IV furosemide + GTN + NIV
PneumoniaAntibiotics + O₂ + fluids
PEO₂ + anticoagulation
PTXChest drain

7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
ClubbingLung cancer, bronchiectasis, CF, abscess, mesothelioma
Not clubbingAsthma, sarcoid (usually)
Central cyanosisHypoxaemia (respiratory/cardiac)
Peripheral cyanosisCold, shock (normal SpO₂)
MethaemoglobinaemiaChocolate blood, methylene blue
TachypnoeaMost sensitive distress sign
Paradoxical breathingDiaphragmatic fatigue
PositionSitting up, leaning forward

8. MCQs (10)

  1. The most common respiratory cause of clubbing is:

A. COPD

B. Bronchiectasis (suppurative lung disease)

C. Asthma

D. Sarcoidosis

E. None

Answer: B — Bronchiectasis.

  1. COPD alone usually:

A. Causes clubbing

B. Does not cause clubbing (look for another cause)

C. Always

D. None

E. Lung cancer

Answer: B — Look for other cause.

  1. The most sensitive sign of respiratory distress is:

A. Cyanosis

B. Tachypnoea (RR >25)

C. Wheeze

D. None

E. Confusion

Answer: B — Tachypnoea.

  1. Paradoxical breathing (abdominal paradox) is a sign of:

A. Asthma

B. Diaphragmatic fatigue

C. COPD

D. Pneumonia

E. None

Answer: B — Diaphragm fatigue.

  1. Central cyanosis is best detected at:

A. Fingers

B. Tongue, lips, oral mucosa

C. Earlobes

D. None

E. Toes

Answer: B — Tongue, lips, mucosa.

  1. Peripheral cyanosis is caused by:

A. Lung disease

B. Sluggish circulation (cold, shock)

C. Heart disease

D. None

E. Cyanotic CHD

Answer: B — Sluggish circulation.

  1. Methaemoglobinaemia is treated with:

A. O₂

B. Methylene blue

C. Naloxone

D. Steroids

E. None

Answer: B — Methylene blue.

  1. Pursed-lip breathing in COPD is for:

A. Speech

B. Maintaining airway patency (auto-PEEP)

C. Sleep

D. None

E. Exercise

Answer: B — Airway patency.

  1. The 5 cyanotic congenital heart diseases are the 5 Ts:

A. Tetralogy, Transposition, Tricuspid atresia, Truncus, TAPVR

B. ASD, VSD, PDA, AV canal, coarctation

C. None

D. ToF, TGA, MVP, MR, AR

E. None

Answer: A — 5 Ts.

  1. Tracheal tug is a sign of:

A. Pneumonia

B. Severe airflow obstruction (↑WOB)

C. Heart failure

D. None

E. Anaemia

Answer: B — Severe obstruction.

9. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 50-year-old with lung cancer and painful swollen joints. Diagnosis:

A. RA

B. Hypertrophic osteoarthropathy

C. None

D. Gout

E. Infection

Answer: B — HPOA (paraneoplastic).

  1. Cyanotic CHD with differential cyanosis (upper cyanotic, lower pink) suggests:

A. Tetralogy of Fallot

B. PDA with Eisenmenger (R→L shunt)

C. TGA

D. None

E. ASD

Answer: B — PDA with Eisenmenger.

  1. The first sign of respiratory failure in a child with asthma:

A. Wheeze

B. Silent chest (worsening)

C. Cyanosis

D. None

E. Tachycardia

Answer: B — Silent chest.

  1. Methaemoglobinaemia shows:

A. Cyanosis improved with O₂

B. Chocolate-brown blood, no response to O₂

C. Normal colour

D. None

E. Pink

Answer: B — Chocolate blood.

  1. A smoker with finger clubbing. Most likely:

A. COPD alone

B. Lung cancer (until proven otherwise)

C. Asthma

D. None

E. Bronchitis

Answer: B — Lung cancer.

  1. The Schamroth test is positive in:

A. Normal

B. Clubbing (loss of nail-fold angle)

C. Cyanosis

D. None

E. Asthma

Answer: B — Loss of diamond.

  1. Cyanosis in the lower extremities only (reverse differential) is seen in:

A. PDA

B. TGA with PDA, pulmonary hypertension, or coarctation

C. ToF

D. None

E. VSD

Answer: B — TGA with coarctation or PDA.

  1. The most common cause of painful clubbing:

A. Idiopathic

B. Lung cancer (hypertrophic osteoarthropathy)

C. Bronchiectasis

D. None

E. CF

Answer: B — Lung cancer.

  1. A 60-year-old with cyanosis and normal SpO₂ (probe on finger). Cause:

A. Hypoxemia

B. Peripheral cyanosis (cold, shock) or methaemoglobinaemia

C. None

D. Heart

E. Lung

Answer: B — Peripheral or methaemoglobin.

  1. The earliest sign of acute respiratory failure is:

A. Cyanosis

B. Tachypnoea

C. Confusion

D. None

E. Wheeze

Answer: B — Tachypnoea.

10. Flashcards

  • Q: Most common respiratory cause of clubbing?

A: Bronchiectasis.

  • Q: Clubbing + smoker?

A: Lung cancer (until proven otherwise).

  • Q: Central cyanosis location?

A: Tongue, lips, mucosa.

  • Q: Peripheral cyanosis?

A: Fingers/toes, cold, sluggish circulation.

  • Q: Methaemoglobinaemia?

A: Chocolate blood, methylene blue.

  • Q: Tachypnoea threshold?

A: RR >25.

  • Q: Paradoxical breathing?

A: Diaphragmatic fatigue.

  • Q: 5 cyanotic CHDs?

A: ToF, TGA, TA, truncus, TAPVR.

  • Q: Pursed-lip breathing?

A: Auto-PEEP in COPD.

  • Q: Most sensitive distress sign?

A: Tachypnoea.

11. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. A 10. B

SBAs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

12. Summary

Clubbing: most common respiratory cause = bronchiectasis; smoker with clubbing = lung cancer. Cyanosis: central (tongue, mucosa) = hypoxaemia; peripheral (fingers) = sluggish circulation. Methaemoglobinaemia: chocolate blood, methylene blue. Distress: tachypnoea most sensitive; paradoxical breathing = diaphragmatic fatigue. Treat underlying cause.

Topic Overview

1. Disease-Level Notes
2. Expansion Targets

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 17.2.07 Fever With Cough And Consolidation Approach
[!important]
Fever + cough + consolidation = pneumonia until proven otherwise. Severity assessment (CURB-65, PSI) guides admission decisions. Common pathogens: S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, atypicals (Mycoplasma, Legionella, Chlamydia). Always consider TB in chronic/progressive presentations.

Related: [[Pneumonia]], [[Tuberculosis]], [[ABG Interpretation]], [[Oxygen Therapy and NIV]], [[Respiratory Failure]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: CURB-65: Confusion, Urea >7, RR ≥30, BP <90/60, Age ≥65. Score 0–1: outpatient; 2: admit; 3+: severe (consider ITU). Empirical antibiotics: amoxicillin (mild), amoxicillin + macrolide (moderate), co-amoxiclav + macrolide (severe). Always think TB in non-resolving.

1. Definition

Pneumonia = infection of the lung parenchyma with consolidation on imaging.

2. Aetiology

By setting

SettingCommon pathogens
Community-acquired (CAP)S. pneumoniae (~50%), H. influenzae, atypicals (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, Legionella)
AspirationAnaerobes, mixed flora
Hospital-acquired (HAP)Pseudomonas, Klebsiella, Staph aureus (esp. MRSA)
Ventilator-associated (VAP)Multi-drug resistant organisms
ImmunocompromisedPJP, fungi, CMV, atypicals

Specific clues

  • Travellers: Legionella (hotels, AC), Mycoplasma (schools)
  • Animal exposure: Chlamydia psittaci (birds), Coxiella (livestock)
  • Alcoholic: Klebsiella, S. pneumoniae, anaerobes
  • Aspiration: anaerobes
  • Post-influenza: Staph aureus (severe)
  • Cystic fibrosis: Pseudomonas, Staph aureus
  • HIV (CD4 <200): PJP
  • Neutropenia: bacteria, fungi (Aspergillus)
  • Travel/sick contacts: TB

3. Clinical Features

Symptoms

  • Fever (often rigors)
  • Cough (productive; rust-coloured sputum in pneumococcus)
  • Dyspnoea
  • Pleuritic chest pain (if pleural involvement)
  • Fatigue, myalgia
  • Confusion (elderly)

Signs

  • Fever, tachycardia, tachypnoea
  • Consolidation signs: dullness, ↓vocal resonance, bronchial breathing, crackles
  • Pleural rub (if pleurisy)
  • Cyanosis (severe)
  • Confusion (hypoxaemia or sepsis)

4. Diagnosis

Investigations

TestPurpose
CXR (PA + lateral)Gold standard — consolidation, complications
Sputum (Gram stain, culture)Pathogen ID (if severe)
Blood culturesIf severe
Urinary antigensS. pneumoniae, Legionella
PCR (respiratory viral panel)Influenza, RSV, SARS-CoV-2
ABGSeverity, hypoxaemia
FBC, U&E, CRP, LFTSeverity, sepsis
SerologyMycoplasma, Chlamydia (paired)
HIV testIf recurrent/atypical
CT chestComplication (abscess, effusion) or non-resolving
Bronchoscopy + BALImmunocompromised, atypical

CXR patterns

PatternCommon cause
Lobar consolidationS. pneumoniae, Klebsiella
Bronchopneumonia (patchy)S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, atypicals
Interstitial/bilateralMycoplasma, viral, PJP, miliary TB
CavitationStaph aureus, Klebsiella, TB, fungi
Pleural effusionComplication, empyema

5. Severity Assessment

CURB-65 (BTS)

  • Confusion (new)
  • Urea >7 mmol/L
  • Respiratory rate ≥30/min
  • Blood pressure (SBP <90 or DBP ≤60)
  • Age ≥65 years
  • Score:
  • 0–1: low risk → outpatient
  • 2: moderate → admit
  • 3–5: high risk → admit, consider ITU (4–5)

PSI (Pneumonia Severity Index)

  • More complex; similar discrimination
  • Use for risk stratification

Other severity markers

  • Hypoxaemia (PaO₂ <60)
  • Acidosis (pH <7.35)
  • Sepsis (qSOFA, SIRS)
  • Multilobar infiltrates
  • Effusion/empyema
  • Bacteraemia

6. Management

Empirical antibiotics

SettingFirst-line
CAP, low severity (CURB 0–1)Amoxicillin 500 mg TDS × 5 days
CAP, moderate (CURB 2)Amoxicillin + macrolide (clarithromycin or azithromycin)
CAP, severe (CURB ≥3)Co-amoxiclav + macrolide (or doxycycline); or levofloxacin
HAP/VAPPiperacillin-tazobactam +/− aminoglycoside/macrolide; consider MRSA cover
AspirationAmoxicillin-clavulanate or clindamycin
Atypical predominantMacrolide (azithromycin) ± β-lactam
LegionellaLevofloxacin or azithromycin (≥7–10 days)

Supportive

  • O₂ (target SpO₂ 94–98%)
  • Hydration (oral if able, IV if not)
  • Analgesia (pleuritic pain: NSAIDs)
  • Antipyretic (paracetamol)
  • Physiotherapy (clearance)
  • Ventilation if respiratory failure (NIV/CPAP/intubation)

Complications

ComplicationManagement
Pleural effusionTap (diagnostic +/− therapeutic)
EmpyemaChest drain + antibiotics + fibrinolytics/VATS
Lung abscessProlonged antibiotics (4–6 weeks)
ARDSLung-protective ventilation, prone, treat cause
Septic shockFluids, vasopressors, antibiotics
DeathSevere CAP, especially elderly, comorbidity

Non-resolving pneumonia

  • Consider: TB, lung cancer, PE, foreign body, empyema, fungal, atypical pathogens
  • CT chest + bronchoscopy
  • Reassess pathogen coverage

7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
Most commonS. pneumoniae
CURB-65Confusion, Urea, RR, BP, Age
CURB 0–1Outpatient, amoxicillin
CURB 2Admit, amoxicillin + macrolide
CURB 3+Severe, co-amoxiclav + macrolide
Red flagConfusion, RR ≥30, BP low, hypoxaemia
AtypicalsMycoplasma, Chlamydia, Legionella
CavitationStaph, Klebsiella, TB, fungi
ComplicationsEffusion, empyema, abscess, ARDS, death
Non-resolvingTB, cancer, PE, fungal

8. MCQs (10)

  1. The most common cause of CAP is:

A. H. influenzae

B. S. pneumoniae

C. Mycoplasma

D. Legionella

E. None

Answer: B — Pneumococcus.

  1. CURB-65 includes all EXCEPT:

A. Confusion

B. Urea

C. RR

D. Albumin

E. Age

Answer: D — Not albumin.

  1. CURB-65 score 0–1:

A. Outpatient, amoxicillin

B. Admit, IV

C. ITU

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: A — Outpatient.

  1. CURB-65 score 3:

A. Outpatient

B. Admit, oral

C. Severe (admit, IV antibiotics, consider ITU)

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: C — Severe.

  1. First-line empirical for severe CAP:

A. Co-amoxiclav + macrolide

B. Amoxicillin alone

C. Azithromycin alone

D. Steroids

E. None

Answer: A — Co-amoxiclav + macrolide.

  1. The most likely cause of post-influenza pneumonia:

A. S. pneumoniae

B. Staph aureus (severe)

C. Mycoplasma

D. None

E. Viral

Answer: B — Staph aureus.

  1. A 60-year-old with pneumonia + cavitation. Most likely:

A. S. pneumoniae

B. Klebsiella or Staph aureus

C. Mycoplasma

D. None

E. Viral

Answer: B — Cavitating.

  1. Non-resolving pneumonia after 6 weeks should prompt workup for:

A. TB, lung cancer, empyema, fungal

B. None

C. Asthma

D. PE

E. None

Answer: A — Workup.

  1. Pleural effusion complicating pneumonia with pH <7.2 requires:

A. Antibiotics only

B. Chest drain

C. Steroids

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — Drain.

  1. The most common pneumonia complication:

A. ARDS

B. Pleural effusion

C. Abscess

D. None

E. Empyema

Answer: B — Effusion.

9. SBA Questions (10)

  1. The CURB-65 component with most prognostic value:

A. Confusion

B. Urea

C. RR

D. BP

E. Age

Answer: A — Confusion.

  1. A 70-year-old with CURB-65 score 4:

A. Outpatient

B. Admit, oral

C. Severe, ITU consideration

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: C — Severe.

  1. The first dose of empirical antibiotic should be given:

A. After CXR

B. As soon as possible (within 4 h of admission)

C. After culture

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — ASAP.

  1. A young adult with CAP and headache, hyponatraemia, hepatic dysfunction. Cause:

A. Pneumococcus

B. Legionella

C. Mycoplasma

D. None

E. Viral

Answer: B — Legionella.

  1. Best diagnostic for S. pneumoniae:

A. Sputum

B. Urinary antigen (sensitivity 80%, specificity >95%)

C. Culture

D. None

E. PCR

Answer: B — Urinary antigen.

  1. Antibiotic duration for uncomplicated CAP:

A. 3 days

B. 5–7 days

C. 14 days

D. 21 days

E. None

Answer: B — 5–7 days.

  1. A patient with pneumonia has pH 7.30, PaO₂ 50, RR 32. Severity:

A. CURB 0

B. CURB ≥3 (severe, consider ITU)

C. CURB 1

D. CURB 2

E. None

Answer: B — Severe.

  1. The first-line antibiotic for Mycoplasma:

A. Amoxicillin

B. Macrolide (azithromycin)

C. Co-amoxiclav

D. None

E. Fluoroquinolone

Answer: B — Macrolide.

  1. A patient with severe CAP and PCV13 + PPSV23 vaccination. Pneumococcal coverage by:

A. No effect

B. Vaccine covers common serotypes

C. None

D. Always fails

E. None

Answer: B — Vaccine covers.

  1. The first sign of improvement in pneumonia:

A. CXR clears

B. Fever resolves, clinical improvement (3–5 days)

C. Sputum clears

D. None

E. ABG normalises

Answer: B — Clinical first.

10. Flashcards

  • Q: Most common CAP pathogen?

A: S. pneumoniae.

  • Q: CURB-65 components?

A: Confusion, Urea, RR, BP, Age.

  • Q: CURB 0–1 management?

A: Outpatient, amoxicillin.

  • Q: CURB 3+ management?

A: Admit, IV, co-amoxiclav + macrolide, consider ITU.

  • Q: Post-influenza pneumonia?

A: Staph aureus.

  • Q: Cavitation?

A: Staph, Klebsiella, TB, fungi.

  • Q: Legionella clues?

A: Hyponatraemia, hepatic dysfunction, GI symptoms.

  • Q: Pneumococcal urinary antigen?

A: Sensitivity 80%, specificity >95%.

  • Q: First-line for atypicals?

A: Macrolide.

  • Q: Non-resolving >6 weeks?

A: CT + bronchoscopy → TB, cancer, fungal.

11. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B 2. D 3. A 4. C 5. A 6. B 7. B 8. A 9. B 10. B

SBAs

  1. A 2. C 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

12. Summary

Fever + cough + consolidation = pneumonia. CURB-65 guides admission. Empirical: amoxicillin (mild); amoxicillin + macrolide (moderate); co-amoxiclav + macrolide (severe). Complications: effusion, empyema, abscess, ARDS, death. Non-resolving → CT + bronchoscopy (TB, cancer, fungal).

Symptoms

4. Diagnosis
Investigations
CXR patterns

CURB-65 (BTS)
PSI (Pneumonia Severity Index)
Other severity markers
6. Management
Empirical antibiotics
Supportive
Complications
Non-resolving pneumonia
7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

▸ 17.2.08 Pleuritic Chest Pain Approach
[!important]
Pleuritic chest pain is sharp, well-localised pain that worsens with inspiration, coughing, and movement. Common causes: PE, pneumothorax, pneumonia (pleurisy), pericarditis, pleural effusion, rib fracture, malignancy. Always exclude PE and PTX in acute pleuritic pain.

Related: [[Pulmonary Embolism]], [[Pneumothorax]], [[Pneumonia]], [[Pleural Effusion]], [[Acute severe breathlessness approach]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: Pleuritic pain + dyspnoea + tachycardia = PE until proven otherwise. Pleuritic pain + unilateral hyperresonance + ↓BS = PTX. Pleuritic + fever + consolidation = pneumonia with pleurisy. Always consider pericarditis (relief on leaning forward, diffuse ST elevation, pericardial rub).

1. Definition

Pleuritic chest pain = sharp, well-localised pain that worsens with inspiration, coughing, and movement due to parietal pleural irritation.

Characteristics

  • Sharp, stabbing
  • Worse with deep inspiration, cough, movement, lying on affected side
  • Better with breath-holding, lying on unaffected side
  • Reproducible on palpation
  • Localised to a specific dermatome

2. Aetiology

Pleural causes (most common)

CauseNotes
Pneumonia with pleurisyFever, cough, consolidation
Pulmonary embolismPleuritic + dyspnoea + tachycardia (small infarct)
PneumothoraxSudden, unilateral, dyspnoea
Pleural effusionDullness, ↓BS
Malignant pleural diseaseMesothelioma, metastatic, weight loss
Pleural infection (empyema)Fever, effusion
Asbestos-related pleurisyBenign, exposure history
Dressler syndromePost-MI, autoimmune
Connective tissue diseaseRA, SLE

Non-pleural causes

CauseNotes
PericarditisRelief leaning forward, pericardial rub, ST elevation
Rib fractureTrauma, focal tenderness, crepitus
MusculoskeletalReproducible, often positional
Chest wall (shingles)Dermatomal, vesicular rash
Costochondritis (Tietze)Reproducible, swelling
Cervical radiculopathyDermatomal, with movement
Aortic dissectionTearing, interscapular, BP differential
MI (atypical)Pressure, central, radiation
PE (also pleuritic)Pleuritic + tachycardia

3. Pathophysiology

Pleural anatomy

  • Visceral pleura: no pain (autonomic innervation)
  • Parietal pleura: rich somatic innervation → pain
  • Diaphragmatic pleura: phrenic nerve (C3–C5) → referred to shoulder

Mechanism of pleuritic pain

  • Inflammation of parietal pleura
  • Stretching during respiration
  • Friction between inflamed surfaces
  • Cough triggers pain

4. Clinical Features

Symptoms

  • Sharp, well-localised pain (often unilateral)
  • Worse with breath, cough, sneeze, movement
  • Relief with shallow breathing, breath-holding, lying on unaffected side
  • May be associated with dyspnoea (especially PE, PTX)
  • ± fever (pneumonia, empyema)
  • ± cough, sputum (pneumonia)
  • ± haemoptysis (PE, cancer)
  • ± pleural rub (audible/feelable)

Signs

  • Pleural rub (creaking, leathery)
  • Focal tenderness (reproducible)
  • Dullness (effusion)
  • ↓Breath sounds (effusion, PTX)
  • Hyperresonance (PTX)
  • Consolidation (pneumonia)
  • Tachycardia, tachypnoea
  • Cyanosis (severe)

5. Diagnosis

Investigations

TestPurpose
CXR (PA + lateral)Pneumothorax, effusion, consolidation
ECGPericarditis, MI, RV strain (PE)
ABGHypoxaemia, A-a gradient (PE)
D-dimer (Wells)PE rule-out
TroponinMI, RV strain
CTPASuspected PE
Pleural USEffusion (faster than CXR)
Pleural tapEffusion → Light's criteria
CT chestLung cancer, mesothelioma, PE

Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Pleuritic chest pain
↓
Examination + CXR + ECG
↓
Unilateral hyperresonance + ↓BS?
Consolidation + fever?
Dullness + ↓BS?
Tachycardia + dyspnoea + normal CXR?
Pericardial rub + diffuse ST elevation?
Rib tenderness + trauma?
Musculoskeletal, shingles, etc.
↓
Pneumothorax → chest drain
Pneumonia → antibiotics
Pleural effusion → tap + Light's
PE → Wells + D-dimer → CTPA
Pericarditis → ECG + echo + Rx
Rib fracture → CXR + analgesia

6. Management

General

  • Treat underlying cause
  • Analgesia: paracetamol, NSAIDs (avoid masking)
  • Position: sitting up, lean forward (pericarditis)
  • O₂ if hypoxaemic
  • Cough suppression (if distressing)

Specific

CauseRx
PneumothoraxChest drain (or aspiration if small)
PEAnticoagulation ± thrombolysis
Pneumonia with pleurisyAntibiotics + analgesia
Pleural effusionTreat cause; therapeutic tap if large
MalignantOncology, palliative, drain + pleurodesis
PericarditisNSAIDs + colchicine
Rib fractureAnalgesia (NSAIDs, opioids, nerve block), chest physiotherapy
MusculoskeletalAnalgesia, heat, physiotherapy
ShinglesAntivirals (acyclovir) + analgesia

7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
DefinitionSharp, worse with inspiration/cough/movement
Most concerningPE, PTX
Pneumonia + pleurisyCommon
PericarditisRelief leaning forward, pericardial rub
Rib fractureTrauma, focal tenderness
First stepsCXR + ECG + Wells + ABG
Pleural rubAudible leathery sound
TreatmentCause + analgesia (NSAIDs)

8. MCQs (10)

  1. The most common cause of pleuritic chest pain is:

A. PE

B. Pneumonia with pleurisy

C. PTX

D. None

E. MI

Answer: B — Pneumonia.

  1. Pleuritic pain is sharp and:

A. Worse at rest

B. Worse with inspiration/cough/movement

C. Constant

D. Dull

E. None

Answer: B — Worse with movement.

  1. The most life-threatening cause of pleuritic pain:

A. Pericarditis

B. PE

C. Pneumonia

D. Musculoskeletal

E. None

Answer: B — PE.

  1. A pleural rub sounds like:

A. Snoring

B. Leathery/creaking

C. Wheeze

D. None

E. Clicking

Answer: B — Leathery/creaking.

  1. The most appropriate first investigation in pleuritic pain:

A. CT

B. CXR + ECG

C. ABG

D. None

E. Echo

Answer: B — CXR + ECG.

  1. Pericarditis pain is relieved by:

A. Lying flat

B. Sitting up and leaning forward

C. Exercise

D. None

E. Eating

Answer: B — Sitting/leaning forward.

  1. Pericarditis ECG shows:

A. ST depression

B. Diffuse ST elevation (concave) + PR depression

C. Q waves

D. Tall R V1

E. None

Answer: B — Diffuse ST elevation.

  1. Rib fracture management includes:

A. Steroids

B. Analgesia (NSAIDs, opioids, nerve block) + chest physiotherapy

C. Antibiotics

D. Surgery

E. None

Answer: B — Analgesia + physio.

  1. The most important step in pleuritic chest pain is to:

A. Prescribe NSAIDs

B. Exclude PE and PTX

C. X-ray only

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — Exclude serious.

  1. Pleuritic pain in a patient with cancer suggests:

A. Muscle strain

B. Malignant pleural disease (mesothelioma, metastasis)

C. None

D. PE

E. Pneumonia

Answer: B — Malignant.

9. SBA Questions (10)

  1. The first step in acute pleuritic chest pain is:

A. NSAIDs

B. CXR + ECG + Wells for PE

C. CT

D. Discharge

E. None

Answer: B — Imaging + ECG.

  1. A 30-year-old with sudden pleuritic pain, unilateral hyperresonance. Diagnosis:

A. PE

B. Pneumothorax

C. Pneumonia

D. None

E. Pericarditis

Answer: B — PTX.

  1. Pleuritic pain with fever and consolidation. Diagnosis:

A. PE

B. Pneumonia with pleurisy

C. PTX

D. None

E. Pericarditis

Answer: B — Pneumonia.

  1. The treatment of pericarditis is:

A. NSAIDs + colchicine

B. Steroids alone

C. Antibiotics

D. Surgery

E. None

Answer: A — NSAIDs + colchicine.

  1. A 50-year-old with known cancer has new pleuritic pain. Most likely:

A. Pneumonia

B. Malignant pleural disease

C. None

D. PE

E. Pericarditis

Answer: B — Malignant.

  1. The most important bedside sign of pleurisy is:

A. Cyanosis

B. Pleural rub

C. Tachycardia

D. None

E. Wheeze

Answer: B — Rub.

  1. Pleuritic pain is described as:

A. Dull, constant

B. Sharp, worse with movement

C. Burning

D. None

E. Cramping

Answer: B — Sharp, worse with movement.

  1. Diaphragmatic pleural pain is referred to:

A. Chest

B. Shoulder (C3–C5 via phrenic)

C. Back

D. None

E. Abdomen

Answer: B — Shoulder.

  1. Pleuritic pain from PE typically arises from:

A. Main pulmonary artery

B. Peripheral infarction of small subpleural vessels

C. Pleural effusion

D. None

E. Heart

Answer: B — Peripheral infarction.

  1. Pleuritic pain management includes:

A. Steroids

B. NSAIDs (ibuprofen) + treat cause

C. Antibiotics

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — NSAIDs + cause.

10. Flashcards

  • Q: Pleuritic pain definition?

A: Sharp, worse with inspiration/cough/movement.

  • Q: Most common cause?

A: Pneumonia with pleurisy.

  • Q: Most life-threatening?

A: PE.

  • Q: First step?

A: CXR + ECG + Wells.

  • Q: Pericarditis relief?

A: Sitting up + leaning forward.

  • Q: Pericarditis ECG?

A: Diffuse ST elevation + PR depression.

  • Q: Pleural rub sound?

A: Leathery/creaking.

  • Q: Diaphragmatic pleurisy referred to?

A: Shoulder (C3–C5).

  • Q: Rib fracture Rx?

A: Analgesia + physio.

  • Q: Malignant pleuritic pain?

A: Mesothelioma, metastasis.

11. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

SBAs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. A 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

12. Summary

Pleuritic chest pain = sharp, worse with inspiration/cough/movement. Most common: pneumonia. Most concerning: PE, PTX. First step: CXR + ECG + Wells for PE. Pericarditis: relief leaning forward, diffuse ST elevation. Treatment: cause + analgesia (NSAIDs).

13. Local Navigation

  • Parent Heading: [[../Symptom Approach to Respiratory Disease|Symptom Approach to Respiratory Disease]]
  • Parent Topic Group: [[../Symptom Approach to Respiratory Disease/Common respiratory presentations|Common respiratory presentations]]
  • Chapter Map: [[../Davidson Chapter 17 - Respiratory Medicine Hierarchy|Respiratory Medicine Hierarchy]]
  • Chapter MOC: [[../Respiratory MOC|Respiratory MOC]]
  • Related: [[Pulmonary Embolism]] · [[Pneumothorax]] · [[Pneumonia]] · [[Pleural Effusion]]
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 17.2.09 Wheeze And Stridor Approach
[!important]
Wheeze = continuous musical sound from lower airway obstruction (asthma, COPD). Stridor = inspiratory monophonic sound from upper airway obstruction (croup, foreign body, anaphylaxis, tumour). Stridor is a medical emergency — never ignore it.

Related: [[Asthma]], [[COPD]], [[Foreign body aspiration in adults]], [[Vocal cord dysfunction and inducible laryngeal obstruction|VCD]], [[Upper airway obstruction and stridor]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: Inspiratory stridor = extrathoracic (laryngeal, subglottic). Expiratory wheeze = intrathoracic (bronchi). Biphasic = fixed obstruction (subglottic stenosis, tracheal tumour). Stridor + anaphylaxis = IM adrenaline. Sudden stridor in child = foreign body.

1. Definition

  • Wheeze: continuous, high-pitched musical sound from lower airway obstruction (airway narrowing, secretions)
  • Stridor: monophonic harsh sound from upper airway obstruction (larynx, trachea)
  • Inspiratory: extrathoracic (above thoracic inlet)
  • Expiratory: intrathoracic (below thoracic inlet)
  • Biphasic: fixed or subglottic

2. Aetiology

Wheeze (lower airway)

CauseNotes
AsthmaExpiratory, polyphonic, variable
COPDExpiratory, chronic, smoking
BronchiolitisChildren, viral
BronchiectasisChronic, often localised
Tumour (endobronchial)Fixed monophonic wheeze (focal)
Heart failure (cardiac asthma)Bilateral
AnaphylaxisAcute, with stridor + hypotension
Vocal cord dysfunction (VCD)Inspiratory, often mimics asthma

Stridor (upper airway)

CauseNotes
Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis)Children, barking cough, viral
EpiglottitisChildren, toxic, drooling, sitting up
Foreign bodySudden, often children, unilateral wheeze
AnaphylaxisAcute, with urticaria, hypotension
Laryngeal oedemaPost-extubation, ACE-i, hereditary angioedema
Laryngeal tumourSmoker, progressive hoarseness
Bilateral vocal cord paralysisPost-thyroidectomy, neurological
Subglottic stenosisPost-intubation
Tracheal stenosisPost-tracheostomy
LaryngomalaciaInfant, noisy breathing
Retropharyngeal abscessChildren, fever, drooling
DiphtheriaUnvaccinated, membrane

3. Pathophysiology

Airway narrowing

  • Air flow through narrowed airway → turbulence → sound
  • Stridor = ≥50% airway narrowing (often critical)
  • Inspiratory stridor = extrathoracic (larynx, subglottic) — narrows on inspiration (negative pressure)
  • Expiratory stridor = intrathoracic (trachea, bronchi) — narrows on expiration (positive pressure)
  • Biphasic = fixed (cartilage) or subglottic

Acute severe obstruction

  • Complete obstruction → stridor → aphonia → respiratory arrest
  • Time-critical (minutes to hours)

4. Clinical Features

Symptoms

  • Noisy breathing (wheeze, stridor)
  • Dyspnoea
  • Stridor (inspiratory, expiratory, biphasic)
  • Hoarseness (laryngeal)
  • Cough (croupy, barking, brassy)
  • Drooling, dysphagia (epiglottitis, abscess)
  • Anaphylaxis symptoms (urticaria, angioedema)

Signs

  • Stridor (auscultate over neck/chest)
  • Wheeze (auscultate chest)
  • Use of accessory muscles
  • Tracheal tug, intercostal recession
  • Cyanosis (severe)
  • Tachycardia, tachypnoea
  • Toxic appearance (epiglottitis, anaphylaxis)
  • Drooling, tripod position (epiglottitis)
  • Stridor louder when supine (VCD, laryngomalacia)

5. Diagnosis

Investigations

TestPurpose
ABGHypoxaemia, hypercapnia (severe)
CXR (inspiration + expiration)Foreign body, mass, mediastinal shift, hyperinflation
Neck X-ray (AP)Subglottic narrowing (steeple sign) in croup
CT neck/chestMass, abscess, foreign body
BronchoscopyForeign body, tumour, biopsy
LaryngoscopyEpiglottitis, vocal cords
Spirometry (flow-volume loop)Extrathoracic vs intrathoracic obstruction
Allergy testsAnaphylaxis triggers
FEES (fibreoptic endoscopic evaluation of swallow)Laryngeal function

Flow-volume loop

  • Extrathoracic obstruction: flattening of inspiratory limb (variable)
  • Intrathoracic (variable): flattening of expiratory limb
  • Fixed: flattening of both limbs

6. Management

General

  • A–B–C assessment
  • Position: sitting up (avoid supine if epiglottitis)
  • O₂ (humidified)
  • Avoid distressing the patient (esp. epiglottitis)
  • Senior/anaesthesia/ENT early
  • Keep child with parent (reduce distress)

Specific

CauseRx
AnaphylaxisIM adrenaline 0.5 mg (1:1000) + IV fluids + antihistamine + steroids + intubate if needed
CroupDexamethasone/nebulised budesonide + nebulised adrenaline if severe
EpiglottitisAnaesthesia + intubation (in OR); ceftriaxone + dexamethasone
Foreign bodyBronchoscopic removal (rigid preferred in children)
Asthma/COPDBronchodilators, steroids, O₂
Laryngeal oedemaAdrenaline (nebulised), steroids, intubate
Vocal cord dysfunctionSpeech therapy, reassurance
Laryngeal tumourENT evaluation, biopsy, oncology
Bilateral VC paralysisTracheostomy
Subglottic stenosisDilatation, stenting

Don'ts

  • Don't examine throat (epiglottitis) — may cause complete obstruction
  • Don't lie child down (epiglottitis)
  • Don't delay intubation in impending obstruction

7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
StridorUpper airway; emergency
WheezeLower airway (asthma, COPD)
Inspiratory stridorExtrathoracic (laryngeal)
ExpiratoryIntrathoracic (trachea)
BiphasicFixed/subglottic
Anaphylaxis RxIM adrenaline 0.5 mg
CroupDexamethasone ± nebulised adrenaline
EpiglottitisAnaesthesia + intubation + ceftriaxone
Foreign bodyBronchoscopic removal
Don't examine throat in epiglottitisMay obstruct

8. MCQs (10)

  1. Inspiratory stridor is caused by:

A. Tracheal tumour

B. Laryngeal obstruction (extrathoracic)

C. COPD

D. Asthma

E. None

Answer: B — Extrathoracic.

  1. Expiratory wheeze is most characteristic of:

A. Laryngeal oedema

B. Lower airway obstruction (asthma, COPD)

C. Foreign body

D. None

E. Croup

Answer: B — Lower airway.

  1. Biphasic stridor suggests:

A. Asthma

B. Fixed (subglottic stenosis, tracheal tumour)

C. COPD

D. None

E. Pneumonia

Answer: B — Fixed.

  1. First-line treatment of anaphylaxis with stridor:

A. Steroids

B. IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (1:1000)

C. Antihistamine

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — IM adrenaline.

  1. The "steeple sign" on neck X-ray suggests:

A. Epiglottitis

B. Croup (subglottic narrowing)

C. Foreign body

D. None

E. Asthma

Answer: B — Croup.

  1. The "thumb sign" on lateral neck X-ray suggests:

A. Croup

B. Epiglottitis (swollen epiglottis)

C. Foreign body

D. None

E. Asthma

Answer: B — Epiglottitis.

  1. A child with sudden cough, stridor, unilateral wheeze. First:

A. Steroids

B. Bronchoscopy (foreign body)

C. Antibiotics

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — Foreign body removal.

  1. The most common cause of croup:

A. Bacterial

B. Viral (parainfluenza)

C. Allergic

D. None

E. Fungal

Answer: B — Parainfluenza.

  1. VCD (vocal cord dysfunction) is treated by:

A. Speech therapy + reassurance (not asthma Rx)

B. Steroids

C. Adrenaline

D. Antibiotics

E. None

Answer: A — Speech therapy.

  1. Stridor in anaphylaxis indicates:

A. Mild reaction

B. Severe/life-threatening (laryngeal oedema)

C. Asthma

D. None

E. Anxiety

Answer: B — Severe.

9. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 30-year-old with sudden onset stridor and urticaria after eating peanuts. Rx:

A. Antihistamine

B. IM adrenaline 0.5 mg immediately

C. Steroids

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — Adrenaline.

  1. A 3-year-old with barking cough and stridor. Most likely:

A. Croup (viral)

B. Epiglottitis

C. Asthma

D. Foreign body

E. None

Answer: A — Croup.

  1. The most appropriate investigation for chronic stridor is:

A. ABG

B. Laryngoscopy +/− bronchoscopy + CT

C. CXR

D. Spirogram

E. None

Answer: B — Direct visualisation + imaging.

  1. A child with sudden unilateral wheeze. Most likely:

A. Asthma

B. Foreign body

C. Pneumonia

D. None

E. Croup

Answer: B — Foreign body.

  1. The first-line treatment of severe croup is:

A. Antibiotics

B. Dexamethasone (oral) ± nebulised adrenaline

C. Theophylline

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — Dexamethasone.

  1. A 50-year-old with hoarseness and progressive stridor, smoker. Best next:

A. Antibiotics

B. Laryngoscopy + CT (laryngeal cancer workup)

C. Wait

D. Steroids

E. None

Answer: B — Laryngeal cancer.

  1. The most common cause of stridor in an adult:

A. Croup

B. Laryngeal tumour (smoker) or laryngeal oedema

C. Epiglottitis

D. Foreign body

E. None

Answer: B — Laryngeal pathology.

  1. The flow-volume loop in VCD shows:

A. Variable extrathoracic obstruction (flattened inspiratory limb)

B. Normal

C. Fixed obstruction

D. Restrictive

E. None

Answer: A — Variable extrathoracic.

  1. Bilateral vocal cord paralysis causes:

A. Hoarseness

B. Stridor (often biphasic) +/− respiratory failure

C. None

D. Wheeze

E. Asthma

Answer: B — Stridor + RF.

  1. The best initial treatment of croup at home:

A. Wait

B. Cool/steam air + reassure + oral dexamethasone if severe

C. Antibiotics

D. Adrenaline

E. None

Answer: B — Cool air + dexamethasone.

10. Flashcards

  • Q: Inspiratory stridor?

A: Extrathoracic (laryngeal).

  • Q: Expiratory wheeze?

A: Lower airway (asthma, COPD).

  • Q: Biphasic stridor?

A: Fixed (subglottic stenosis, tracheal tumour).

  • Q: Anaphylaxis + stridor?

A: IM adrenaline 0.5 mg.

  • Q: Croup steeple sign?

A: Subglottic narrowing.

  • Q: Epiglottitis thumb sign?

A: Swollen epiglottis.

  • Q: Foreign body in child?

A: Bronchoscopic removal.

  • Q: VCD Rx?

A: Speech therapy.

  • Q: Don't in epiglottitis?

A: Examine throat (may cause obstruction).

  • Q: Croup cause?

A: Parainfluenza (viral).

11. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. A 10. B

SBAs

  1. B 2. A 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. A 9. B 10. B

12. Summary

Stridor = upper airway emergency. Inspiratory = extrathoracic (laryngeal); expiratory = intrathoracic; biphasic = fixed. Anaphylaxis: IM adrenaline. Croup: dexamethasone. Epiglottitis: anaesthesia + intubation. Foreign body: bronchoscopic removal. VCD: speech therapy. Don't examine throat in epiglottitis.

Stridor (upper airway)
