2. Definition
3. Core Anatomy / Pathophysiology
Resistance mechanisms
Transmission
4. Normal Values / Important Cut-offs
Diagnostic Thresholds
Bedaquiline QTc Monitoring
Linezolid Myelosuppression Monitoring
5. Classification Summary
6. Etiology / Causes of Resistance
Primary (transmitted)
Acquired (amplification)
7. Risk Factors for DR-TB
8. Clinical Features
9. Investigations
1. Rapid Molecular (First-line)
2. Culture DST (Gold Standard)
3. Whole Genome Sequencing (WGS)
4. Monitoring During DR-TB Treatment
10. Interpretation Frameworks
1. GeneXpert RIF Resistance → Algorithm
2. Drug Selection for Individualised Regimen (WHO 2022)
3. Treatment Outcomes (WHO Definitions)
11. Management
1. Shorter All-Oral MDR/RR-TB Regimen (9–11 months) — PREFERRED FOR MDR/RR-TB
2. BPaL Regimen (6 months) — FOR PRE-XDR / INTOLERANT TO SHORTER REGIMEN
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 17.5.05 Hospital Acquired And Ventilator Associated Pneumonia

Related: [[Pneumonia]], [[Community-acquired pneumonia severity assessment]], [[Ventilatory support and escalation]], [[ARDS]], [[ICU management]], [[Sepsis]], [[Antimicrobial stewardship]], [[Multidrug-resistant organisms]]

[!important]
HAP = pneumonia occurring ≥48h after hospital admission (not incubating at admission). VAP = pneumonia occurring >48–72h after endotracheal intubation. Key FCPS/MRCP: VAP = most common ICU infection; MDROs (P. aeruginosa, MRSA, Acinetobacter, ESBL) drive empiric therapy; Clinical Pulmonary Infection Score (CPIS) for diagnosis; short-course therapy (7–8 days) non-inferior; de-escalation critical; ventilator bundle for prevention.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define HAP and VAP and distinguish from CAP and aspiration pneumonia
  • Identify MDRO risk factors and apply local antibiograms for empiric therapy
  • Apply CPIS for VAP diagnosis and understand limitations
  • Prescribe empiric therapy per local epidemiology and severity
  • Implement de-escalation at 48–72h based on cultures
  • Apply short-course (7–8 days) therapy evidence
  • Implement ventilator bundle for VAP prevention
  • Recognise VAP complications (empyema, lung abscess, septic shock)

2. Definition

TermDefinition
HAPPneumonia occurring ≥48h after hospital admission, not incubating at admission
VAPPneumonia occurring >48–72h after endotracheal intubation (mechanical ventilation)
HCAP (historical)Healthcare-associated pneumonia — no longer a distinct category (IDSA/ATS 2016) — treat as CAP or HAP based on MDRO risk
FCPS/MRCP tip: VAP = most common ICU-acquired infection (10–20% ventilated patients). Mortality 10–20% (attributable ~10%).

3. Core Microbiology

Typical Pathogens by Setting

PathogenHAPVAPNotes
Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA/MRSA)CommonMost common (20–30%)MRSA ~20–50% of S. aureus
Pseudomonas aeruginosaCommonVery common (15–25%)MDRO hallmark, biofilm on tube
Enterobacteriaceae (E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterobacter)CommonCommonESBL, carbapenemase increasing
Acinetobacter baumanniiIncreasingICU-endemicXDR/PDR (pan-drug resistant)
Stenotrophomonas maltophiliaRareIncreasingIntrinsic resistance
Haemophilus influenzaeCommon (non-ventilated)Less commonNon-typable, biofilm
Streptococcus pneumoniaeHAP (non-ventilated)Rare in VAPCAP-like
AnaerobesAspiration-relatedAspiration-relatedPeptostreptococcus, Prevotella
Fungi (Candida, Aspergillus)RareColonisation vs infectionUsually colonisation

MDRO Risk Factors (Guide Empiric Coverage)

Risk FactorPathogens of Concern
Prior IV antibiotics (90 days)MRSA, Pseudomonas, ESBL, CRE
ICU stay >5 daysMDR Pseudomonas, Acinetobacter
High local MDRO prevalenceKnow your ICU antibiogram
ImmunosuppressionFungi, CMV, PJP, resistant bacteria
Structural lung disease (bronchiectasis)Pseudomonas, NTM
Recent hospitalisation (90 days)MDROs

4. Clinical Features

HAP (Non-Ventilated)

  • New/worsening cough (purulent sputum)
  • Fever (>38.3°C) or hypothermia (<36°C)
  • Leucocytosis or leucopenia
  • New/worsening infiltrate on CXR
  • Hypoxaemia, increased O2 requirement
  • Tachypnoea, tachycardia

VAP (Ventilated)

  • New/worsening infiltrate on CXR (often difficult to interpret)
  • Purulent tracheal secretions (increased volume, colour change)
  • Fever/hypothermia, leucocytosis/leucopenia
  • Worsening gas exchange (↑ FiO2 requirement, ↓ PaO2/FiO2)
  • Haemodynamic instability (septic shock)
  • NO single sign is specific — need combination
FCPS/MRCP tip: Clinical diagnosis of VAP is unreliable — need CPIS + cultures + CXR.

5. Diagnosis

1. Clinical Pulmonary Infection Score (CPIS) — VAP

Component0 Points1 Point2 Points
Temperature36.5–38.4°C36–36.4 or 38.5–38.9<36 or >38.9
Leucocytes4–11 ×10⁹/L<4 or >11<4 or >11 + bands >5%
Tracheal secretionsMinimalModeratePurulent
CXRNo infiltrateDiffuse/patchyLocalised infiltrate
PaO2/FiO2>240 / >3.2 kPa—<240 / <3.2 kPa
Culture (tracheal aspirate)No growthGrowthPositive (quantitative)

Score ≥6 = probable VAP; <6 = unlikely VAP

Limitation: CPIS non-specific (sensitivity ~70%, specificity ~60%). Use with cultures + clinical judgement.

2. Microbiological Sampling

SampleMethodNotes
Endotracheal aspirate (ETA)Non-bronchoscopic, quantitative/semi-quantitativeEasy, routine, contamination risk
Bronchoscopic BAL / PSBBronchoscopic, quantitativeHigher specificity, lower contamination
Mini-BALNon-bronchoscopic, plugged catheterCompromise
Blood cultures×2 setsBacteraemia detection

Quantitative thresholds (predict true infection):

  • ETA: ≥10⁶ CFU/mL
  • BAL: ≥10⁴ CFU/mL
  • PSB (protected specimen brush): ≥10³ CFU/mL

3. Imaging

  • CXR: New infiltrate (consolidation, GGO, cavitation) — poor inter-observer agreement
  • CT (if diagnostic uncertainty): Better characterisation, complications (abscess, empyema)

[Truncated — see hospital-acquired-and-ventilator-associated-pneumonia.md for full content]

Topic Overview

1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition
3. Core Microbiology
Typical Pathogens by Setting
MDRO Risk Factors (Guide Empiric Coverage)
4. Clinical Features
HAP (Non-Ventilated)
VAP (Ventilated)
5. Diagnosis
1. Clinical Pulmonary Infection Score (CPIS) — VAP
2. Microbiological Sampling
3. Imaging

▸ 17.5.06 Latent Tuberculosis Infection

Related: [[Pulmonary tuberculosis]], [[Drug-resistant tuberculosis]], [[HIV/TB coinfection]], [[BCG vaccination]], [[Immunosuppression]], [[TNF-alpha inhibitors]], [[Biologics]], [[Immigration screening]]

[!important]
Latent TB infection (LTBI) = Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection without active disease (no symptoms, normal CXR, negative microbiology). Key FCPS/MRCP: IGRA > TST (BCG independent), treat before immunosuppression (anti-TNF, steroids >15mg, transplant), regimens: 3HR, 4R, 3HP, 6H/9H, 3HP preferred (completion), rule out active TB first (clinical, CXR, sputum), monitor hepatotoxicity.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define LTBI and distinguish from active TB
  • Select appropriate test (IGRA vs TST) and interpret in context of BCG
  • Apply treatment indications (contacts, immunosuppression, HIV, silica, CKD, etc.)
  • Prescribe appropriate regimen (3HP, 4R, 3HR, 6H/9H) with monitoring
  • Rule out active TB before LTBI treatment (clinical, CXR, sputum)
  • Manage special populations (HIV, pregnancy, children, CKD, liver disease)
  • Monitor hepatotoxicity and adherence

2. Definition

Latent TB Infection (LTBI) = M. tuberculosis infection with immune control but no active disease.

  • No symptoms (asymptomatic)
  • Normal CXR (or old fibrotic scars only)
  • Negative sputum (smear/culture/PCR)
  • Positive immune test (IGRA or TST)
FCPS/MRCP tip: LTBI ≠ Active TB. LTBI is NOT infectious. LTBI can reactivate (5–10% lifetime, higher if immunocompromised). IGRA preferred over TST (BCG independent).

3. Pathophysiology

  1. Inhalation of M. tuberculosis → alveolar macrophage phagocytosis
  2. Immune response: Th1 (IFN-γ, TNF-α) → macrophage activation → granuloma formation
  3. Containment: Bacteria dormant in granuloma (caseous centre, fibrotic capsule)
  4. Latency: Bacteria viable but non-replicating (dormant)
  5. Reactivation: Immune impairment → granuloma breakdown → active TB

4. Diagnosis

1. Interferon-Gamma Release Assay (IGRA) — PREFERRED

TestPrincipleAdvantages
QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus (QFT-Plus)ELISA: IFN-γ response to ESAT-6/CFP-10 (TB-specific antigens)BCG independent, single visit, objective
T-SPOT.TBELISPOT: IFN-γ producing T-cells to ESAT-6/CFP-10BCG independent, higher sensitivity in immunocompromised
FeatureIGRATST
BCG effectNoneFalse + (if BCG after infancy)
Sensitivity (active TB)~85–90%~80%
Specificity>95%~60–70% (BCG)
Visits1 (blood draw)2 (read at 48–72h)
Reader biasNoneYes
ImmunocompromisedT-SPOT > QFTLower sensitivity
FCPS/MRCP tip: IGRA is preferred for most adults. TST only if IGRA unavailable or children <5y.

2. Tuberculin Skin Test (TST / Mantoux)

  • 5 TU PPD intradermal → read induration at 48–72h
  • Cut-offs (UK/NICE):
  • ≥5mm: HIV, contacts, immunosuppressed, fibrotic CXR
  • ≥10mm: Recent immigrants from high TB burden, healthcare workers, IVDU, prisoners
  • ≥15mm: Low risk (no risk factors)

3. Ruling Out Active TB (MANDATORY Before LTBI Treatment)

InvestigationIndication
Clinical assessmentCough, fever, weight loss, night sweats, haemoptysis
CXR (PA erect)Mandatory — exclude active TB, old fibrosis
Sputum (×3)GeneXpert MTB/RIF Ultra, smear, culture — if cough
Symptom screenStandardised questionnaire
FCPS/MRCP tip: Never treat LTBI without excluding active TB first. Normal CXR + no symptoms = LTBI likely.

5. Treatment Indications (Who to Treat)

High Priority (Strong Recommendation)

GroupRationale
Close contacts of pulmonary TB (especially <5y, immunocompromised)High progression risk
HIV (any CD4)20–30x reactivation risk
Anti-TNF therapy (infliximab, adalimumab, etc.)High reactivation risk (screen before starting)
Other biologics (anti-IL-6, anti-IL-17, anti-IL-23, JAK inhibitors)Increased risk
Steroids ≥15mg prednisolone ≥4 weeksImmunosuppression
Solid organ / HSCT transplant (pre/post)Immunosuppression
CKD / Dialysis10–25x risk
Silicosis3x risk
Gastrectomy / Jejunoileal bypassHistorical risk
Head/Neck/Lung cancer (pre-chemo/radiotherapy)Immunosuppression

Lower Priority (Individualised)

  • Healthcare workers (newly positive conversion)
  • Prisoners, homeless, IVDU (high prevalence settings)
  • Immigrants from high TB burden countries (screening programmes)
  • Children <5y (household contacts)

6. Treatment Regimens (Adults)

RegimenDurationDosingKey Features
3HP (3 months weekly Rifapentine + INH)12 weeksRPT 900mg + INH 900mg + Pyridoxine 25mg weekly (DOT)Preferred (highest completion, lowest hepatotoxicity)
4R (4 months daily Rifampicin)4 monthsR 10mg/kg daily (max 600mg)Alternative if 3HP contraindicated/unavailable
3HR (3 months daily Rifampicin + INH)3 monthsR 10mg/kg + H 5mg/kg daily + PyridoxineShorter than 6H, higher hepatotoxicity than 3HP
6H / 9H (6–9 months daily INH)6–9 monthsH 5mg/kg (max 300mg) + PyridoxineIf rifampicin contraindicated (interactions, resistance)
FCPS/MRCP tip: 3HP is preferred (high completion, low toxicity). 3HR if RPT unavailable. 6H/9H if rifampicin contraindicated.

Paediatric Dosing (Weight-Based)

RegimenDosing
3HPRPT 15mg/kg (max 900mg) + INH 15–25mg/kg (max 900mg) weekly ×12
4RR 15mg/kg (max 600mg) daily ×4mo
3HRR 15mg/kg + H 10–20mg/kg daily ×3mo

Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6)

  • 25–50mg daily with INH-containing regimens
  • Prevents peripheral neuropathy (especially in malnourished, HIV, diabetes, alcohol, pregnancy, renal failure)

7. Monitoring During Treatment

ParameterFrequency
Clinical review (symptoms, adherence)Monthly
LFTs (ALT, AST, bilirubin)Baseline, then monthly (more often if risk factors)
Symptom screen (nausea, rash, jaundice, neuropathy)Each visit
Pyridoxine complianceEach visit
INH/RPT adherence (DOT for 3HP)Weekly

Hepatotoxicity Grading & Action

GradeALT/ASTAction
Grade 11–2.5x ULNContinue, monitor weekly
Grade 22.6–5x ULNContinue, monitor 2x/week; consider holding if symptomatic
Grade 35.1–10x ULNHOLD all drugs, investigate, restart sequentially
Grade 4>10x ULNSTOP ALL, urgent referral
Restart sequence: INH → RIF → RPT (least to most hepatotoxic)

8. Special Populations

1. HIV

  • Screen all HIV+ at diagnosis and annually if high risk
  • IGRA preferred (TST false - in low CD4)
  • Treat LTBI regardless of CD4 (ART + LTBI treatment reduces TB by 90%)
  • Regimen: 3HP OK with DTG-based ART; rifampicin → rifabutin if on PI/NNRTI (except DTG)

2. Pregnancy

  • Defer LTBI treatment until postpartum (unless high risk: HIV, recent contact)
  • If must treat: INH 300mg daily + Pyridoxine 25mg daily (safe, avoid RIF/RPT in 1st trimester if possible)
  • Breastfeeding: INH/RIF safe (low milk levels); pyridoxine for infant if on INH

3. Chronic Kidney Disease / Dialysis

  • INH/RIF/PZA — no dose adjustment (hepatic metabolism)
  • Pyridoxine 25mg post-dialysis
  • 3HP preferred (weekly dosing convenient)

4. Liver Disease

  • Child-Pugh A: Standard regimens with close LFT monitoring
  • Child-Pugh B/C: Avoid INH/RIF; consider 4R (rifampicin only) or INH alone with extreme caution; expert advice

5. Immunosuppression (Anti-TNF, Steroids, Transplant)

  • Screen BEFORE starting immunosuppression
  • Treat LTBI for 1 month BEFORE starting anti-TNF if possible
  • Continue LTBI treatment during immunosuppression

9. Drug Interactions

Rifampicin / Rifapentine — Potent CYP3A4 Inducers

Affected DrugsManagement
ART (PIs, NNRTIs)Switch to DTG (50mg BD with rifampicin) or raltegravir; avoid PIs
DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban)Switch to warfarin/LMWH
OCPUse additional contraception (RIF reduces levels)
CorticosteroidsIncrease dose (RIF induces metabolism)
Antifungals (azoles)Avoid or use with caution (levels ↓)
Immunosuppressants (tacrolimus, cyclosporine, sirolimus)Monitor levels, increase dose
Anti-seizure (carbamazepine, phenytoin)Monitor levels (mutual induction)

Isoniazid

  • Pyridoxine (prevents neuropathy)
  • Avoid alcohol (hepatotoxicity)
  • Tyramine-rich foods (mild MAOI effect — rare)

10. Treatment Completion & Outcomes

OutcomeDefinition
CompletedFinished regimen within recommended time (e.g., 12 weeks for 3HP)
IncompleteDid not finish (default, toxicity, pregnancy)
DefaultedMissed ≥2 months consecutive

Post-treatment: No routine repeat testing. Re-infection possible — re-screen if new exposure.

11. BCG Vaccination & LTBI Testing

  • BCG → false + TST (if given after infancy, multiple doses)
  • IGRA = BCG independent (uses ESAT-6/CFP-10 — absent in BCG)
  • BCG scar ≠ LTBI — IGRA/TST needed

12. Cost-Effectiveness

  • 3HP most cost-effective (high completion, low toxicity)
  • Targeted testing (high-risk groups) > population screening
  • IGRA cost-effective vs TST (avoids false + treatment)

13. Prognosis

  • Without treatment: 5–10% lifetime reactivation (higher if immunocompromised)
  • With treatment: 60–90% risk reduction (regimen dependent)
  • 3HP: ~80% completion, ~80% efficacy
  • 4R: ~75% completion, ~80% efficacy
  • 6H/9H: ~60% completion, ~90% efficacy (if completed)

14. Topic Correlation

  • [[Pulmonary tuberculosis]] — active TB

[Content truncated for rendering — see latent-tuberculosis-infection.md for full content]

High Priority (Strong Recommendation)
Lower Priority (Individualised)
6. Treatment Regimens (Adults)
Paediatric Dosing (Weight-Based)
Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6)

2. Pregnancy
3. Chronic Kidney Disease / Dialysis

▸ 17.5.07 Lung Abscess And Necrotizing Pneumonia

Related: [[Pneumonia]], [[Aspiration pneumonia]], [[Empyema and pleural infection]], [[Bronchiectasis]], [[Anaerobic infections]], [[Post-obstructive pneumonia]], [[Airway obstruction]], [[Bronchial carcinoid tumor]], [[Lung cancer]]

[!important]
Lung abscess = necrotising pneumonia with cavitation containing pus/necrotic debris, usually from aspiration of oropharyngeal anaerobes or post-obstructive (tumour, foreign body). Key FCPS/MRCP: Cavitating lesion with air-fluid level on CXR, putrid sputum, anaerobic + mixed flora, prolonged antibiotics (clindamycin/β-lactam+metronidazole), drainage rarely needed, exclude malignancy (post-obstructive), TB, fungal, necrotising pneumonia = fulminant, often S. aureus (PVL+) or Strep. pyogenes, high mortality.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define lung abscess and necrotizing pneumonia and distinguish from empyema, pneumonia, lung cancer
  • Identify aetiology (aspiration anaerobes, post-obstructive, haematogenous, necrotising pneumonia)
  • Recognise clinical features (putrid sputum, air-fluid level, digital clubbing)
  • Apply diagnostic criteria (CXR/CT: cavity with air-fluid level, thick wall, putrid sputum, anaerobes)
  • Manage with prolonged antibiotics (clindamycin, β-lactam+metronidazole, carbapenems) and postural drainage
  • Recognise complications (empyema, bronchopleural fistula, haemoptysis, metastatic spread)
  • Differentiate from empyema, TB, lung cancer, fungal abscess, metastases

2. Definition

TermDefinition
Lung abscessCavitating lesions in lung parenchyma containing purulent material (pus/necrotic debris), usually >2cm, thick wall, air-fluid level, caused by necrotising pneumonia
Necrotizing pneumoniaFulminant, rapidly progressive pneumonia with extensive parenchymal necrosis, often multiple small cavities or single large cavity, high mortality
FCPS/MRCP tip: Lung abscess = cavity with air-fluid level, putrid sputum, anaerobes. Necrotising pneumonia = rapid, multiple cavities, S. aureus (PVL+)/Strep. pyogenes, high mortality.

3. Aetiology

1. Aspiration (Most Common, ~60-80%)

  • Oropharyngeal anaerobes (Peptostreptococcus, Prevotella, Porphyromonas, Fusobacterium, Bacteroides) + aerobes (Strep, Staph, H. influenzae) → polymicrobial
  • Risk factors: Alcoholism, seizure, stroke, anaesthesia, poor dentition, dysphagia, drug overdose, elderly, debilitated
  • Location: Right lower lobe (most common), posterior segment upper lobe (if supine)

2. Post-Obstructive (Bronchial Obstruction)

  • Bronchogenic carcinoma (most common), benign tumour (carcinoid), foreign body, enlarged lymph nodes, endobronchial TB, mucous plug
  • Pathophysiology: Obstruction → distal atelectasis → secondary infection → abscess
  • Key: Always exclude malignancy in new lung abscess, especially >40y, smoker

3. Haematogenous (Septic Emboli)

  • Right-sided endocarditis (tricuspid valve, IVDU), septic thrombophlebitis, metastatic septic emboli
  • Multiple bilateral cavities, often peripheral
  • Organisms: Staph aureus (tricuspid endocarditis), Gram-negatives, fungi

4. Necrotising Pneumonia (Primary)

  • S. aureus (PVL+ community-acquired, MRSA), Strep. pyogenes (Group A strep), Klebsiella, Gram-negatives
  • Fulminant, rapid necrosis, multiple cavities, high mortality (30-50%)

5. Other

  • Immunocompromised: Nocardia, Actinomyces, fungi (Aspergillus, Mucor), TB, PJP
  • Connective tissue: GPA, rheumatoid nodules
  • Inhalation: Chemical, hydrocarbon, foreign body

4. Clinical Features

Lung Abscess (Subacute/Chronic)

FeatureDescription
CoughProductive, putrid/foul-smelling sputum (hallmark)
FeverLow-grade, intermittent, night sweats
Weight loss, anorexiaChronic
HaemoptysisCommon (streaks to massive)
Pleuritic chest painIf pleural involvement
Digital clubbingRapid onset (2–4 weeks), indicates chronicity

Necrotizing Pneumonia (Acute/Fulminant)

  • High fever, rigors, severe dyspnoea, haemoptysis, confusion, shock
  • Rapid progression (hours–days), bilateral/multiple cavities
  • Septic shock, ARDS, multi-organ failure common

Examination

FindingAbscessNecrotising Pneumonia
FeverLow-grade, intermittentHigh, rigors
Breath soundsBronchial breathing over cavityReduced, crackles
PercussionDull over consolidationDull
ClubbingEarly (2-4 wks)Late/absent
CyanosisIf severeCommon

5. Investigations

1. Imaging

ModalityFindings
CXRCavitation with air-fluid level (classic), thick irregular wall (>1cm), surrounding consolidation, usually single, RLL > posterior upper lobe
CT ChestCavity with thick wall, air-fluid level, surrounding GGO/consolidation, vessel extension to wall (feeding vessels), exclude malignancy (wall nodules), detect multiple/bilateral
UltrasoundPleural effusion (parapneumonic/empyema), peripheral abscess localisation
FCPS/MRCP tip: Lung abscess = single cavity, air-fluid level, thick wall, putrid sputum. Necrotising pneumonia = multiple cavities, rapid, S. aureus PVL+. Exclude cancer (wall thickening/nodularity) — CT + bronchoscopy if >40y/smoker.

2. Sputum/Microbiology

SampleKey Findings
Sputum (expectorated)Putrid/foul smell (anaerobes), mixed flora (anaerobes + aerobes)
Gram stainMixed Gram+/Gram-, Gram-negative rods/anaerobes
CulturePolymicrobial: Anaerobes (Peptostreptococcus, Prevotella, Bacteroides, Fusobacterium) + Aerobes (Strep milleri, Staph, H. influenzae, Klebsiella)
Blood culturesPositive in 10-20% (haematogenous spread, endocarditis)
Anaerobic cultureEssential (send in anaerobic transport)

3. Exclusion of Malignancy (Critical if >40y, smoker)

  • CT Chest with contrast: Wall thickness, nodularity, enhancement
  • Bronchoscopy: Exclude endobronchial tumour, foreign body, obtain samples
  • CT-guided biopsy if wall nodularity/suspicious
  • PET-CT: Metabolic activity (SUV max)

4. TB/Other Exclusions

  • GeneXpert MTB/RIF Ultra on sputum
  • AFB smear/culture (TB can cause abscess)
  • Fungal serology/culture (Aspergillus, endemic fungi)
  • Serology: Actinomyces, Nocardia, Legiona, Q fever

[Truncated — see lung-abscess-and-necrotizing-pneumonia.md for full content]

Topic Overview

1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition
3. Aetiology
1. Aspiration (Most Common, ~60-80%)
2. Post-Obstructive (Bronchial Obstruction)
3. Haematogenous (Septic Emboli)
4. Necrotising Pneumonia (Primary)
5. Other
4. Clinical Features
Lung Abscess (Subacute/Chronic)
Necrotizing Pneumonia (Acute/Fulminant)
Examination
5. Investigations
1. Imaging
2. Sputum/Microbiology
3. Exclusion of Malignancy (Critical if >40y, smoker)
4. TB/Other Exclusions
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 17.5.08 Non Tuberculous Mycobacterial Pulmonary Disease

Related: [[Pulmonary tuberculosis]], [[Drug-resistant tuberculosis]], [[Bronchiectasis]], [[Lady Windermere syndrome]], [[Immunocompromised host]], [[Lung cancer]], [[Cystic fibrosis]], [[Immunosuppression]]

[!important]
NTM-PD = pulmonary disease caused by environmental mycobacteria (not M. tuberculosis complex). MAC (M. avium complex) = most common. Key FCPS/MRCP: ATS/IDSA diagnostic criteria (clinical + radiological + microbiological), MAC = nodular/bronchiectatic (elderly women, Lady Windermere) or fibrocavitary (smokers, men); M. kansasii = upper lobe cavitary (TB mimic); M. abscessus = rapid grower, resistant, CF/non-CF; treatment = multi-drug, prolonged (12mo post-culture conversion), MDR/M. abscessus = poor prognosis.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define NTM-PD and distinguish from TB and colonisation/contamination
  • Apply ATS/IDSA 2007/2020 diagnostic criteria (clinical + radiological + microbiological)
  • Identify common NTM species and their clinical/radiological phenotypes
  • Prescribe standard multi-drug regimens per species (MAC, M. kansasii, M. xenopi, M. abscessus)
  • Apply treatment monitoring (monthly sputum cultures, 12mo post-conversion)
  • Recognise complications (drug toxicity, relapse, drug resistance, drug interactions)
  • Manage special populations (CF, non-CF bronchiectasis, immunosuppression, Lady Windermere)

2. Definition

Non-tuberculous mycobacterial pulmonary disease (NTM-PD) = symptomatic pulmonary disease caused by environmental mycobacteria (non-tuberculous mycobacteria, NTM) meeting ATS/IDSA diagnostic criteria. Not transmissible person-to-person (environmental acquisition).

NTM Classification (Runyon):

GroupGrowth RateExamplesPigment
I (Photochromogens)SlowM. kansasii, M. marinum, M. szulgaiYellow/orange in light
II (Scotochromogens)SlowM. scrofulaceum, M. gordonaePigmented in dark
III (Non-chromogens)SlowM. avium complex (MAC), M. xenopi, M. malmoense, M. haemophilumNon-pigmented
IV (Rapid growers)<7 daysM. abscessus, M. fortuitum, M. chelonaeVariable
FCPS/MRCP tip: MAC (Group III) = most common NTM-PD (80-90%). M. kansasii = TB mimic. M. abscessus = rapid grower, highly resistant, CF/non-CF bronchiectasis.

3. ATS/IDSA Diagnostic Criteria (2007/2020) — ALL REQUIRED

CriterionRequirement
1. ClinicalPulmonary symptoms (cough, sputum, haemoptysis, dyspnoea, constitutional) OR compatible CXR/HRCT findings AND exclusion of other diagnoses (TB, malignancy, etc.)
2. RadiologicalCXR/HRCT showing nodules, bronchiectasis, cavities, consolidation, tree-in-bud — consistent with NTM
3. MicrobiologicalA. Positive culture from ≥2 separate expectorated sputum (or 1 bronchial wash/lavage) OR B. Positive culture from lung biopsy with granulomatous inflammation OR C. Transbronchial/other biopsy with granuloma + positive culture
FCPS/MRCP tip: Single positive sputum = colonisation/contamination. Need ≥2 positive sputa OR 1 BAL/biopsy. Exclude TB (GeneXpert, culture).

4. Common NTM Species & Clinical Phenotypes

1. Mycobacterium avium Complex (MAC) — ~80-90% NTM-PD

PhenotypeDemographicsHRCTClinical
Nodular/BronchiectaticElderly women (thin, non-smoker, "Lady Windermere"), non-CF bronchiectasis, pectus excavatum, scoliosisMid/upper zone nodules + bronchiectasis (tree-in-bud), right middle lobe/lingulaChronic cough, fatigue, weight loss, low-grade fever, no cavitation
FibrocavitaryOlder men, smokers, COPD, prior TBUpper lobe cavities (TB mimic), consolidationMore severe, haemoptysis, rapid progression, higher bacterial load

2. Mycobacterium kansasii — Second most common

  • Phenotype: Upper lobe cavitary disease, TB mimic
  • Demographics: Men, smokers, middle-aged
  • HRCT: Upper lobe cavities, consolidation, mediastinal lymphadenopathy
  • Treatment: Rifampicin-based (like TB) — high cure rate (>90%)

3. Mycobacterium xenopi

  • Thermophilic (optimal 42°C) → hot water systems
  • Phenotype: Chronic cavitary/fibrotic, upper lobes
  • Demographics: Elderly, men, comorbidities
  • Treatment: Difficult, prolonged (clarithromycin/rifampicin/ethambutol)

4. Mycobacterium abscessus (Rapid Grower, Group IV)

  • Subspecies: M. abscessus subsp. abscessus (erm(41) functional → inducible macrolide resistance), massiliense (non-functional erm(41) → macrolide susceptible), bolletii
  • Demographics: CF (increasing), non-CF bronchiectasis, post-surgical, skin/soft tissue
  • HRCT: Bronchiectasis, nodules, consolidation, cavities
  • Resistance: Highly drug-resistant (erm(41) → inducible clarithromycin resistance)
  • Treatment: Multi-drug IV + oral (amoxicillin-clavulanate, cefoxitin, imipenem + macrolide if susceptible) — prolonged, toxic, poor cure rate (~30-50%)

5. Mycobacterium fortuitum / chelonae (Rapid Growers)

  • Post-surgical, post-traumatic, catheter-related
  • Pulmonary disease rare (usually skin/soft tissue)

5. Clinical Features

Common

  • Chronic cough (productive, often mucoid/purulent)
  • Fatigue, weight loss, low-grade fever, night sweats
  • Dyspnoea (progressive)
  • Haemoptysis (fibrocavitary forms)
  • Recurrent "pneumonias" unresponsive to standard antibiotics

Examination

  • Crackles (bibasal or localised)
  • Clubbing (chronic)
  • Signs of underlying disease: bronchiectasis, CTD, CF, immunosuppression

6. Investigations

1. Microbiology (Diagnostic Cornerstone)

SampleMethodCriteria
Sputum (≥3 early morning)Culture (solid LJ + liquid MGIT) + AFB smear≥2 positive cultures (separate days)
Bronchoscopy (BAL, bronchial wash)Culture≥1 positive culture + compatible clinical/radiological
Lung biopsyCulture + histologyGranulomatous inflammation + positive culture
Species identification: MALDI-TOF, 16S rRNA sequencing, hsp65 sequencing, whole genome sequencing (for subspecies/ resistance)

2. Drug Susceptibility Testing (DST)

SpeciesRecommended DST
MACClarithromycin (only reliable predictor); amikacin (optional)
M. kansasiiRifampicin (predictor); clarithromycin, ethambutol
M. xenopiClarithromycin, rifampicin, ethambutol
M. abscessusClarithromycin (inducible resistance), amoxicillin-clavulanate, cefoxitin, imipenem, amikacin, tigecycline
Clarithromycin DST for MAC: MIC ≤0.25 = susceptible; MIC ≥0.5 = resistant

3. Imaging

  • HRCT: Tree-in-bud (active), bronchiectasis, nodules, cavities, consolidation
  • CXR: Cavities (fibrocavitary), nodules, bronchiectasis

4. Exclusion of TB

  • GeneXpert MTB/RIF Ultra (rapid)
  • AFB smear/culture (differentiate M. tb complex)
  • Line probe assay (MTBDRplus)

7. ATS/IDSA Treatment Regimens (Standardised)

1. MAC Nodular/Bronchiectatic (Three-Drug Oral)

DrugDoseDuration
Azithromycin250mg daily (or 500mg 3x/week)12 months post-culture conversion
Ethambutol15mg/kg daily (max 1200mg)Same
Rifampicin10mg/kg daily (max 600mg)Same
Alternative: Rifabutin (if rifampicin interactions) + azithromycin + ethambutol
Duration: 12 months after first negative sputum culture (total ~15–18 months)

2. MAC Fibrocavitary / Severe (Three-Drug IV + Oral)

PhaseRegimen
Initial (2–3 months)IV Amikacin 10–15mg/kg 3x/week + IV/PO Azithromycin 250mg daily + PO Ethambutol 15mg/kg daily + PO Rifampicin 10mg/kg daily
ContinuationPO Azithromycin + Ethambutol + Rifampicin (12mo post-conversion)

3. M. kansasii (Three-Drug Oral — Like TB)

DrugDoseDuration
Rifampicin10mg/kg (max 600mg) daily12 months post-conversion
Ethambutol15mg/kg dailySame
Clarithromycin 500mg BD OR Isoniazid 300mg daily500mg BD / 300mgSame
High cure rate (>90%) with rifampicin-based regimen.

4. M. abscessus (Complex Multi-Drug IV + Oral)

PhaseRegimen
Initial (IV, 2–4 weeks)IV Cefoxitin 2g q6h + Imipenem 500mg q6h OR Meropenem 1g q8h + Amoxicillin-clavulanate 1.2g q6h + (if macrolide susceptible) Azithromycin 250mg daily
Continuation (Oral, prolonged)Oral Macrolide (if susceptible) + Oral β-lactam (amox-clav) + Oral inhaled amikacin (optional)
Duration12 months post-culture conversion (often >2 years total)
Key: Subspecies identification (abscessus vs massiliense) determines macrolide utility. Inducible erm(41) resistance in M. abscessus subsp. abscessus.

5. M. xenopi

  • Clarithromycin 500mg BD + Rifampicin 600mg + Ethambutol 15mg/kg ×12mo post-conversion

8. Monitoring During Treatment

ParameterFrequency
Sputum culture (AFB + mycobacterial)Monthly until 3 consecutive negative, then 3-monthly
LFTs (ALT, AST, bilirubin)Baseline, 2-weekly ×1mo, then monthly
FBC (neutropenia — linezolid, rifampicin)Monthly
Renal function (amikacin, tenofovir)Monthly (weekly if on aminoglycosides)
Audiometry (amikacin, streptomycin)Baseline, then 3-monthly
Visual acuity/colour vision (ethambutol)Baseline, monthly
ECG/QTc (macrolides, bedaquiline)Baseline, then as needed
Weight, symptomsMonthly

9. Drug Interactions / Toxicity

DrugKey ToxicityKey Interaction
Rifampicin/RifabutinHepatotoxicity, orange fluids, thrombocytopeniaPotent CYP3A4 inducer (↓ DOACs, OCP, ART, steroids, azoles)
EthambutolOptic neuritis (colour vision, acuity)Avoid if baseline optic neuropathy
Macrolides (clarithro/azithro)QT prolongation, GI, hepatotoxicityCYP3A4 inhibitor (↑ statins, DOACs, tacrolimus)
AmikacinNephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, vestibularLoop diuretics ↑ risk
Cefoxitin/ImipenemRenal adjust, seizures (imipenem)—
LinezolidMyelosuppression (thrombocytopenia), neuropathy, serotonin syndromeSSRIs/SNRIs → serotonin syndrome

10. Special Situations

Lady Windermere Syndrome (MAC in Non-CF Bronchiectasis)

  • Demographics: Elderly thin women, non-smokers, pectus excavatum/scoliosis, mitral valve prolapse
  • HRCT: Right middle lobe/lingula nodules + bronchiectasis (tree-in-bud)
  • Management: Standard 3-drug MAC regimen; avoid unnecessary treatment if asymptomatic

Cystic Fibrosis

  • M. abscessus increasing; M. avium, M. kansasii also
  • Screening: Annual sputum cultures
  • M. abscessus: Multi-drug IV + inhaled amikacin, consider lung transplant implications

Immunosuppressed (HIV, Transplant, Biologics)

  • Disseminated disease more common
  • MAC most common (CD4 <50)
  • Prophylaxis: Azithromycin 1200mg weekly (CD4 <50) or clarithromycin 500mg BD
  • Treatment: Same regimens, adjust for drug interactions (rifampicin + ART)

11. Complications

  • Drug toxicity (hepatotoxicity, optic neuritis, QT prolongation, nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity)
  • Drug interactions (rifampicin induction, macrolide inhibition)
  • Relapse (5–20% after completion)
  • Acquired drug resistance (especially macrolides in MAC, inducible in M. abscessus)
  • Progression to fibrosis/cavities
  • Massive haemoptysis (cavitary forms)

12. Red Flags / Emergencies

  • Massive haemoptysis → BAE

[Content truncated for rendering — see non-tuberculous-mycobacterial-pulmonary-disease.md for full content]

Topic Overview

1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition
3. ATS/IDSA Diagnostic Criteria (2007/2020) — ALL REQUIRED
4. Common NTM Species & Clinical Phenotypes
1. Mycobacterium avium Complex (MAC) — ~80-90% NTM-PD
2. Mycobacterium kansasii — Second most common
3. Mycobacterium xenopi
4. Mycobacterium abscessus (Rapid Grower, Group IV)
5. Mycobacterium fortuitum / chelonae (Rapid Growers)
5. Clinical Features
Common
Examination
6. Investigations
1. Microbiology (Diagnostic Cornerstone)
2. Drug Susceptibility Testing (DST)
3. Imaging
4. Exclusion of TB
7. ATS/IDSA Treatment Regimens (Standardised)
1. MAC Nodular/Bronchiectatic (Three-Drug Oral)
2. MAC Fibrocavitary / Severe (Three-Drug IV + Oral)
3. M. kansasii (Three-Drug Oral — Like TB)
4. M. abscessus (Complex Multi-Drug IV + Oral)
5. M. xenopi
8. Monitoring During Treatment
9. Drug Interactions / Toxicity
10. Special Situations
Lady Windermere Syndrome (MAC in Non-CF Bronchiectasis)
Cystic Fibrosis
Immunosuppressed (HIV, Transplant, Biologics)

▸ 17.5.09 Opportunistic Pulmonary Infections
[!important]
Opportunistic pulmonary infections occur in immunocompromised patients (HIV, transplant, chemotherapy, steroids, immunosuppressants). They include PJP, fungal infections (Aspergillus, Cryptococcus), CMV, NTM, and reactivation TB. Diagnosis requires aggressive workup (CT, BAL, biopsy) and empirical broad-spectrum therapy in critically ill patients.

Related: [[Pneumonia]], [[Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia|PJP]], [[Pulmonary aspergillosis syndromes]], [[HIV Infection and AIDS|HIV Infection and AIDS]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: In an HIV patient with CD4 <200 and pneumonia → think PJP. In a transplant/chemo patient with cavitating lesion → think Aspergillus or Nocardia. In severe neutropenia → think invasive aspergillosis. Always consider TB reactivation in immunocompromised.

1. Definition

Opportunistic pulmonary infections = infections caused by organisms that typically do not cause disease in immunocompetent hosts, but exploit immunocompromised states.

2. Risk Factors (immunocompromise)

CategoryExamples
HIV/AIDSCD4 <200, <100, <50
Transplant (SOT, HSCT)Immunosuppression, GVHD
ChemotherapyNeutropenia, lymphopenia
SteroidsHigh-dose, prolonged
ImmunosuppressantsBiologics, anti-TNF
Haematological malignancyLeukaemia, lymphoma
Solid tumoursAdvanced disease
DiabetesHyperglycaemia impairs immunity
Chronic diseaseCKD, cirrhosis, malnutrition
AgeElderly

3. Common Organisms by Immune Defect

DefectCommon organisms
CD4 <200 (HIV)PJP, bacterial (S. pneumo, H. flu)
CD4 <100 (HIV)Toxoplasma, Cryptococcus, MAC
CD4 <50 (HIV)CMV, MAC, disseminated fungal
NeutropeniaBacteria (Pseudomonas, Staph), invasive aspergillosis, Candida
Cellular immunity (transplant)CMV, PJP, fungi, mycobacteria
Humoral immunity (CVID, etc.)Encapsulated bacteria

4. Specific Infections

Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP)

  • CD4 <200 (HIV); transplant; high-dose steroids
  • Subacute dyspnoea, dry cough, fever
  • CXR: bilateral perihilar/interstitial infiltrates
  • HRCT: ground-glass opacities (often upper lobe in non-HIV)
  • Diagnosis: induced sputum or BAL → silver/Pertussis stain, PCR
  • Treatment: TMP-SMX high dose + steroids (if PaO₂ <70 or A-a >35)
  • Prophylaxis: TMP-SMX when CD4 <200

Invasive aspergillosis

  • Neutropenia (especially prolonged)
  • Fever + new infiltrate despite antibiotics
  • HRCT: nodule with halo sign (GGO around nodule), air crescent sign (late)
  • Diagnosis: BAL (Aspergillus PCR, galactomannan), biopsy
  • Treatment: voriconazole (1st line), isavuconazole, liposomal amphotericin B
  • Surgery for localised disease

Cytomegalovirus (CMV) pneumonia

  • Transplant (especially HSCT, lung)
  • Fever, dyspnoea, dry cough, hypoxaemia
  • CXR: bilateral interstitial infiltrates
  • Diagnosis: BAL CMV PCR, viral load, intranuclear inclusions ("owl's eye")
  • Treatment: ganciclovir IV (or valganciclovir oral)
  • Prophylaxis: valganciclovir in high-risk

Cryptococcus

  • HIV (CD4 <100), transplant
  • Pneumonia + meningitis (often)
  • Diagnosis: serum/CSF cryptococcal antigen (CrAg)
  • Treatment: amphotericin B + flucytosine → fluconazole

NTM (non-tuberculous mycobacteria)

  • MAC (esp. CD4 <50 in HIV), immunocompromised
  • Disseminated disease in HIV
  • Treatment: macrolide + ethambutol + rifabutin (×12+ months)

Nocardia

  • Cell-mediated immunity defect
  • Cavitating pneumonia (often upper lobe)
  • CNS dissemination (brain abscess)
  • Diagnosis: modified acid-fast stain, culture
  • Treatment: TMP-SMX (or imipenem + amikacin for severe)

Other

  • Aspergillus fumigatus → ABPA (in asthma/CF), aspergilloma
  • Candida (rare pulmonary pathogen)
  • Toxoplasma (HIV, CD4 <100)
  • Pneumocystis (covered above)

5. Clinical Features

Symptoms

  • Fever (often only sign)
  • Dyspnoea (subacute or acute)
  • Cough (dry or productive)
  • Hypoxaemia (may be profound)
  • Constitutional: weight loss, night sweats, fatigue

Signs

  • Fever, tachycardia, tachypnoea
  • Crackles (diffuse or focal)
  • Hypoxaemia (silent chest if severe)
  • Signs of underlying disease

6. Diagnosis

Investigations

TestPurpose
CT chestPattern recognition, biopsy guidance
SputumAFB, fungal, culture, cytology
Blood culturesBacteria, fungi
GalactomannanAspergillus
CMV PCRCMV viraemia
Cryptococcal AgCrAg
PJP PCR / silver stainPJP on BAL/sputum
BALBronchoscopy with lavage
Transbronchial biopsyFor definitive diagnosis
HIV testAlways consider
CD4 countSeverity of HIV
GalactomannanAspergillus

Empirical treatment

  • Critically ill immunocompromised with pneumonia:
  • Empiric broad-spectrum (anti-bacterial + anti-PJP + anti-fungal + anti-CMV)
  • E.g. meropenem + TMP-SMX + voriconazole + ganciclovir
  • Adjust to culture/biopsy

7. Management

General

  • Treat underlying cause (HIV, malignancy)
  • Antimicrobial therapy (specific to organism)
  • Supportive: O₂, ventilation
  • Reduce immunosuppression (if possible)
  • PJP prophylaxis when CD4 <200 (TMP-SMX)
  • Vaccinations (inactivated, avoid live)

Specific

InfectionTreatment
PJPTMP-SMX 15–20 mg/kg/day TMP + steroids if PaO₂ <70
Invasive AspergillusVoriconazole (6–12 weeks)
CMV pneumoniaGanciclovir IV
CryptococcusAmphotericin B + flucytosine → fluconazole
MACMacrolide + ethambutol + rifabutin
NocardiaTMP-SMX (or imipenem + amikacin)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
DefinitionInfection in immunocompromised
HIV CD4 <200PJP
HIV CD4 <100Toxoplasma, Cryptococcus
HIV CD4 <50CMV, MAC
Neutropenia + cavitatingInvasive Aspergillus
PJP RxTMP-SMX + steroids (if severe)
Aspergillus RxVoriconazole (1st line)
CMV RxGanciclovir
MAC RxMacrolide + ethambutol + rifabutin
Empiric RxBroad-spectrum in critically ill

9. MCQs (10)

  1. In HIV with CD4 <200 and pneumonia, most likely:

A. TB

B. PJP

C. Strep pneumo

D. Aspergillus

E. None

Answer: B — PJP.

  1. First-line treatment for PJP:

A. TMP-SMX (high dose) ± steroids

B. Acyclovir

C. Meropenem

D. Vancomycin

E. None

Answer: A — TMP-SMX.

  1. Steroids are added in PJP when:

A. Always

B. PaO₂ <70 mmHg or A-a gradient >35 mmHg

C. Never

D. Only in HIV

E. None

Answer: B — Severe hypoxaemia.

  1. The most likely organism in neutropenic patient with cavitating lesion:

A. PJP

B. Invasive Aspergillus

C. S. aureus

D. TB

E. None

Answer: B — Aspergillus.

  1. First-line treatment for invasive aspergillosis:

A. Voriconazole

B. TMP-SMX

C. Ganciclovir

D. Amphotericin

E. None

Answer: A — Voriconazole.

  1. The "halo sign" on HRCT suggests:

A. PJP

B. Invasive aspergillosis (GGO around nodule)

C. TB

D. Sarcoidosis

E. None

Answer: B — Aspergillus.

  1. CMV pneumonia is most common in:

A. HIV

B. Transplant (esp. HSCT, lung)

C. Elderly

D. Children

E. None

Answer: B — Transplant.

  1. First-line treatment for CMV pneumonia:

A. Acyclovir

B. Ganciclovir IV

C. TMP-SMX

D. Voriconazole

E. None

Answer: B — Ganciclovir.

  1. The most common cause of focal cavitating pneumonia in cell-mediated immunity defect:

A. S. aureus

B. Nocardia

C. PJP

D. CMV

E. None

Answer: B — Nocardia.

  1. Nocardia is best treated with:

A. TMP-SMX (or imipenem + amikacin)

B. Voriconazole

C. Ganciclovir

D. Acyclovir

E. None

Answer: A — TMP-SMX.

10. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 40-year-old with HIV (CD4 80) has fever, dry cough, bilateral infiltrates. Most likely:

A. Bacterial pneumonia

B. PJP

C. TB

D. Sarcoidosis

E. None

Answer: B — PJP.

  1. Best initial treatment for severe PJP (PaO₂ 60):

A. TMP-SMX alone

B. TMP-SMX + prednisolone (prednisone taper)

C. Acyclovir

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — TMP-SMX + steroids.

  1. The most sensitive test for PJP:

A. CXR

B. PJP PCR on BAL or induced sputum

C. Sputum culture

D. ABG

E. None

Answer: B — PJP PCR.

  1. The drug of choice for cryptococcal meningitis in HIV:

A. Amphotericin B + flucytosine (induction), then fluconazole

B. TMP-SMX

C. Voriconazole

D. Acyclovir

E. None

Answer: A — Ampho B + flucytosine.

  1. MAC prophylaxis in HIV is indicated when CD4 <:

A. 100

B. 50

C. 200

D. 500

E. None

Answer: B — CD4 <50 (azithromycin weekly).

  1. Aspergillus galactomannan is:

A. Urine test

B. Serum/BAL marker for invasive aspergillosis

C. Skin test

D. None

E. Sputum test

Answer: B — Serum/BAL.

  1. CMV pneumonia in HSCT recipient. Treatment duration:

A. 1 week

B. 2–3 weeks (until clearance)

C. 3 months

D. 6 months

E. None

Answer: B — 2–3 weeks.

  1. The most common pulmonary infection in HIV (any CD4):

A. PJP

B. Bacterial (S. pneumo, H. flu)

C. TB

D. MAC

E. None

Answer: B — Bacterial most common.

  1. The first-line treatment for toxoplasmosis in HIV:

A. Pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + folinic acid

B. TMP-SMX

C. Voriconazole

D. Ganciclovir

E. None

Answer: A — Pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine.

  1. PJP prophylaxis in HIV is started when CD4 <:

A. 100

B. 200

C. 50

D. 500

E. None

Answer: B — CD4 <200.

11. Flashcards

  • Q: HIV CD4 <200 pneumonia?

A: PJP.

  • Q: PJP Rx?

A: TMP-SMX + steroids if severe.

  • Q: Invasive Aspergillus in neutropenia?

A: Voriconazole.

  • Q: CMV pneumonia in transplant?

A: Ganciclovir.

  • Q: Cryptococcus Rx?

A: Ampho B + flucytosine.

  • Q: MAC Rx?

A: Macrolide + ethambutol + rifabutin.

  • Q: Nocardia Rx?

A: TMP-SMX.

  • Q: Halo sign?

A: Invasive aspergillosis (GGO around nodule).

  • Q: Toxoplasmosis Rx?

A: Pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine.

  • Q: PJP prophylaxis?

A: CD4 <200.

12. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B 2. A 3. B 4. B 5. A 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. A

SBAs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. A 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. A 10. B

13. Summary

Opportunistic pulmonary infections occur in immunocompromised. PJP (HIV CD4 <200): TMP-SMX + steroids if severe. Invasive aspergillosis (neutropenia): voriconazole. CMV (transplant): ganciclovir. MAC: macrolide + ethambutol + rifabutin. Always consider TB reactivation. Empiric broad-spectrum in critically ill.

▸ 17.5.12 Pneumocystis Jirovecii Pneumonia

Related: [[HIV]], [[Opportunistic pulmonary infections]], [[Atypical pneumonia]], [[Immunocompromised host]], [[ARDS]], [[ABG Interpretation]], [[Indications for intubation in respiratory disease]], [[Cotrimoxazole]], [[Dapsone]], [[Pentamidine]]

[!important]
PCP = opportunistic fungal pneumonia caused by Pneumocystis jirovecii (formerly P. carinii). Classic in HIV/AIDS (CD4 <200), also in iatrogenic immunosuppression (steroids, chemo, transplant, biologics). Key FCPS/MRCP: Subacute dyspnoea, dry cough, fever, hypoxaemia out of proportion to CXR, bilateral perihilar ground-glass on HRCT, induced sputum/BAL for PCR/GMS stain, cotrimoxazole 1st line, adjunctive steroids if PaO2 <9.3 kPa (70 mmHg), prophylaxis if CD4 <200.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Recognise classic clinical presentation in HIV and non-HIV immunosuppressed hosts
  • Apply diagnostic algorithm (clinical + HRCT + induced sputum/BAL PCR + β-D-glucan)
  • Interpret HRCT (bilateral perihilar ground-glass, sparing apices/bases, cysts in chronic)
  • Initiate treatment (cotrimoxazole 1st line, alternatives for intolerance)
  • Apply adjunctive steroid criteria (PaO2 <9.3 kPa or A-a gradient >35 mmHg)
  • Manage prophylaxis (indications, agents, discontinuation)
  • Differentiate from other pneumonias (TB, bacterial, viral, fungal, PJP IRIS)

2. Definition

Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP) = opportunistic pneumonia caused by the fungus Pneumocystis jirovecii (obligate extracellular pulmonary pathogen). Affects immunocompromised hosts: HIV (CD4 <200), steroids (>20mg prednisolone ×4 weeks), chemotherapy, transplant, biologics (anti-TNF, rituximab), primary immunodeficiency.

FCPS/MRCP tip: Non-HIV PCP often more acute, severe, higher mortality than HIV-PCP. Steroids are the #1 risk factor in non-HIV.

3. Core Anatomy / Pathophysiology

Pneumocystis jirovecii

  • Fungus (formerly classified as protozoan), obligate extracellular pulmonary pathogen
  • Life cycle: Trophic forms → cysts (5–8 µm) → release trophic forms
  • Transmission: Airborne, person-to-person (asymptomatic carriers)
  • Attachment: Type I pneumocytes → surfactant depletion → alveolar collapse

Immune Evasion / Pathogenesis

  1. CD4+ T-cell depletion → loss of macrophage activation (IFN-γ, TNF-α)
  2. Impaired phagocytosis → uncontrolled replication in alveoli
  3. Foamy exudate (trophic forms, cysts, surfactant proteins) fills alveoli
  4. Type I pneumocyte injury → surfactant dysfunction → atelectasis
  5. Inflammatory response → β-D-glucan release (diagnostic marker)
  6. Non-HIV: More intense inflammation → worse hypoxaemia, faster progression

4. Clinical Features

HIV-Associated PCP (Classic)

  • Subacute onset (days–weeks)
  • Progressive dyspnoea (exertional → rest)
  • Dry, non-productive cough
  • Low-grade fever
  • Weight loss, night sweats
  • Chest discomfort (vague)
  • CD4 <200 (often <100), high viral load
  • Often on no prophylaxis or failed adhesion

Non-HIV PCP (Iatrogenic Immunosuppression)

  • Acute, fulminant onset (hours–days)
  • High fever, severe dyspnoea, rapid progression to ARDS
  • Higher mortality (30–50% vs 10–20% HIV)
  • Risk factors: Steroids >20mg pred ×4 weeks, chemo, transplant, biologics, rheumatology
  • CXR may be normal early (but HRCT shows GGO)

Examination

  • Tachypnoea, tachycardia
  • Bibasal fine crackles (often late)
  • Cyanosis (severe hypoxaemia)
  • Normal or reduced breath sounds
  • No focal signs (unlike bacterial pneumonia)
  • Oral thrush (concurrent candidiasis clue in HIV)

5. Investigations

1. ABG / Oxygenation (KEY)

ParameterTypical PCP
PaO2Markedly reduced (<8 kPa / 60 mmHg)
A-a gradientMarkedly elevated (>35 mmHg)
pHRespiratory alkalosis (early), then respiratory acidosis (late)
SpO2Hypoxaemia out of proportion to CXR findings
FCPS/MRCP tip: Hypoxaemia disproportionate to CXR = classic PCP clue. A-a gradient >35 mmHg = adjunctive steroids indicated.

2. HRCT (Gold Standard Imaging)

FindingDescription
Bilateral perihilar ground-glass opacityClassic — "bat's wings" but perihilar, sparing apices and bases
Upper lobe sparingEarly disease often spares apices
CystsThin-walled, multiple (chronic/infected), pneumatoceles
PneumothoraxIncreased risk (cyst rupture), especially non-HIV
No consolidationUnless bacterial co-infection
No significant lymphadenopathyUnless lymphoma co-infection
Normal in 10–15%Especially early/non-HIV

3. Microbiological Diagnosis (Definitive)

SampleMethodSensitivity
Induced sputum (hypertonic saline)PCR (real-time)80–90% (HIV), 50–70% (non-HIV)
GMS stain (Gomori methenamine silver)60–80%
Immunofluorescence (DFA)70–90%
Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL)PCR>95% (gold standard)
GMS / DFA / Toluidine blue O90–95%
Serum β-D-glucanFungitell assay95% sensitivity, 80% specificity (supportive)
FCPS/MRCP tip: Induced sputum PCR = first-line non-invasive. BAL PCR = gold standard if sputum negative. β-D-glucan = supportive, not diagnostic (false + in other fungi, bacteraemia, haemodialysis).

4. HIV / Immunological Workup

  • HIV test (4th gen Ag/Ab) if unknown status
  • CD4 count, viral load (if HIV+)
  • Immunoglobulins, lymphocyte subsets (if non-HIV immunosuppressed)
  • Drug history (steroids, chemo, biologics, transplant drugs)

5. Exclusion of Co-infections

  • Sputum culture (bacterial, AFB, fungal)
  • Blood cultures (×2)
  • Legionella / Pneumococcal urinary antigen
  • Viral PCR panel (influenza, RSV, COVID, CMV)
  • CMV PCR (if transplant/immunosuppressed)

6. Interpretation Frameworks

1. Diagnostic Probability Algorithm

Immunocompromised host + subacute dyspnoea + fever + dry cough
    ↓
ABG: Marked hypoxaemia, ↑ A-a gradient
HRCT: Bilateral perihilar GGO (classic)
    ↓
Induced sputum PCR (first-line)
    ↓
Positive → PCP CONFIRMED
Negative → BAL PCR (gold standard)
    ↓
β-D-glucan positive (supportive)

2. Adjunctive Steroid Indication (Critical)

ParameterThresholdAction
PaO2<9.3 kPa (70 mmHg) on room airGIVE STEROIDS
A-a gradient>35 mmHg (4.7 kPa)GIVE STEROIDS
SpO2<92% on room airConsider steroids
Evidence: Steroids reduce mortality and intubation in moderate-severe PCP (PaO2 <70 mmHg). Start WITHIN 72h of antibiotics.

3. Severity Assessment

SeverityCriteriaManagement
MildPaO2 ≥9.3 kPa, ambulatoryOral cotrimoxazole, outpatient possible
ModeratePaO2 <9.3 kPa, not ventilatedIV cotrimoxazole + adjunctive steroids, admit
SevereMechanical ventilation / ICUIV cotrimoxazole + steroids, ICU

7. Diagnosis

Confirmed: Positive PCR/GMS/DFA on induced sputum OR BAL

Probable: Clinical + HRCT + β-D-glucan + exclusion of alternatives (no microbiological confirmation)

Clinical diagnosis: In resource-limited settings, treat empirically if high probability

8. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDifferentiating Features
Bacterial pneumoniaAcute, productive cough, focal consolidation, neutrophilia, responds to antibiotics
TBSubacute/chronic, night sweats, weight loss, upper lobe, cavitation, AFB+
Viral pneumonia (influenza, COVID, CMV)Viral PCR positive, variable CXR, often + fever/myalgia
Fungal (Aspergillus, Cryptococcus)Immunocompromised, nodules/cavities, galactomannan/antigen +ve
PCP-IRISWorsening 2–8 weeks post-ART, paradoxical, neutrophilic BAL
PJP drug reactionRash, fever, eosinophilia, temporal with cotrimoxazole

9. Management

1. First-Line Treatment: Cotrimoxazole (TMP-SMX)

RouteDoseDuration
IV (moderate-severe)TMP 15–20 mg/kg/day + SMX 75–100 mg/kg/day in 3–4 divided doses21 days
PO (mild, step-down)TMP 15–20 mg/kg/day + SMX 75–100 mg/kg/day (2–3 divided doses)21 days

Renal adjustment: Reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min

Folinic acid (10–25 mg/day) — reduces haematological toxicity (optional, not standard in all guidelines)

2. Adjunctive Corticosteroids (CRITICAL for Moderate-Severe)

IndicationRegimen
PaO2 <9.3 kPa (70 mmHg) OR A-a gradient >35 mmHgPrednisolone 40 mg BD ×5 days → 40 mg OD ×5 days → 20 mg OD ×11 days (21 days total)
OR Methylprednisolone IV 75% of oral dose if NPO

Timing: Start within 72h of cotrimoxazole (ideally simultaneously)

Mechanism: Reduces inflammation-mediated lung injury

3. Alternative Regimens (Cotrimoxazole Intolerance/Allergy)

RegimenDoseDurationNotes
Dapsone 100 mg/day + TMP 15 mg/kg/dayPO21 daysAlternative for mild-moderate sulfa allergy
Clindamycin 600 mg IV/PO q6h + Primaquine 30 mg/dayPO/IV21 daysG6PD test before primaquine
Atovaquone 750 mg BDPO (with food)21 daysMild-moderate only, expensive, ↓ absorption
Pentamidine 4 mg/kg IV dailyIV21 daysNephrotoxic, pancreatic toxicity, hypotension, hypoglycaemia — last resort

4. Prophylaxis (Primary & Secondary)

IndicationAgentDose
HIV: CD4 <200 or oropharyngeal candidiasis or AIDS-defining illnessCotrimoxazole 960 mg daily (or 480 mg daily)Lifelong until CD4 >200 ×3–6 months on ART
HIV: Cotrimoxazole allergyDapsone 50 mg daily (or 100 mg 3x/week)Same
Atovaquone 1500 mg daily
Aerosolised pentamidine 300 mg monthlyNebulised
Non-HIV immunosuppression (steroids >20mg ×4w, chemo, transplant, biologics)Cotrimoxazole 960 mg 3x/week (or daily)Duration of immunosuppression
Post-transplant (solid organ/HSCT)Cotrimoxazole 960 mg daily6–12 months (lung/HSCT longer)

5. Discontinuation of Prophylaxis (HIV)

CriteriaAction
CD4 >200 for ≥3–6 months on effective ART (viral load suppressed)STOP prophylaxis
CD4 100–200 with viral suppressionConsider stopping (case-by-case)
Treatment failure / viral reboundRESTART prophylaxis

▸ 17.5.14 Post Obstructive Pneumonia
[!important]
Post-obstructive pneumonia is recurrent or non-resolving pneumonia distal to a bronchial obstruction, classically caused by lung cancer (especially squamous). It is a paraneoplastic clue in smokers. Always exclude endobronchial lesion in non-resolving/recurrent pneumonia, particularly in smokers.

Related: [[Pneumonia]], [[Lung Cancer]], [[Aspiration pneumonia]], [[ABG Interpretation]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: Recurrent pneumonia in the same lobe = obstruction until proven otherwise → bronchoscopy. Most common cause: lung cancer (squamous most likely, also small cell). Other causes: foreign body (especially children), post-TB stricture, mucoid impaction, extrinsic compression.

1. Definition

Post-obstructive pneumonia = pneumonia occurring distal to a partial or complete airway obstruction, leading to impaired drainage, mucus stasis, and recurrent or non-resolving infection.

2. Aetiology

Endobronchial obstruction

CauseNotes
Lung cancerSquamous (most common), small cell, others
Carcinoid tumourYoung, non-smokers
Benign tumourPapilloma, lipoma, hamartoma
Foreign bodyChildren, elderly, neurological disease
Mucus plugAsthma, ABPA, post-op
Blood clotTrauma, bronchiectasis
TB stricturePost-TB
Post-intubation stenosisTracheal stenosis

Extrinsic compression

  • Lymphoma
  • Lymph nodes (sarcoid, TB, malignancy)
  • Goitre
  • Aortic aneurysm
  • Mediastinal mass

Functional

  • Bronchomalacia
  • Dynamic airway collapse
  • Vocal cord dysfunction

3. Pathophysiology

Sequence

  1. Airway obstruction (partial or complete)
  2. ↓Mucociliary clearance
  3. Mucus stasis + bacterial colonisation
  4. Distal atelectasis
  5. Post-obstructive pneumonia
  6. Abscess formation (incomplete drainage)
  7. Recurrent infections

4. Clinical Features

Symptoms

  • Recurrent pneumonia in same lobe (key clue)
  • Non-resolving pneumonia (>6 weeks despite antibiotics)
  • Cough (productive, may be purulent)
  • Haemoptysis (especially with malignancy)
  • Fever, dyspnoea, weight loss
  • Recurrent infection despite appropriate Rx

Signs

  • Fever, tachypnoea, tachycardia
  • Consolidation signs (crackles, dullness)
  • Focal wheeze (fixed, monophonic — suggests obstruction)
  • ↓Breath sounds (collapse)
  • Clubbing (malignancy, chronic)
  • Cachexia (malignancy)

5. Diagnosis

Investigations

TestFinding
CXRPneumonia in same lobe, ± mass, atelectasis, hilar enlargement
CT chestMass, obstruction, distal consolidation, lymph nodes
BronchoscopyGold standard — visualise obstruction, biopsy, brushings, BAL
SputumCytology (malignant cells), AFB, culture
CT-guided biopsyFor peripheral lesions
EBUSMediastinal staging

Key features suggesting obstruction

  • Persistent or recurrent consolidation in same lobe
  • Volume loss (atelectasis) distal
  • Focal wheeze not clearing with treatment
  • Haemoptysis
  • Weight loss, smoking history

6. Management

Treat pneumonia (initial)

  • Empirical antibiotics (cover typical +/− anaerobes)
  • Bronchoscopy for clearance (remove secretions/foreign body)
  • Positioning (gravity drainage)

Definitive treatment

  • Treat underlying cause:
  • Lung cancer: surgery (if resectable), chemo, radiotherapy, immunotherapy, targeted therapy
  • Foreign body: bronchoscopic removal
  • Stricture: dilatation, stent
  • Mucus plug: bronchoscopic removal, mucolytics

Specific to lung cancer

  • Staging (TNM 8th)
  • MDT discussion
  • Resection if operable (early stage)
  • Chemo/radiotherapy if inoperable
  • Palliative care if advanced

7. Red flags for lung cancer

  • Smoker >40 with new cough, haemoptysis, weight loss
  • Recurrent pneumonia same lobe
  • Non-resolving pneumonia (>6 weeks)
  • New lobar collapse
  • Pleural effusion (malignant)
  • Lymphadenopathy
  • Smoking + obstructive features + any of above = high suspicion

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
DefinitionPneumonia distal to obstruction
Most common causeLung cancer (squamous)
Key clueRecurrent pneumonia in same lobe
InvestigationCT chest + bronchoscopy
Foreign bodyCommon in children, elderly
TreatmentTreat cause + antibiotics
BronchoscopyDiagnostic + therapeutic (clearance, biopsy, stent)
SquamousMost common in central/endobronchial

9. MCQs (10)

  1. Most common cause of post-obstructive pneumonia in adults:

A. Foreign body

B. Lung cancer (squamous)

C. Mucus plug

D. Stricture

E. None

Answer: B — Lung cancer.

  1. Recurrent pneumonia in the same lobe should prompt:

A. Antibiotics

B. Bronchoscopy to exclude obstruction (e.g. lung cancer)

C. Physiotherapy

D. Steroids

E. None

Answer: B — Bronchoscopy.

  1. The most common lung cancer type causing post-obstructive pneumonia:

A. Adenocarcinoma

B. Squamous cell carcinoma (central, endobronchial)

C. Small cell

D. Large cell

E. None

Answer: B — Squamous.

  1. The best initial investigation for suspected post-obstructive pneumonia:

A. CXR

B. CT chest + bronchoscopy

C. Spirogram

D. ABG

E. None

Answer: B — CT + bronchoscopy.

  1. Non-resolving pneumonia despite appropriate antibiotics after how many weeks should prompt workup for obstruction:

A. 1

B. 3

C. 6

D. 12

E. None

Answer: C — 6 weeks.

  1. A 65-year-old smoker with right middle lobe consolidation that doesn't clear. Most likely:

A. Pneumonia only

B. Endobronchial obstruction (lung cancer)

C. TB

D. Asthma

E. None

Answer: B — Endobronchial lesion.

  1. A child with sudden wheeze + post-obstructive pneumonia. Most likely:

A. Asthma

B. Foreign body aspiration

C. Lung cancer

D. TB

E. None

Answer: B — Foreign body.

  1. The mechanism of post-obstructive pneumonia is:

A. Direct infection

B. Impaired mucus clearance + stasis + distal atelectasis

C. Allergy

D. None

E. Unknown

Answer: B — Mucus stasis.

  1. Most useful bronchoscopy in post-obstructive pneumonia:

A. Only diagnostic

B. Both diagnostic (biopsy) and therapeutic (clearance, stent)

C. Only therapeutic

D. None

E. Diagnostic only

Answer: B — Both.

  1. A 70-year-old with post-obstructive pneumonia from squamous cancer, resectable. Best:

A. Palliative

B. Surgical resection (lobectomy)

C. Chemo only

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — Surgery.

10. SBA Questions (10)

  1. Squamous cell carcinoma location is typically:

A. Peripheral

B. Central (endobronchial)

C. Diffuse

D. Apical

E. None

Answer: B — Central.

  1. A patient with non-resolving pneumonia 8 weeks after antibiotics. Next:

A. Repeat antibiotics

B. CT chest +/− bronchoscopy

C. Wait

D. Discharge

E. None

Answer: B — Workup.

  1. The most common cause of post-obstructive pneumonia in a child is:

A. Malignancy

B. Foreign body

C. Mucus

D. Stricture

E. None

Answer: B — Foreign body.

  1. Bronchoscopy in post-obstructive pneumonia serves as:

A. Diagnostic only

B. Both diagnostic and therapeutic

C. Not useful

D. Surgery

E. None

Answer: B — Both.

  1. Squamous cell carcinoma is associated with:

A. Smoking (strongest)

B. Never smoking

C. Asbestos

D. None

E. Allergy

Answer: A — Smoking.

  1. The first symptom of post-obstructive pneumonia in lung cancer is often:

A. Haemoptysis

B. Recurrent infection in same lobe

C. Weight loss

D. None

E. Pain

Answer: B — Recurrent infection.

  1. Carcinoid tumour is typically:

A. Young, non-smoker, central, endobronchial

B. Smoker

C. Old

D. None

E. Peripheral

Answer: A — Young non-smoker.

  1. The gold standard for diagnosis of post-obstructive pneumonia due to lung cancer is:

A. CXR

B. Bronchoscopy with biopsy

C. Sputum

D. CT

E. None

Answer: B — Bronchoscopy with biopsy.

  1. The most appropriate first-line investigation for recurrent pneumonia in a smoker is:

A. Spirogram

B. CT chest + bronchoscopy

C. Sputum

D. ABG

E. None

Answer: B — CT + bronchoscopy.

  1. Squamous cell carcinoma of the lung is associated with:

A. EGFR mutation

B. Smoking (cavitating, central)

C. ALK rearrangement

D. None

E. Asbestos only

Answer: B — Smoking.

11. Flashcards

  • Q: Most common cause of post-obstructive pneumonia?

A: Lung cancer (squamous).

  • Q: Recurrent pneumonia in same lobe?

A: Bronchoscopy (excludes obstruction).

  • Q: Most common lung cancer type?

A: Squamous (central, endobronchial, cavitating).

  • Q: Non-resolving pneumonia definition?

A: >6 weeks despite antibiotics.

  • Q: Foreign body in child?

A: Most common cause.

  • Q: Bronchoscopy role?

A: Diagnostic + therapeutic.

  • Q: Red flag for lung cancer?

A: Smoker with recurrent/non-resolving pneumonia, haemoptysis, weight loss.

  • Q: Carcinoid tumour typical patient?

A: Young, non-smoker.

  • Q: Squamous location?

A: Central, endobronchial.

  • Q: Best investigation?

A: CT + bronchoscopy.

12. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. C 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

SBAs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. A 6. B 7. A 8. B 9. B 10. B

13. Summary

Post-obstructive pneumonia = pneumonia distal to airway obstruction. Most common cause: lung cancer (squamous). Recurrent pneumonia in same lobe = bronchoscopy. Treat cause + antibiotics. Foreign body in children. Non-resolving >6 weeks → workup.

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 17.5.15 Pulmonary Aspergillosis Syndromes
[!important]
Aspergillus causes a spectrum of pulmonary disease: ABPA (hypersensitivity in asthma/CF), aspergilloma (mycetoma in pre-existing cavity), invasive aspergillosis (immunocompromised), chronic necrotising aspergillosis (semi-invasive), and IgE-mediated asthma. Diagnosis and treatment differ markedly by syndrome.

Related: [[ABPA and bronchiectasis]], [[Opportunistic pulmonary infections]], [[Asthma]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: ABPA = asthma + ↑IgE + central bronchiectasis + HAM on CT → prednisolone + itraconazole. Aspergilloma = fungal ball in old TB cavity (air crescent sign) → surgery or BAE for haemoptysis. Invasive aspergillosis = neutropenic + halo sign → voriconazole.

1. Spectrum of Aspergillus Disease

SyndromeHostPathologyTreatment
ABPAAsthma, CFHypersensitivity (Type I/III)Steroids + itraconazole
AspergillomaPre-existing cavityFungal ballObservation, BAE, surgery
Invasive aspergillosisNeutropenicTissue invasionVoriconazole
Chronic necrotising (semi-invasive)Mild immunocompromiseSlow invasionAntifungal ± surgery
IgE-mediated asthmaAsthmaAllergyAvoid exposure + asthma Rx

2. ABPA (Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis)

Definition

  • Hypersensitivity reaction to Aspergillus fumigatus in asthma (1–2%) and CF (7–15%)
  • Non-invasive; recurrent exacerbations

Diagnosis (ISHAM criteria)

Essential (both):

  • Asthma (or CF)
  • ↑Total serum IgE >1000 IU/mL

Other (≥2 of 6):

  1. Positive immediate skin-prick to A. fumigatus
  2. ↑Specific IgE to *A. fumigatus**
  3. ↑Serum precipitins (IgG to A. fumigatus)
  4. Peripheral blood eosinophilia >500/µL
  5. Central (proximal) bronchiectasis on HRCT
  6. High-attenuation mucus (HAM) on HRCT (pathognomonic)

Treatment

  • Prednisolone 0.5–0.75 mg/kg/day × 2 weeks → taper
  • Itraconazole 200 mg BD as steroid-sparing
  • Omalizumab (anti-IgE) in refractory
  • Monitor IgE, eosinophils, CXR

3. Aspergilloma (Mycetoma)

Definition

  • Fungal ball (mass of hyphae + mucus + debris) in pre-existing lung cavity (TB, sarcoidosis, emphysema, lung cyst)
  • Non-invasive but can cause massive haemoptysis

Diagnosis

  • CXR: mobile intracavitary mass with air crescent sign
  • CT chest: intracavitary mass + air crescent
  • Aspergillus precipitins (positive in 70%)
  • Aspergillus IgG ↑

Treatment

  • Asymptomatic: observation
  • Haemoptysis:
  • Bronchial artery embolisation (BAE) for acute
  • Surgical resection for isolated lesion + recurrent/persistent
  • Antifungal (itraconazole/voriconazole) — limited role
  • Intracavitary amphotericin (rare)

4. Invasive Aspergillosis

Host

  • Severe neutropenia (<500, especially <100)
  • HSCT, SOT (lung, liver)
  • High-dose steroids
  • Advanced AIDS (CD4 <100)

Diagnosis (EORTC/MSG criteria — proven/probable/possible)

  • Host (neutropenia, transplant, steroids)
  • Clinical (fever, symptoms, imaging)
  • Mycological (Aspergillus culture from sputum/BAL, galactomannan, PCR)

HRCT findings

  • Halo sign: nodule with surrounding GGO (early; ~5 days)
  • Air crescent sign: crescent of air around nodule (late; ~2 weeks)
  • Cavitation (late)

Treatment

  • Voriconazole (first-line) 6 mg/kg IV ×2 → 4 mg/kg IV BD
  • Isavuconazole (alternative)
  • Liposomal amphotericin B (alternative/rescue)
  • Posaconazole (prophylaxis in HSCT, salvage)
  • Anidulafungin (echinocandin, salvage)
  • Duration: 6–12 weeks
  • Surgery for localised invasive disease

5. Chronic Necrotising (Semi-Invasive) Aspergillosis

Host

  • Mild immunocompromise (COPD, diabetes, alcohol, low-dose steroids)
  • Slow progression

Features

  • Chronic cough, weight loss, fatigue
  • Cavitary lesion (often upper lobe)
  • Fungal hyphae in tissue

Treatment

  • Antifungal (voriconazole or itraconazole) 3–6 months
  • Surgery if localised

6. IgE-Mediated Asthma (Aspergillus sensitisation)

  • Positive skin-prick or specific IgE to Aspergillus in asthma without meeting ABPA criteria
  • Treat as asthma + avoid exposure

7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
ABPAAsthma/CF + ↑IgE + central bronchiectasis + HAM
ABPA RxPrednisolone + itraconazole
AspergillomaFungal ball in cavity; air crescent sign
Aspergilloma RxBAE (acute) or surgery (recurrent)
Invasive AspergillusNeutropenic + halo sign
Invasive RxVoriconazole (1st line)
Air crescent signLate invasive or aspergilloma
Halo signEarly invasive (GGO around nodule)
GalactomannanDiagnostic marker

8. MCQs (10)

  1. ABPA is most commonly associated with:

A. COPD

B. Asthma and CF

C. Lung cancer

D. None

E. TB

Answer: B — Asthma and CF.

  1. First-line treatment for ABPA:

A. Prednisolone + itraconazole

B. Voriconazole alone

C. Surgery

D. Theophylline

E. None

Answer: A — Steroids + itraconazole.

  1. The pathognomonic HRCT finding in ABPA:

A. Cavitation

B. High-attenuation mucus (HAM)

C. Pleural effusion

D. Nodules

E. None

Answer: B — HAM.

  1. Air crescent sign in a TB cavity suggests:

A. Active TB

B. Aspergilloma

C. Lung cancer

D. None

E. Pneumonia

Answer: B — Aspergilloma.

  1. First-line treatment for massive haemoptysis in aspergilloma:

A. Antifungal

B. Bronchial artery embolisation (BAE)

C. Steroids

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — BAE.

  1. First-line treatment for invasive aspergillosis:

A. Voriconazole

B. Fluconazole

C. Acyclovir

D. TMP-SMX

E. None

Answer: A — Voriconazole.

  1. The halo sign on HRCT is seen in:

A. Aspergilloma

B. Invasive aspergillosis (early)

C. TB

D. Sarcoid

E. None

Answer: B — Early invasive.

  1. ABPA in CF: prevalence:

A. 1%

B. 5%

C. 7–15%

D. 50%

E. None

Answer: C — 7–15%.

  1. Serum galactomannan is a marker of:

A. ABPA

B. Invasive aspergillosis

C. TB

D. Sarcoidosis

E. None

Answer: B — Invasive.

  1. The minimum IgE for ABPA diagnosis (ISHAM):

A. >100

B. >1000 IU/mL

C. >500

D. >100

E. None

Answer: B — >1000.

9. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 30-year-old with asthma has ↑IgE to 3000, central bronchiectasis, HAM. Diagnosis:

A. Asthma

B. ABPA

C. CF

D. None

E. Sarcoid

Answer: B — ABPA.

  1. The best initial treatment for ABPA exacerbation:

A. Prednisolone (0.5 mg/kg/day) + itraconazole

B. Voriconazole

C. Surgery

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: A — Steroids + itraconazole.

  1. A 60-year-old with old TB has new mass in cavity with air crescent. Best management:

A. Antifungal

B. Observe if asymptomatic; BAE if haemoptysis

C. Steroids

D. Surgery

E. None

Answer: B — Observe/BAE.

  1. Invasive aspergillosis is most common in:

A. Asthma

B. Neutropenic (HSCT, leukaemia)

C. COPD

D. None

E. CF

Answer: B — Neutropenic.

  1. The first sign of invasive aspergillosis on CT:

A. Cavitation

B. Halo sign (GGO around nodule)

C. Pleural effusion

D. None

E. Air crescent

Answer: B — Halo.

  1. Treatment duration for invasive aspergillosis:

A. 1 week

B. 6–12 weeks

C. 3 days

D. 1 year

E. None

Answer: B — 6–12 weeks.

  1. ABPA in CF: first-line antifungal:

A. Voriconazole

B. Itraconazole

C. Posaconazole

D. None

E. Ampho B

Answer: B — Itraconazole.

  1. A neutropenic patient has fever + new cavitating lesion + halo sign. Treatment:

A. Wait

B. Voriconazole (empiric)

C. Steroids

D. Discharge

E. None

Answer: B — Empiric voriconazole.

  1. The most useful serum marker for invasive aspergillosis:

A. CRP

B. Galactomannan

C. ESR

D. None

E. LDH

Answer: B — Galactomannan.

  1. Aspergilloma surgical resection is indicated for:

A. Always

B. Recurrent/persistent haemoptysis + isolated lesion + good surgical candidate

C. Never

D. All

E. None

Answer: B — Selected cases.

10. Flashcards

  • Q: ABPA Rx?

A: Prednisolone + itraconazole.

  • Q: Aspergilloma Rx (acute haemoptysis)?

A: BAE.

  • Q: Invasive Aspergillus Rx?

A: Voriconazole.

  • Q: ABPA pathognomonic CT?

A: HAM (high-attenuation mucus).

  • Q: Air crescent sign?

A: Aspergilloma or late invasive.

  • Q: Halo sign?

A: Early invasive aspergillosis (GGO around nodule).

  • Q: ABPA IgE threshold?

A: >1000 IU/mL.

  • Q: Galactomannan?

A: Marker of invasive aspergillosis.

  • Q: Aspergilloma cavity origin?

A: Old TB (most common).

  • Q: Treatment duration invasive?

A: 6–12 weeks.

11. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B 2. A 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. A 7. B 8. C 9. B 10. B

SBAs

  1. B 2. A 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

12. Summary

Aspergillus causes 5 main syndromes: ABPA (asthma + ↑IgE + HAM → steroids + itraconazole), aspergilloma (fungal ball → BAE/surgery), invasive (neutropenic + halo → voriconazole), chronic necrotising, IgE asthma. Diagnosis: imaging (CT, air crescent, halo, HAM), galactomannan, precipitins, skin-prick.
